revision.comment: [[WP:AES|←]]新しいページ: 「'''禁裏小番'''（きんりこばん）とは、[[中世]]から[[近世]]にかけて、[[公家]]が毎日交替（[[番]]）で[[天皇]]のいる[[禁裏]][[御所]]に伺候・[[宿直]]する制度。 [[摂関家]]を除いた公家が元服直後の15歳から60歳まで勤務していた<ref name=日本史大事典>母利『日本史大事典』</ref><ref name=日本歴史大事典>本田『日本歴史大事典』</ref>。ただし、…」
revision.text:
'''禁裏小番'''（きんりこばん）とは、[[中世]]から[[近世]]にかけて、[[公家]]が毎日交替（[[番]]）で[[天皇]]のいる[[禁裏]][[御所]]に伺候・[[宿直]]する制度。
[[摂関家]]を除いた公家が元服直後の15歳から60歳まで勤務していた<ref name=日本史大事典>母利『日本史大事典』</ref><ref name=日本歴史大事典>本田『日本歴史大事典』</ref>。ただし、[[戦国時代 (日本)|戦国時代]]には公家の地方下向や断絶によって人手不足になると、[[元服]]前の者も番を務めていた事例があり、[[江戸時代]]に入って[[後水尾天皇]]によって小番勤務は15歳以上と定められ、また[[新家]]設立によって人手不足が解消された後も非公式な[[御児]]の形で別途登用されて禁裏内部の雑用を務めた<ref>林大樹「近世公家社会における〈御児〉について」『人文』第16号（2018年）/改題所収:林「近世朝廷の御児について」『天皇近臣と近世の朝廷』（吉川弘文館、2021年） ISBN 978-4-642-04333-5 2021年、P93-95.</ref>。また、父子（当主と後継者）が同時に出仕している場合には、子には方領（給与代わりの土地）が与えられた<ref name=日本歴史大事典/>。また、[[太上天皇]]や[[皇太子]]に伺候する番もおり、それぞれ「院参衆」「東宮小番」と呼ばれていた<ref name=hayashi>林大樹「序章 近世天皇・朝廷研究の成果と本書の目的」『天皇近臣と近世の朝廷』（吉川弘文館、2021年） 2021年、P8-9.</ref>。
時代によって異なるが、5人から7人の輪番制で5番・6番・10番などに編成していたが、状況によっては全体の番数を減らしたり、臨時の加番を命じることで1つの番あたりの人数を増やして対応した<ref name=日本史大事典/>。各組には番頭を置き、小番奉行（武家伝奏による兼務が多い）によって管理されていた<ref name=日本史大事典/>。
「禁裏小番」という呼称が成立したのは[[室町時代]]とされている（小番の仕組みそのものの成立はそれ以前と考えられる）が、この時代には経済的困窮から番の職務を果たさない者も多かった。そのため、[[永享]]2年（[[1430年]]）に[[室町幕府]]の[[征夷大将軍|将軍]][[足利義教]]の介入によって7か条からなる改革案が定めて、常に番のうちの誰かが御所に詰めている状態にすることとして、これに違反した者には所領没収などの措置が取られた<ref name=国史大辞典>田沼『国史大辞典』</ref>。[[江戸幕府]]も『[[禁中並公家諸法度]]』において「昼夜之御番」という表現で禁裏小番が朝廷に奉仕する公家の任務として、[[家業]]・学問と共に重視されるべきものと位置づけられていた<ref name=日本史大事典/>。
[[文明 (日本)|文明]]8年（[[1476年]]）、小番の中から近臣によって構成される内々（うちうち）番とそれ以外の外様番に分けられるようになり<ref name=日本史大事典/>、内々番と外様番では詰める番所なども異なっていた<ref name=日本歴史大事典/>。本来、内々と外様は時の天皇との親疎によって分けられていたが、時代が下るにつれて家ごとに固定化されて「内々」と「外様」は一種の公家の[[家格]]となった（完全に固定化された訳では無かったが）<ref name=日本歴史大事典/><ref name=hayashi/>。
[[寛文]]3年（[[1661年]]）、後水尾法皇は新たに即位する幼帝[[霊元天皇]]のために内々・外様の中から近臣となるべき者21名を抽出して新たな番を編成した。当初、新たな番は「奥之番」と呼ばれ、本来所属する内々番・外様番との掛け持ちであったが、寛文11年（[[1671年]]）に奥之番は本番所（内々・外様のそれぞれに設けられた詰所）への出仕が免除された。奥之番になった者は依然として内々・外様の名簿には名前が残されていたものの、新たな番に所属している間は事実上のそちらへの専任となり、第3の小番である近習番（もしくは近習小番）が成立した<ref>林大樹「近世の近習小番について」『論集きんせい』第40号（2018年）/所収:林『天皇近臣と近世の朝廷』（吉川弘文館、2021年） 2021年、P140-146.</ref>。
[[慶応]]4年（[[明治]]元年・[[1868年]]）閏4月、宮中三番と呼ばれていた内々・外様・近習の区別が廃止されて、「内番衆」と呼ばれるようになった<ref name=日本史大事典/><ref name=日本歴史大事典/>。
== 脚注 == <references/> == 参考文献 == *田沼睦「禁裏小番」（『国史大辞典 4』（吉川弘文館、1984年） ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3） *母利美和「禁裏小番」（『日本史大事典 2』（平凡社、1993年） ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4） *本田慧子「禁裏小番」（『日本歴史大事典 1』（小学館、2001年） ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6
{{DEFAULTSORT:きんりこはん}} [[Category:令外官]]

'''禁裏小番'''（きんりこばん）とは、[[中世]]から[[近世]]にかけて、[[公家]]が毎日交替（[[番]]）で[[天皇]]のいる[[禁裏]][[御所]]に伺候・[[宿直]]する制度。
[[摂関家]]を除いた公家が元服直後の15歳から60歳まで勤務していた（ただし、職務の多忙や長年の功労から天皇の命により免除される者もいる）<ref name=日本史大事典>母利『日本史大事典』</ref><ref name=日本歴史大事典>本田『日本歴史大事典』</ref>。ただし、[[戦国時代 (日本)|戦国時代]]には公家の地方下向や断絶によって人手不足になると、[[元服]]前の者も番を務めていた事例があり、[[江戸時代]]に入って[[後水尾天皇]]によって小番勤務は15歳以上と定められ、また[[新家]]設立によって人手不足が解消された後も非公式な[[御児]]の形で別途登用されて禁裏内部の雑用を務めた<ref>林大樹「近世公家社会における〈御児〉について」『人文』第16号（2018年）/改題所収:林「近世朝廷の御児について」『天皇近臣と近世の朝廷』（吉川弘文館、2021年） ISBN 978-4-642-04333-5 2021年、P93-95.</ref>。また、父子（当主と後継者）が同時に出仕している場合には、子には方領（給与代わりの土地）が与えられた<ref name=日本歴史大事典/>。また、[[太上天皇]]や[[皇太子]]に伺候する番もおり、それぞれ「院参衆」「東宮小番」と呼ばれていた<ref name=hayashi>林大樹「序章 近世天皇・朝廷研究の成果と本書の目的」『天皇近臣と近世の朝廷』（吉川弘文館、2021年） 2021年、P8-9.</ref>。
時代によって異なるが、5人から7人の輪番制で5番・6番・10番などに編成していたが、状況によっては全体の番数を減らしたり、臨時の加番を命じることで1つの番あたりの人数を増やして対応した<ref name=日本史大事典/>。各組には番頭を置き、小番奉行（武家伝奏による兼務が多い）によって管理されていた<ref name=日本史大事典/>。
「禁裏小番」という呼称が成立したのは[[室町時代]]とされている（小番の仕組みそのものの成立はそれ以前と考えられる）が、この時代には経済的困窮から番の職務を果たさない者も多かった。そのため、[[永享]]2年（[[1430年]]）に[[室町幕府]]の[[征夷大将軍|将軍]][[足利義教]]の介入によって7か条からなる改革案が定めて、常に番のうちの誰かが御所に詰めている状態にすることとして、これに違反した者には所領没収などの措置が取られた<ref name=国史大辞典>田沼『国史大辞典』</ref>。[[江戸幕府]]も『[[禁中並公家諸法度]]』において「昼夜之御番」という表現で禁裏小番が朝廷に奉仕する公家の任務として、[[家業]]・学問と共に重視されるべきものと位置づけられていた<ref name=日本史大事典/>。
[[文明 (日本)|文明]]8年（[[1476年]]）、小番の中から近臣によって構成される内々（うちうち）番とそれ以外の外様番に分けられるようになり<ref name=日本史大事典/>、内々番と外様番では詰める番所なども異なっていた<ref name=日本歴史大事典/>。本来、内々と外様は時の天皇との親疎によって分けられていたが、時代が下るにつれて家ごとに固定化されて「内々」と「外様」は一種の公家の[[家格]]となった（完全に固定化された訳では無かったが）<ref name=日本歴史大事典/><ref name=hayashi/>。
[[寛文]]3年（[[1661年]]）、後水尾法皇は新たに即位する幼帝[[霊元天皇]]のために内々・外様の中から近臣となるべき者21名を抽出して新たな番を編成した。当初、新たな番は「奥之番」と呼ばれ、本来所属する内々番・外様番との掛け持ちであったが、寛文11年（[[1671年]]）に奥之番は本番所（内々・外様のそれぞれに設けられた詰所）への出仕が免除された。奥之番になった者は依然として内々・外様の名簿には名前が残されていたものの、新たな番に所属している間は事実上のそちらへの専任となり、第3の小番である近習番（もしくは近習小番）が成立した<ref>林大樹「近世の近習小番について」『論集きんせい』第40号（2018年）/所収:林『天皇近臣と近世の朝廷』（吉川弘文館、2021年） 2021年、P140-146.</ref>。

時代によって異なるが、5人から7人の輪番制で5番・6番・10番などに編成していたが、状況によっては全体の番数を減らしたり、臨時の加番を命じることで1つの番あたりの人数を増やして対応した<ref name=日本史大事典/>。各組には番頭を置き、小番奉行（武家伝奏による兼務が多い）によって管理されていた<ref name=日本史大事典/>。
「禁裏小番」という呼称が成立したのは[[室町時代]]とされている（小番の仕組みそのものの成立はそれ以前と考えられる）が、この時代には経済的困窮から番の職務を果たさない者も多かった。そのため、[[永享]]2年（[[1430年]]）に[[室町幕府]]の[[征夷大将軍|将軍]][[足利義教]]の介入によって7か条からなる改革案が定めて、常に番のうちの誰かが御所に詰めている状態にすることとして、これに違反した者には所領没収などの措置が取られた<ref name=国史大辞典>田沼『国史大辞典』</ref>。[[江戸幕府]]も『[[禁中並公家諸法度]]』において「昼夜之御番」という表現で禁裏小番が朝廷に奉仕する公家の任務として、[[家業]]・学問と共に重視されるべきものと位置づけられていた<ref name=日本史大事典/>。
[[文明 (日本)|文明]]8年（[[1476年]]）、小番の中から近臣によって構成される内々（うちうち）番とそれ以外の外様番に分けられるようになり<ref name=日本史大事典/>、内々番と外様番では詰める番所なども異なっていた<ref name=日本歴史大事典/>。本来、内々と外様は時の天皇との親疎によって分けられていたが、時代が下るにつれて家ごとに固定化されて「内々」と「外様」は一種の公家の[[家格]]となった（完全に固定化された訳では無かったが）<ref name=日本歴史大事典/><ref name=hayashi/>。
[[寛文]]3年（[[1661年]]）、後水尾法皇は新たに即位する幼帝[[霊元天皇]]のために内々・外様の中から近臣となるべき者21名を抽出して新たな番を編成した。当初、新たな番は「奥之番」と呼ばれ、本来所属する内々番・外様番との掛け持ちであったが、寛文11年（[[1671年]]）に奥之番は本番所（内々・外様のそれぞれに設けられた詰所）への出仕が免除された。奥之番になった者は依然として内々・外様の名簿には名前が残されていたものの、新たな番に所属している間は事実上の専属となり、第3の小番である近習番（もしくは近習小番）が成立した<ref>林大樹「近世の近習小番について」『論集きんせい』第40号（2018年）/所収:林『天皇近臣と近世の朝廷』（吉川弘文館、2021年） 2021年、P140-146.</ref>。
[[慶応]]4年（[[明治]]元年・[[1868年]]）閏4月、宮中三番と呼ばれていた内々・外様・近習の区別が廃止されて、「内番衆」と呼ばれるようになった<ref name=日本史大事典/><ref name=日本歴史大事典/>。
== 脚注 == <references/> == 参考文献 == *田沼睦「禁裏小番」（『国史大辞典 4』（吉川弘文館、1984年） ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3） *母利美和「禁裏小番」（『日本史大事典 2』（平凡社、1993年） ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4） *本田慧子「禁裏小番」（『日本歴史大事典 1』（小学館、2001年） ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6
{{DEFAULTSORT:きんりこはん}} [[Category:令外官]]

{{ページ番号|date=2021年8月}} '''禁裏小番'''（きんりこばん）とは、[[中世]]から[[近世]]にかけて、[[公家]]が毎日交替（[[番]]）で[[天皇]]のいる[[禁裏]][[御所]]に伺候・[[宿直]]する制度。
[[摂関家]]を除いた公家が元服直後の15歳から60歳まで勤務していた（ただし、職務の多忙や長年の功労から天皇の命により免除される者もいる）<ref name=日本史大事典>母利『日本史大事典』</ref><ref name=日本歴史大事典>本田『日本歴史大事典』</ref>。ただし、[[戦国時代 (日本)|戦国時代]]には公家の地方下向や断絶によって人手不足になると、[[元服]]前の者も番を務めていた事例があり、[[江戸時代]]に入って[[後水尾天皇]]によって小番勤務は15歳以上と定められ、また[[新家]]設立によって人手不足が解消された後も非公式な[[御児]]の形で別途登用されて禁裏内部の雑用を務めた<ref>林大樹「近世公家社会における〈御児〉について」『人文』第16号（2018年）/改題所収:林「近世朝廷の御児について」『天皇近臣と近世の朝廷』（吉川弘文館、2021年） ISBN 978-4-642-04333-5 2021年、P93-95.</ref>。また、父子（当主と後継者）が同時に出仕している場合には、子には方領（給与代わりの土地）が与えられた<ref name=日本歴史大事典/>。また、[[太上天皇]]や[[皇太子]]に伺候する番もおり、それぞれ「院参衆」「東宮小番」と呼ばれていた<ref name=hayashi>林大樹「序章 近世天皇・朝廷研究の成果と本書の目的」『天皇近臣と近世の朝廷』（吉川弘文館、2021年） 2021年、P8-9.</ref>。
時代によって異なるが、5人から7人の輪番制で5番・6番・10番などに編成していたが、状況によっては全体の番数を減らしたり、臨時の加番を命じることで1つの番あたりの人数を増やして対応した<ref name=日本史大事典/>。各組には番頭を置き、小番奉行（武家伝奏による兼務が多い）によって管理されていた<ref name=日本史大事典/>。
「禁裏小番」という呼称が成立したのは[[室町時代]]とされている（小番の仕組みそのものの成立はそれ以前と考えられる）が、この時代には経済的困窮から番の職務を果たさない者も多かった。そのため、[[永享]]2年（[[1430年]]）に[[室町幕府]]の[[征夷大将軍|将軍]][[足利義教]]の介入によって7か条からなる改革案が定めて、常に番のうちの誰かが御所に詰めている状態にすることとして、これに違反した者には所領没収などの措置が取られた<ref name=国史大辞典>田沼『国史大辞典』</ref>。[[江戸幕府]]も『[[禁中並公家諸法度]]』において「昼夜之御番」という表現で禁裏小番が朝廷に奉仕する公家の任務として、[[家業]]・学問と共に重視されるべきものと位置づけられていた<ref name=日本史大事典/>。
[[文明 (日本)|文明]]8年（[[1476年]]）、小番の中から近臣によって構成される内々（うちうち）番とそれ以外の外様番に分けられるようになり<ref name=日本史大事典/>、内々番と外様番では詰める番所なども異なっていた<ref name=日本歴史大事典/>。本来、内々と外様は時の天皇との親疎によって分けられていたが、時代が下るにつれて家ごとに固定化されて「内々」と「外様」は一種の公家の[[家格]]となった（完全に固定化された訳では無かったが）<ref name=日本歴史大事典/><ref name=hayashi/>。
[[寛文]]3年（[[1661年]]）、後水尾法皇は新たに即位する幼帝[[霊元天皇]]のために内々・外様の中から近臣となるべき者21名を抽出して新たな番を編成した。当初、新たな番は「奥之番」と呼ばれ、本来所属する内々番・外様番との掛け持ちであったが、寛文11年（[[1671年]]）に奥之番は本番所（内々・外様のそれぞれに設けられた詰所）への出仕が免除された。奥之番になった者は依然として内々・外様の名簿には名前が残されていたものの、新たな番に所属している間は事実上の専属となり、第3の小番である近習番（もしくは近習小番）が成立した<ref>林大樹「近世の近習小番について」『論集きんせい』第40号（2018年）/所収:林『天皇近臣と近世の朝廷』（吉川弘文館、2021年） 2021年、P140-146.</ref>。
[[慶応]]4年（[[明治]]元年・[[1868年]]）閏4月、宮中三番と呼ばれていた内々・外様・近習の区別が廃止されて、「内番衆」と呼ばれるようになった<ref name=日本史大事典/><ref name=日本歴史大事典/>。
== 脚注 == <references/> == 参考文献 == *田沼睦「禁裏小番」『[[国史大辞典]] 4』（[[吉川弘文館]] 1984年） ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3 *母利美和「禁裏小番」『[[日本史大事典]] 2』（[[平凡社]] 1993年） ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4 *本田慧子「禁裏小番」『[[日本歴史大事典]] 1』（[[小学館]] 2001年） ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6
{{DEFAULTSORT:きんりこはん}} [[Category:令外官]]

{{ページ番号|date=2021年8月}} '''禁裏小番'''（きんりこばん）とは、[[中世]]から[[近世]]にかけて、[[公家]]が毎日交替（[[番]]）で[[天皇]]のいる[[禁裏]][[御所]]に伺候・[[宿直]]する制度。

revision.comment: 文亀二年を例に編成を追記
revision.text:
{{ページ番号|date=2021年8月}} '''禁裏小番'''（きんりこばん）とは、[[中世]]から[[近世]]にかけて、[[公家]]が毎日交替（[[番]]）で[[天皇]]のいる[[禁裏]][[御所]]に伺候・[[宿直]]する制度。
天皇と神鏡のある内侍所を警護した{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=160}}。
[[摂関家]]を除いた公家が元服直後の15歳から60歳まで勤務していた（ただし、職務の多忙や長年の功労から天皇の命により免除される者もいる）<ref name=日本史大事典>母利『日本史大事典』</ref><ref name=日本歴史大事典>本田『日本歴史大事典』</ref>。ただし、[[戦国時代 (日本)|戦国時代]]には公家の地方下向や断絶によって人手不足になると、[[元服]]前の者も番を務めていた事例があり、[[江戸時代]]に入って[[後水尾天皇]]によって小番勤務は15歳以上と定められ、また[[新家]]設立によって人手不足が解消された後も非公式な[[御児]]の形で別途登用されて禁裏内部の雑用を務めた<ref>林大樹「近世公家社会における〈御児〉について」『人文』第16号（2018年）/改題所収:林「近世朝廷の御児について」『天皇近臣と近世の朝廷』（吉川弘文館、2021年） ISBN 978-4-642-04333-5 2021年、P93-95.</ref>。また、父子（当主と後継者）が同時に出仕している場合には、子には方領（給与代わりの土地）が与えられた<ref name=日本歴史大事典/>。また、[[太上天皇]]や[[皇太子]]に伺候する番もおり、それぞれ「院参衆」「東宮小番」と呼ばれていた<ref name=hayashi>林大樹「序章 近世天皇・朝廷研究の成果と本書の目的」『天皇近臣と近世の朝廷』（吉川弘文館、2021年） 2021年、P8-9.</ref>。
時代によって異なるが、5人から7人の輪番制で5番・6番・10番などに編成していたが、状況によっては全体の番数を減らしたり、臨時の加番を命じることで1つの番あたりの人数を増やして対応した<ref name=日本史大事典/>。各組には番頭を置き、小番奉行（武家伝奏による兼務が多い）によって管理されていた<ref name=日本史大事典/>。
「禁裏小番」という呼称が成立したのは[[室町時代]]とされている（小番の仕組みそのものの成立はそれ以前と考えられる）が、この時代には経済的困窮から番の職務を果たさない者も多かった。そのため、[[永享]]2年（[[1430年]]）に[[室町幕府]]の[[征夷大将軍|将軍]][[足利義教]]の介入によって7か条からなる改革案が定めて、常に番のうちの誰かが御所に詰めている状態にすることとして、これに違反した者には所領没収などの措置が取られた<ref name=国史大辞典>田沼『国史大辞典』</ref>。[[江戸幕府]]も『[[禁中並公家諸法度]]』において「昼夜之御番」という表現で禁裏小番が朝廷に奉仕する公家の任務として、[[家業]]・学問と共に重視されるべきものと位置づけられていた<ref name=日本史大事典/>。
1443年（嘉吉3）9月に[[禁闕の変]]が起き、内裏に[[後南朝]]方が乱入した時は[[甘露寺親長]]、[[四辻季春]]の若手公家が太刀を抜いて敵方と戦い、[[後花園天皇]]を避難させた{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=13}}。
[[文明 (日本)|文明]]8年（[[1476年]]）、小番の中から近臣によって構成される内々（うちうち）番とそれ以外の外様番に分けられるようになり<ref name=日本史大事典/>、内々番と外様番では詰める番所なども異なっていた<ref name=日本歴史大事典/>。本来、内々と外様は時の天皇との親疎によって分けられていたが、時代が下るにつれて家ごとに固定化されて「内々」と「外様」は一種の公家の[[家格]]となった（完全に固定化された訳では無かったが）<ref name=日本歴史大事典/><ref name=hayashi/>。
[[寛文]]3年（[[1661年]]）、後水尾法皇は新たに即位する幼帝[[霊元天皇]]のために内々・外様の中から近臣となるべき者21名を抽出して新たな番を編成した。当初、新たな番は「奥之番」と呼ばれ、本来所属する内々番・外様番との掛け持ちであったが、寛文11年（[[1671年]]）に奥之番は本番所（内々・外様のそれぞれに設けられた詰所）への出仕が免除された。奥之番になった者は依然として内々・外様の名簿には名前が残されていたものの、新たな番に所属している間は事実上の専属となり、第3の小番である近習番（もしくは近習小番）が成立した<ref>林大樹「近世の近習小番について」『論集きんせい』第40号（2018年）/所収:林『天皇近臣と近世の朝廷』（吉川弘文館、2021年） 2021年、P140-146.</ref>。
[[慶応]]4年（[[明治]]元年・[[1868年]]）閏4月、宮中三番と呼ばれていた内々・外様・近習の区別が廃止されて、「内番衆」と呼ばれるようになった<ref name=日本史大事典/><ref name=日本歴史大事典/>。
== 編成 == 文亀二年（1502）、正月時の禁裏小番の編成は以下の通りであった{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=156-157}}。
近臣番
* 一番 松木宗綱 山科言国 広橋守光 * 二番 三条西実隆 勧修寺経郷 東坊城和長 * 三番 四辻季経 甘露寺元長 冷泉永宣 * 四番 正親町公兼 田向重治 五条為学 * 五番 綾小路俊量 白川忠富王 五辻富仲 * 六番 勧修寺政顕 鷲尾隆康
外様番
* 一番 中御門宣胤 橋本公夏 北畠政宗 * 二番 三条実香 園基富 清閑寺家幸 * 三番 町広光 中院通世 一条実治 * 四番 四辻実仲 二条実望 四条隆永 * 五番 武者小路縁光 姉小路基綱 四条隆秀 * 六番 冷泉政為 坊城俊名 世尊寺行季 * 七番 冷泉為広 高倉永康 柳原資定 * 八番 中山宣親 飛鳥井雅俊 一条公博 * 九番 小倉季種 持明院基春 日野高光 * 十番 高辻長直 大炊御門経名
近臣番は天皇に近い公家から選ばれ天盃が下された。外様番の中には家領経営のため、地方に下ったまま全く出仕しないものもいたという{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=158}}。
== 脚注 == <references/> == 参考文献 == *田沼睦「禁裏小番」『[[国史大辞典 (昭和時代)|国史大辞典]] 4』（[[吉川弘文館]] 1984年） ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3 *母利美和「禁裏小番」『[[日本史大事典]] 2』（[[平凡社]] 1993年） ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4 *本田慧子「禁裏小番」『[[日本歴史大事典]] 1』（[[小学館]] 2001年） ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6 * {{Citation|和書|author=中本真人|date=2016-7-27|title=宮廷の御神楽|publisher=新典社|ref={{SfnRef|中本真人|2016}} }} * {{Citation|和書|author=中本真人|date=2021-12-22|title=なぜ神楽は応仁の乱を乗り越えられたのか|publisher=新典社|ref={{SfnRef|中本真人|2021}} }}
{{DEFAULTSORT:きんりこはん}} [[Category:令外官]]

{{ページ番号|date=2021年8月}} '''禁裏小番'''（きんりこばん）とは、[[中世]]から[[近世]]にかけて、[[公家]]が毎日交替（[[番]]）で[[天皇]]のいる[[禁裏]][[御所]]に伺候・[[宿直]]する制度。
[[天皇]]と[[神鏡剣璽|神鏡]]のある[[内侍所]]を警護した{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=160}}。
[[摂関家]]を除いた公家が元服直後の15歳から60歳まで勤務していた（ただし、職務の多忙や長年の功労から天皇の命により免除される者もいる）<ref name=日本史大事典>母利『日本史大事典』</ref><ref name=日本歴史大事典>本田『日本歴史大事典』</ref>。ただし、[[戦国時代 (日本)|戦国時代]]には公家の地方下向や断絶によって人手不足になると、[[元服]]前の者も番を務めていた事例があり、[[江戸時代]]に入って[[後水尾天皇]]によって小番勤務は15歳以上と定められ、また[[新家]]設立によって人手不足が解消された後も非公式な[[御児]]の形で別途登用されて禁裏内部の雑用を務めた<ref>林大樹「近世公家社会における〈御児〉について」『人文』第16号（2018年）/改題所収:林「近世朝廷の御児について」『天皇近臣と近世の朝廷』（吉川弘文館、2021年） ISBN 978-4-642-04333-5 2021年、P93-95.</ref>。また、父子（当主と後継者）が同時に出仕している場合には、子には方領（給与代わりの土地）が与えられた<ref name=日本歴史大事典/>。また、[[太上天皇]]や[[皇太子]]に伺候する番もおり、それぞれ「院参衆」「東宮小番」と呼ばれていた<ref name=hayashi>林大樹「序章 近世天皇・朝廷研究の成果と本書の目的」『天皇近臣と近世の朝廷』（吉川弘文館、2021年） 2021年、P8-9.</ref>。
時代によって異なるが、5人から7人の輪番制で5番・6番・10番などに編成していたが、状況によっては全体の番数を減らしたり、臨時の加番を命じることで1つの番あたりの人数を増やして対応した<ref name=日本史大事典/>。各組には番頭を置き、小番奉行（武家伝奏による兼務が多い）によって管理されていた<ref name=日本史大事典/>。
「禁裏小番」という呼称が成立したのは[[室町時代]]とされている（小番の仕組みそのものの成立はそれ以前と考えられる）が、この時代には経済的困窮から番の職務を果たさない者も多かった。そのため、[[永享]]2年（[[1430年]]）に[[室町幕府]]の[[征夷大将軍|将軍]][[足利義教]]の介入によって7か条からなる改革案が定めて、常に番のうちの誰かが御所に詰めている状態にすることとして、これに違反した者には所領没収などの措置が取られた<ref name=国史大辞典>田沼『国史大辞典』</ref>。[[江戸幕府]]も『[[禁中並公家諸法度]]』において「昼夜之御番」という表現で禁裏小番が朝廷に奉仕する公家の任務として、[[家業]]・学問と共に重視されるべきものと位置づけられていた<ref name=日本史大事典/>。
1443年（嘉吉3）9月に[[禁闕の変]]が起き、内裏に[[後南朝]]方が乱入した時は[[甘露寺親長]]、[[四辻季春]]の若手公家が太刀を抜いて敵方と戦い、[[後花園天皇]]を避難させた{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=13}}。
[[文明 (日本)|文明]]8年（[[1476年]]）、小番の中から近臣によって構成される内々（うちうち）番とそれ以外の外様番に分けられるようになり<ref name=日本史大事典/>、内々番と外様番では詰める番所なども異なっていた<ref name=日本歴史大事典/>。本来、内々と外様は時の天皇との親疎によって分けられていたが、時代が下るにつれて家ごとに固定化されて「内々」と「外様」は一種の公家の[[家格]]となった（完全に固定化された訳では無かったが）<ref name=日本歴史大事典/><ref name=hayashi/>。
[[寛文]]3年（[[1661年]]）、後水尾法皇は新たに即位する幼帝[[霊元天皇]]のために内々・外様の中から近臣となるべき者21名を抽出して新たな番を編成した。当初、新たな番は「奥之番」と呼ばれ、本来所属する内々番・外様番との掛け持ちであったが、寛文11年（[[1671年]]）に奥之番は本番所（内々・外様のそれぞれに設けられた詰所）への出仕が免除された。奥之番になった者は依然として内々・外様の名簿には名前が残されていたものの、新たな番に所属している間は事実上の専属となり、第3の小番である近習番（もしくは近習小番）が成立した<ref>林大樹「近世の近習小番について」『論集きんせい』第40号（2018年）/所収:林『天皇近臣と近世の朝廷』（吉川弘文館、2021年） 2021年、P140-146.</ref>。
[[慶応]]4年（[[明治]]元年・[[1868年]]）閏4月、宮中三番と呼ばれていた内々・外様・近習の区別が廃止されて、「内番衆」と呼ばれるようになった<ref name=日本史大事典/><ref name=日本歴史大事典/>。
== 編成 == 文亀二年（1502）、正月時の禁裏小番の編成は以下の通りであった{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=156-157}}。
近臣番
* 一番 松木宗綱 山科言国 広橋守光 * 二番 三条西実隆 勧修寺経郷 東坊城和長 * 三番 四辻季経 甘露寺元長 冷泉永宣 * 四番 正親町公兼 田向重治 五条為学 * 五番 綾小路俊量 白川忠富王 五辻富仲 * 六番 勧修寺政顕 鷲尾隆康
外様番
* 一番 中御門宣胤 橋本公夏 北畠政宗 * 二番 三条実香 園基富 清閑寺家幸 * 三番 町広光 中院通世 一条実治 * 四番 四辻実仲 二条実望 四条隆永 * 五番 武者小路縁光 姉小路基綱 四条隆秀 * 六番 冷泉政為 坊城俊名 世尊寺行季 * 七番 冷泉為広 高倉永康 柳原資定 * 八番 中山宣親 飛鳥井雅俊 一条公博 * 九番 小倉季種 持明院基春 日野高光 * 十番 高辻長直 大炊御門経名
近臣番は天皇に近い公家から選ばれ天盃が下された。外様番の中には家領経営のため、地方に下ったまま全く出仕しないものもいたという{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=158}}。

== 脚注 == <references/> == 参考文献 == *田沼睦「禁裏小番」『[[国史大辞典 (昭和時代)|国史大辞典]] 4』（[[吉川弘文館]] 1984年） ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3 *母利美和「禁裏小番」『[[日本史大事典]] 2』（[[平凡社]] 1993年） ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4 *本田慧子「禁裏小番」『[[日本歴史大事典]] 1』（[[小学館]] 2001年） ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6 * {{Citation|和書|author=中本真人|date=2016-7-27|title=宮廷の御神楽|publisher=新典社|ref={{SfnRef|中本真人|2016}} }} * {{Citation|和書|author=中本真人|date=2021-12-22|title=なぜ神楽は応仁の乱を乗り越えられたのか|publisher=新典社|ref={{SfnRef|中本真人|2021}} }}

revision.comment: Created page with "{{ページ番号|date=2021年8月}} '''Kinri Koban''' (禁裏小番) refers to the system, from the [[Middle Ages|middle ages]] through the [[Early modern period (Japan)|early modern period]], in which {{ill|Kuge|en|公家}} served in daily rotation ({{ill|Ban (Japanese system)|en|番}}) at the {{ill|Kinri|en|禁裏}} to attend and {{ill|Shukutoku|en|宿直}} for the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|天皇}}. They were tasked with guarding the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|天皇..."
revision.text:
{{ページ番号|date=2021年8月}} '''Kinri Koban''' (禁裏小番) refers to the system, from the [[Middle Ages|middle ages]] through the [[Early modern period (Japan)|early modern period]], in which {{ill|Kuge|en|公家}} served in daily rotation ({{ill|Ban (Japanese system)|en|番}}) at the {{ill|Kinri|en|禁裏}} to attend and {{ill|Shukutoku|en|宿直}} for the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|天皇}}.
They were tasked with guarding the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|天皇}} and the {{ill|Sacred Mirror|en|神鏡}}, located at the {{ill|Naishi-dokoro|en|内侍所}} {{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=160}}.
Court nobles, excluding those from the {{ill|Sekkan family|en|摂関家}}, served in this role from the age of 15 after {{ill|Genpuku|en|元服}} until 60 (although those with busy official duties or longstanding contributions were sometimes exempted by imperial decree)<ref name="日本史大事典">Mori, "Nihonshi Daijiten."</ref><ref name="日本歴史大事典">Honda, "Nihon Rekishi Daijiten."</ref>. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|戦国時代 (日本)}}, due to manpower shortages caused by the relocation of nobles to rural areas and the extinction of some families, there were cases where those younger than 15 performed these duties. In the {{ill|Edo period|en|江戸時代}}, {{ill|Emperor Go-Mizunoo|en|後水尾天皇}} established the age of 15 as the minimum for Koban duties, and the establishment of {{ill|Shinke|en|新家}} resolved personnel shortages. Non-official roles, such as {{ill|Oko|en|御児}} for miscellaneous tasks within the palace, were also fulfilled by appointees<ref>Hayashi Daiki, "The Role of 'Gojiko' in Early Modern Court Nobility," Jinbun, Issue 16, 2018; revised version in "Tenno Kinshin to Kinsei no Chotei" (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021), ISBN 978-4-642-04333-5, pp. 93–95.</ref>. In cases where fathers and sons (the family head and successor) served concurrently, sons were granted {{ill|Horyo|en|方領}} (land as compensation)<ref name="日本歴史大事典"/>. Additionally, those serving the {{ill|Retired Emperor|en|太上天皇}} or the {{ill|Crown Prince|en|皇太子}} were known as "In-san-shu" or "Togu Koban" respectively<ref name=hayashi>Hayashi Daiki, "Introduction to Modern Court Studies and Objectives," in "Tenno Kinshin to Kinsei no Chotei" (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021), pp. 8–9.</ref>.
The system varied over time, with rotations organized into groups of five to seven (e.g., five, six, or ten groups). In response to circumstances, the overall number of groups could be reduced or temporary additions ({{ill|Kaban|en|加番}}) ordered to increase group sizes<ref name="日本史大事典"/>. Each group was led by a head ({{ill|Banto|en|番頭}}) and overseen by a {{ill|Koban Bugyo|en|小番奉行}}, often concurrently held by a {{ill|Buke Denso|en|武家伝奏}}<ref name="日本史大事典"/>.
The term "Kinri Koban" was established during the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|室町時代}} (although the system itself predates this). Economic hardships during this time led to many neglecting their duties. To address this, in {{ill|Eikyo 2|en|永享}}, 1430, {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshinori|en|足利義教}} of the {{ill|Muromachi Shogunate|en|室町幕府}} implemented reforms mandating that someone from the rotation always be on duty in the palace. Violators faced confiscation of their estates<ref name="国史大辞典">Tanuma, "Kokushi Daijiten."</ref>. The {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|en|江戸幕府}} reinforced this in the {{ill|Kinchu Narabini Kuge Shohatto|en|禁中並公家諸法度}}, recognizing Koban as an essential duty alongside {{ill|Kagyo|en|家業}} and learning<ref name="日本史大事典"/>.
In September 1443 ({{ill|Kakitsu 3|en|嘉吉}}), during the {{ill|Rebellion of the Emperor's Mausoleum|en|禁闕の変}}, the palace was invaded by {{ill|Southern Court loyalists|en|後南朝}}, and young court nobles such as {{ill|Kanroji Chikanaga|en|甘露寺親長}} and {{ill|Yotsutsuji Tokiharu|en|四辻季春}} defended the {{ill|Emperor Go-Hanazono|en|後花園天皇}} by drawing swords and escorting him to safety{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=13}}.
In {{ill|Bunmei 8|en|文明}} (1476), Koban members were divided into "Uchi-uchi" (close attendants) and "Tazama" (outsiders){{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=156–157}}. Over time, these designations became hereditary and reflected a form of {{ill|Kuge House Rank|en|家格}}<ref name="日本歴史大事典"/><ref name=hayashi/>.
In {{ill|Kanbun 3|en|寛文}} (1661), {{ill|Retired Emperor Go-Mizunoo|en|後水尾法皇}} created a new rotation, "Oku no Ban," composed of 21 close attendants selected from the Uchi-uchi and Tazama groups. Initially, these members served double roles, but by {{ill|Kanbun 11|en|寛文}}, 1671, they were exempt from their original duties and formed the specialized "Kinjushu Koban" (Close Attendants)<ref>Hayashi Daiki, "On the Formation of Kinjushu Koban in the Early Modern Era," Ronshu Kinsei, Vol. 40, 2018.</ref>.
In {{ill|Keio 4|en|慶応}}, 1868, the distinctions between Uchi-uchi, Tazama, and Kinjushu were abolished, and the roles were consolidated under "Naiban-shu" (Inner Attendants)<ref name="日本史大事典"/><ref name="日本歴史大事典"/>.
== Structure == In {{ill|Bunki 2|en|文亀}}, 1502, the composition of Kinri Koban was as follows{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=156–157}}:
**Close Attendants (近臣番)**
* First Group: Matsuki Munetsuna, Yamashina Norikuni, Hirohata Morimitsu * Second Group: Sanjonishi Sanetaka, Kanshuji Tsunemichi, Higashibojo Kazunaga * ...
**Outsiders (外様番)**
* First Group: Nakamikado Nobutaka, Hashimoto Kimitomo, Kitabatake Masamune * ...
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|天皇}} * {{ill|Kinri|en|禁裏}} * {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|en|三種の神器}} * {{ill|Naishi-dokoro|en|内侍所}} * {{ill|Kuge|en|公家}}
== References == <references/>
== Bibliography == * Tanuma Mutsuo, "Kinri Koban," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten (Showa)|en|国史大辞典}}, Vol. 4, Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1984, ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3 * Mori Miwa, "Kinri Koban," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten|en|日本史大事典}}, Vol. 2, Heibonsha, 1993, ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4 * Honda Keiko, "Kinri Koban," in {{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|en|日本歴史大事典}}, Vol. 1, Shogakukan, 2001, ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6 * Nakamoto Makoto, {{ill|Court Rituals and Imperial Gagaku|en|宮廷の御神楽}}, Shinten-sha, 2016 * Nakamoto Makoto, {{ill|Why Kagura Survived the Onin War|en|なぜ神楽は応仁の乱を乗り越えられたのか}}, Shinten-sha, 2021
[[Category:Historical titles]] [[Category:Japanese nobility]] [[Category:Imperial Court of Japan]] [[ja:禁裏小番]]

{{ページ番号|date=2021年8月}} '''Kinri Koban''' (禁裏小番) refers to the system, from the [[Middle Ages|middle ages]] through the [[Early modern period (Japan)|early modern period]], in which {{ill|Kuge|en|公家}} served in daily rotation ({{ill|Ban (Japanese system)|en|番}}) at the {{ill|Kinri|en|禁裏}} to attend and {{ill|Shukutoku|en|宿直}} for the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|天皇}}.
They were tasked with guarding the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|天皇}} and the {{ill|Sacred Mirror|en|神鏡}}, located at the {{ill|Naishi-dokoro|en|内侍所}} {{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=160}}.
Court nobles, excluding those from the {{ill|Sekkan family|en|摂関家}}, served in this role from the age of 15 after {{ill|Genpuku|en|元服}} until 60 (although those with busy official duties or longstanding contributions were sometimes exempted by imperial decree)<ref name="日本史大事典">Mori, "Nihonshi Daijiten."</ref><ref name="日本歴史大事典">Honda, "Nihon Rekishi Daijiten."</ref>. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|戦国時代 (日本)}}, due to manpower shortages caused by the relocation of nobles to rural areas and the extinction of some families, there were cases where those younger than 15 performed these duties. In the {{ill|Edo period|en|江戸時代}}, {{ill|Emperor Go-Mizunoo|en|後水尾天皇}} established the age of 15 as the minimum for Koban duties, and the establishment of {{ill|Shinke|en|新家}} resolved personnel shortages. Non-official roles, such as {{ill|Oko|en|御児}} for miscellaneous tasks within the palace, were also fulfilled by appointees<ref>Hayashi Daiki, "The Role of 'Gojiko' in Early Modern Court Nobility," Jinbun, Issue 16, 2018; revised version in "Tenno Kinshin to Kinsei no Chotei" (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021), ISBN 978-4-642-04333-5, pp. 93–95.</ref>. In cases where fathers and sons (the family head and successor) served concurrently, sons were granted {{ill|Horyo|en|方領}} (land as compensation)<ref name="日本歴史大事典"/>. Additionally, those serving the {{ill|Retired Emperor|en|太上天皇}} or the {{ill|Crown Prince|en|皇太子}} were known as "In-san-shu" or "Togu Koban" respectively<ref name=hayashi>Hayashi Daiki, "Introduction to Modern Court Studies and Objectives," in "Tenno Kinshin to Kinsei no Chotei" (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021), pp. 8–9.</ref>.
The system varied over time, with rotations organized into groups of five to seven (e.g., five, six, or ten groups). In response to circumstances, the overall number of groups could be reduced or temporary additions ({{ill|Kaban|en|加番}}) ordered to increase group sizes<ref name="日本史大事典"/>. Each group was led by a head ({{ill|Banto|en|番頭}}) and overseen by a {{ill|Koban Bugyo|en|小番奉行}}, often concurrently held by a {{ill|Buke Denso|en|武家伝奏}}<ref name="日本史大事典"/>.
The term "Kinri Koban" was established during the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|室町時代}} (although the system itself predates this). Economic hardships during this time led to many neglecting their duties. To address this, in {{ill|Eikyo 2|en|永享}}, 1430, {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshinori|en|足利義教}} of the {{ill|Muromachi Shogunate|en|室町幕府}} implemented reforms mandating that someone from the rotation always be on duty in the palace. Violators faced confiscation of their estates<ref name="国史大辞典">Tanuma, "Kokushi Daijiten."</ref>. The {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|en|江戸幕府}} reinforced this in the {{ill|Kinchu Narabini Kuge Shohatto|en|禁中並公家諸法度}}, recognizing Koban as an essential duty alongside {{ill|Kagyo|en|家業}} and learning<ref name="日本史大事典"/>.
In September 1443 ({{ill|Kakitsu 3|en|嘉吉}}), during the {{ill|Rebellion of the Emperor's Mausoleum|en|禁闕の変}}, the palace was invaded by {{ill|Southern Court loyalists|en|後南朝}}, and young court nobles such as {{ill|Kanroji Chikanaga|en|甘露寺親長}} and {{ill|Yotsutsuji Tokiharu|en|四辻季春}} defended the {{ill|Emperor Go-Hanazono|en|後花園天皇}} by drawing swords and escorting him to safety{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=13}}.
In {{ill|Bunmei 8|en|文明}} (1476), Koban members were divided into "Uchi-uchi" (close attendants) and "Tazama" (outsiders){{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=156–157}}. Over time, these designations became hereditary and reflected a form of {{ill|Kuge House Rank|en|家格}}<ref name="日本歴史大事典"/><ref name=hayashi/>.
In {{ill|Kanbun 3|en|寛文}} (1661), {{ill|Retired Emperor Go-Mizunoo|en|後水尾法皇}} created a new rotation, "Oku no Ban," composed of 21 close attendants selected from the Uchi-uchi and Tazama groups. Initially, these members served double roles, but by {{ill|Kanbun 11|en|寛文}}, 1671, they were exempt from their original duties and formed the specialized "Kinjushu Koban" (Close Attendants)<ref>Hayashi Daiki, "On the Formation of Kinjushu Koban in the Early Modern Era," Ronshu Kinsei, Vol. 40, 2018.</ref>.
In {{ill|Keio 4|en|慶応}}, 1868, the distinctions between Uchi-uchi, Tazama, and Kinjushu were abolished, and the roles were consolidated under "Naiban-shu" (Inner Attendants)<ref name="日本史大事典"/><ref name="日本歴史大事典"/>.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|天皇}} * {{ill|Kinri|en|禁裏}} * {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|en|三種の神器}} * {{ill|Naishi-dokoro|en|内侍所}} * {{ill|Kuge|en|公家}}

[[Category:Historical titles]] [[Category:Japanese nobility]] [[Category:Imperial Court of Japan]] [[ja:禁裏小番]]

{{ページ番号|date=2021年8月}} '''Kinri Koban''' (禁裏小番) refers to the system, from the [[Middle Ages|middle ages]] through the [[Early modern period (Japan)|early modern period]], in which {{ill|Kuge|en|公家}} served in daily rotation ({{ill|Ban (Japanese system)|en|番}}) at the {{ill|Kinri|en|禁裏}} to attend and {{ill|Shukutoku|en|宿直}} for the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|天皇}}.
They were tasked with guarding the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|天皇}} and the {{ill|Sacred Mirror|en|神鏡}}, located at the {{ill|Naishi-dokoro|en|内侍所}} {{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=160}}.
Court nobles, excluding those from the {{ill|Sekkan family|en|摂関家}}, served in this role from the age of 15 after {{ill|Genpuku|en|元服}} until 60 (although those with busy official duties or longstanding contributions were sometimes exempted by imperial decree)<ref name="日本史大事典">Mori, "Nihonshi Daijiten."</ref><ref name="日本歴史大事典">Honda, "Nihon Rekishi Daijiten."</ref>. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|戦国時代 (日本)}}, due to manpower shortages caused by the relocation of nobles to rural areas and the extinction of some families, there were cases where those younger than 15 performed these duties. In the {{ill|Edo period|en|江戸時代}}, {{ill|Emperor Go-Mizunoo|en|後水尾天皇}} established the age of 15 as the minimum for Koban duties, and the establishment of {{ill|Shinke|en|新家}} resolved personnel shortages. Non-official roles, such as {{ill|Oko|en|御児}} for miscellaneous tasks within the palace, were also fulfilled by appointees<ref>Hayashi Daiki, "The Role of 'Gojiko' in Early Modern Court Nobility," Jinbun, Issue 16, 2018; revised version in "Tenno Kinshin to Kinsei no Chotei" (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021), ISBN 978-4-642-04333-5, pp. 93–95.</ref>. In cases where fathers and sons (the family head and successor) served concurrently, sons were granted {{ill|Horyo|en|方領}} (land as compensation)<ref name="日本歴史大事典"/>. Additionally, those serving the {{ill|Retired Emperor|en|太上天皇}} or the {{ill|Crown Prince|en|皇太子}} were known as "In-san-shu" or "Togu Koban" respectively<ref name=hayashi>Hayashi Daiki, "Introduction to Modern Court Studies and Objectives," in "Tenno Kinshin to Kinsei no Chotei" (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021), pp. 8–9.</ref>.
The system varied over time, with rotations organized into groups of five to seven (e.g., five, six, or ten groups). In response to circumstances, the overall number of groups could be reduced or temporary additions ({{ill|Kaban|en|加番}}) ordered to increase group sizes<ref name="日本史大事典"/>. Each group was led by a head ({{ill|Banto|en|番頭}}) and overseen by a {{ill|Koban Bugyo|en|小番奉行}}, often concurrently held by a {{ill|Buke Denso|en|武家伝奏}}<ref name="日本史大事典"/>.
The term "Kinri Koban" was established during the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|室町時代}} (although the system itself predates this). Economic hardships during this time led to many neglecting their duties. To address this, in {{ill|Eikyo 2|en|永享}}, 1430, {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshinori|en|足利義教}} of the {{ill|Muromachi Shogunate|en|室町幕府}} implemented reforms mandating that someone from the rotation always be on duty in the palace. Violators faced confiscation of their estates<ref name="国史大辞典">Tanuma, "Kokushi Daijiten."</ref>. The {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|en|江戸幕府}} reinforced this in the {{ill|Kinchu Narabini Kuge Shohatto|en|禁中並公家諸法度}}, recognizing Koban as an essential duty alongside {{ill|Kagyo|en|家業}} and learning<ref name="日本史大事典"/>.
In September 1443 ({{ill|Kakitsu 3|en|嘉吉}}), during the {{ill|Rebellion of the Emperor's Mausoleum|en|禁闕の変}}, the palace was invaded by {{ill|Southern Court loyalists|en|後南朝}}, and young court nobles such as {{ill|Kanroji Chikanaga|en|甘露寺親長}} and {{ill|Yotsutsuji Tokiharu|en|四辻季春}} defended the {{ill|Emperor Go-Hanazono|en|後花園天皇}} by drawing swords and escorting him to safety{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=13}}.
In {{ill|Bunmei 8|en|文明}} (1476), Koban members were divided into "Uchi-uchi" (close attendants) and "Tazama" (outsiders){{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=156–157}}. Over time, these designations became hereditary and reflected a form of {{ill|Kuge House Rank|en|家格}}<ref name="日本歴史大事典"/><ref name=hayashi/>.
In {{ill|Kanbun 3|en|寛文}} (1661), {{ill|Retired Emperor Go-Mizunoo|en|後水尾法皇}} created a new rotation, "Oku no Ban," composed of 21 close attendants selected from the Uchi-uchi and Tazama groups. Initially, these members served double roles, but by {{ill|Kanbun 11|en|寛文}}, 1671, they were exempt from their original duties and formed the specialized "Kinjushu Koban" (Close Attendants)<ref>Hayashi Daiki, "On the Formation of Kinjushu Koban in the Early Modern Era," Ronshu Kinsei, Vol. 40, 2018.</ref>.
In {{ill|Keio 4|en|慶応}}, 1868, the distinctions between Uchi-uchi, Tazama, and Kinjushu were abolished, and the roles were consolidated under "Naiban-shu" (Inner Attendants)<ref name="日本史大事典"/><ref name="日本歴史大事典"/>.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|天皇}} * {{ill|Kinri|en|禁裏}} * {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|en|三種の神器}} * {{ill|Naishi-dokoro|en|内侍所}} * {{ill|Kuge|en|公家}}
== References == <references/>
== Bibliography == * Tanuma Mutsuo, "Kinri Koban," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten (Showa)|en|国史大辞典}}, Vol. 4, Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1984, ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3 * Mori Miwa, "Kinri Koban," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten|en|日本史大事典}}, Vol. 2, Heibonsha, 1993, ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4 * Honda Keiko, "Kinri Koban," in {{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|en|日本歴史大事典}}, Vol. 1, Shogakukan, 2001, ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6 * Nakamoto Makoto, {{ill|Court Rituals and Imperial Gagaku|en|宮廷の御神楽}}, Shinten-sha, 2016 * Nakamoto Makoto, {{ill|Why Kagura Survived the Onin War|en|なぜ神楽は応仁の乱を乗り越えられたのか}}, Shinten-sha, 2021

The system varied over time, with rotations organized into groups of five to seven (e.g., five, six, or ten groups). In response to circumstances, the overall number of groups could be reduced or temporary additions ({{ill|Kaban|en|加番}}) ordered to increase group sizes<ref name="日本史大事典"/>. Each group was led by a head ({{ill|Banto|en|番頭}}) and overseen by a {{ill|Koban Bugyo|en|小番奉行}}, often concurrently held by a {{ill|Buke Denso|en|武家伝奏}}<ref name="日本史大事典"/>.
The term "Kinri Koban" was established during the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|室町時代}} (although the system itself predates this). Economic hardships during this time led to many neglecting their duties. To address this, in {{ill|Eikyo 2|en|永享}}, 1430, {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshinori|en|足利義教}} of the {{ill|Muromachi Shogunate|en|室町幕府}} implemented reforms mandating that someone from the rotation always be on duty in the palace. Violators faced confiscation of their estates<ref name="国史大辞典">Tanuma, "Kokushi Daijiten."</ref>. The {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|en|江戸幕府}} reinforced this in the {{ill|Kinchu Narabini Kuge Shohatto|en|禁中並公家諸法度}}, recognizing Koban as an essential duty alongside {{ill|Kagyo|en|家業}} and learning<ref name="日本史大事典"/>.
In September 1443 ({{ill|Kakitsu 3|en|嘉吉}}), during the {{ill|Rebellion of the Emperor's Mausoleum|en|禁闕の変}}, the palace was invaded by {{ill|Southern Court loyalists|en|後南朝}}, and young court nobles such as {{ill|Kanroji Chikanaga|en|甘露寺親長}} and {{ill|Yotsutsuji Tokiharu|en|四辻季春}} defended the {{ill|Emperor Go-Hanazono|en|後花園天皇}} by drawing swords and escorting him to safety{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=13}}.
In {{ill|Bunmei 8|en|文明}} (1476), Koban members were divided into "Uchi-uchi" (close attendants) and "Tazama" (outsiders){{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=156–157}}. Over time, these designations became hereditary and reflected a form of {{ill|Kuge House Rank|en|家格}}<ref name="日本歴史大事典"/><ref name=hayashi/>.
In {{ill|Kanbun 3|en|寛文}} (1661), {{ill|Retired Emperor Go-Mizunoo|en|後水尾法皇}} created a new rotation, "Oku no Ban," composed of 21 close attendants selected from the Uchi-uchi and Tazama groups. Initially, these members served double roles, but by {{ill|Kanbun 11|en|寛文}}, 1671, they were exempt from their original duties and formed the specialized "Kinjushu Koban" (Close Attendants)<ref>Hayashi Daiki, "On the Formation of Kinjushu Koban in the Early Modern Era," Ronshu Kinsei, Vol. 40, 2018.</ref>.
In {{ill|Keio 4|en|慶応}}, 1868, the distinctions between Uchi-uchi, Tazama, and Kinjushu were abolished, and the roles were consolidated under "Naiban-shu" (Inner Attendants)<ref name="日本史大事典"/><ref name="日本歴史大事典"/>.

{{ページ番号|date=2021年8月}} '''Kinri Koban''' (禁裏小番) refers to the system, from the [[Middle Ages|middle ages]] through the [[Early modern period (Japan)|early modern period]], in which {{ill|Kuge|en|公家}} served in daily rotation ({{ill|Ban (Japanese system)|en|番}}) at the {{ill|Kinri|en|禁裏}} to attend and {{ill|Shukutoku|en|宿直}} for the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|天皇}}.
They were tasked with guarding the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|天皇}} and the {{ill|Sacred Mirror|en|神鏡}}, located at the {{ill|Naishi-dokoro|en|内侍所}} {{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=160}}.
Court nobles, excluding those from the {{ill|Sekkan family|en|摂関家}}, served in this role from the age of 15 after {{ill|Genpuku|en|元服}} until 60 (although those with busy official duties or longstanding contributions were sometimes exempted by imperial decree)<ref name="日本史大事典">Mori, "Nihonshi Daijiten."</ref><ref name="日本歴史大事典">Honda, "Nihon Rekishi Daijiten."</ref>. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|戦国時代 (日本)}}, due to manpower shortages caused by the relocation of nobles to rural areas and the extinction of some families, there were cases where those younger than 15 performed these duties. In the {{ill|Edo period|en|江戸時代}}, {{ill|Emperor Go-Mizunoo|en|後水尾天皇}} established the age of 15 as the minimum for Koban duties, and the establishment of {{ill|Shinke|en|新家}} resolved personnel shortages. Non-official roles, such as {{ill|Oko|en|御児}} for miscellaneous tasks within the palace, were also fulfilled by appointees<ref>Hayashi Daiki, "The Role of 'Gojiko' in Early Modern Court Nobility," Jinbun, Issue 16, 2018; revised version in "Tenno Kinshin to Kinsei no Chotei" (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021), ISBN 978-4-642-04333-5, pp. 93–95.</ref>. In cases where fathers and sons (the family head and successor) served concurrently, sons were granted {{ill|Horyo|en|方領}} (land as compensation)<ref name="日本歴史大事典"/>. Additionally, those serving the {{ill|Retired Emperor|en|太上天皇}} or the {{ill|Crown Prince|en|皇太子}} were known as "In-san-shu" or "Togu Koban" respectively<ref name=hayashi>Hayashi Daiki, "Introduction to Modern Court Studies and Objectives," in "Tenno Kinshin to Kinsei no Chotei" (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021), pp. 8–9.</ref>.
The system varied over time, with rotations organized into groups of five to seven (e.g., five, six, or ten groups). In response to circumstances, the overall number of groups could be reduced or temporary additions ({{ill|Kaban|en|加番}}) ordered to increase group sizes<ref name="日本史大事典"/>. Each group was led by a head ({{ill|Banto|en|番頭}}) and overseen by a {{ill|Koban Bugyo|en|小番奉行}}, often concurrently held by a {{ill|Buke Denso|en|武家伝奏}}<ref name="日本史大事典"/>.
The term "Kinri Koban" was established during the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|室町時代}} (although the system itself predates this). Economic hardships during this time led to many neglecting their duties. To address this, in {{ill|Eikyo 2|en|永享}}, 1430, {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshinori|en|足利義教}} of the {{ill|Muromachi Shogunate|en|室町幕府}} implemented reforms mandating that someone from the rotation always be on duty in the palace. Violators faced confiscation of their estates<ref name="国史大辞典">Tanuma, "Kokushi Daijiten."</ref>. The {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|en|江戸幕府}} reinforced this in the {{ill|Kinchu Narabini Kuge Shohatto|en|禁中並公家諸法度}}, recognizing Koban as an essential duty alongside {{ill|Kagyo|en|家業}} and learning<ref name="日本史大事典"/>.
In September 1443 ({{ill|Kakitsu 3|en|嘉吉}}), during the {{ill|Rebellion of the Emperor's Mausoleum|en|禁闕の変}}, the palace was invaded by {{ill|Southern Court loyalists|en|後南朝}}, and young court nobles such as {{ill|Kanroji Chikanaga|en|甘露寺親長}} and {{ill|Yotsutsuji Tokiharu|en|四辻季春}} defended the {{ill|Emperor Go-Hanazono|en|後花園天皇}} by drawing swords and escorting him to safety{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=13}}.
In {{ill|Bunmei 8|en|文明}} (1476), Koban members were divided into "Uchi-uchi" (close attendants) and "Tazama" (outsiders){{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=156–157}}. Over time, these designations became hereditary and reflected a form of {{ill|Kuge House Rank|en|家格}}<ref name="日本歴史大事典"/><ref name=hayashi/>.
In {{ill|Kanbun 3|en|寛文}} (1661), {{ill|Retired Emperor Go-Mizunoo|en|後水尾法皇}} created a new rotation, "Oku no Ban," composed of 21 close attendants selected from the Uchi-uchi and Tazama groups. Initially, these members served double roles, but by {{ill|Kanbun 11|en|寛文}}, 1671, they were exempt from their original duties and formed the specialized "Kinjushu Koban" (Close Attendants)<ref>Hayashi Daiki, "On the Formation of Kinjushu Koban in the Early Modern Era," Ronshu Kinsei, Vol. 40, 2018.</ref>.
In {{ill|Keio 4|en|慶応}}, 1868, the distinctions between Uchi-uchi, Tazama, and Kinjushu were abolished, and the roles were consolidated under "Naiban-shu" (Inner Attendants)<ref name="日本史大事典"/><ref name="日本歴史大事典"/>.

{{ページ番号|date=2021年8月}} '''Kinri Koban''' (禁裏小番) refers to the system, from the [[Middle Ages|middle ages]] through the [[Early modern period (Japan)|early modern period]], in which {{ill|Kuge|en|公家}} served in daily rotation ({{ill|Ban (Japanese system)|en|番}}) at the {{ill|Kinri|en|禁裏}} to attend and {{ill|Shukutoku|en|宿直}} for the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|天皇}}.
They were tasked with guarding the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|天皇}} and the {{ill|Sacred Mirror|en|神鏡}}, located at the {{ill|Naishi-dokoro|en|内侍所}} {{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=160}}.
Court nobles, excluding those from the {{ill|Sekkan family|en|摂関家}}, served in this role from the age of 15 after {{ill|Genpuku|en|元服}} until 60 (although those with busy official duties or longstanding contributions were sometimes exempted by imperial decree)<ref name="日本史大事典">Mori, "Nihonshi Daijiten."</ref><ref name="日本歴史大事典">Honda, "Nihon Rekishi Daijiten."</ref>. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|戦国時代 (日本)}}, due to manpower shortages caused by the relocation of nobles to rural areas and the extinction of some families, there were cases where those younger than 15 performed these duties. In the {{ill|Edo period|en|江戸時代}}, {{ill|Emperor Go-Mizunoo|en|後水尾天皇}} established the age of 15 as the minimum for Koban duties, and the establishment of {{ill|Shinke|en|新家}} resolved personnel shortages. Non-official roles, such as {{ill|Oko|en|御児}} for miscellaneous tasks within the palace, were also fulfilled by appointees<ref>Hayashi Daiki, "The Role of 'Gojiko' in Early Modern Court Nobility," Jinbun, Issue 16, 2018; revised version in "Tenno Kinshin to Kinsei no Chotei" (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021), ISBN 978-4-642-04333-5, pp. 93–95.</ref>. In cases where fathers and sons (the family head and successor) served concurrently, sons were granted {{ill|Horyo|en|方領}} (land as compensation)<ref name="日本歴史大事典"/>. Additionally, those serving the {{ill|Retired Emperor|en|太上天皇}} or the {{ill|Crown Prince|en|皇太子}} were known as "In-san-shu" or "Togu Koban" respectively<ref name=hayashi>Hayashi Daiki, "Introduction to Modern Court Studies and Objectives," in "Tenno Kinshin to Kinsei no Chotei" (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021), pp. 8–9.</ref>.
The system varied over time, with rotations organized into groups of five to seven (e.g., five, six, or ten groups). In response to circumstances, the overall number of groups could be reduced or temporary additions ({{ill|Kaban|en|加番}}) ordered to increase group sizes<ref name="日本史大事典"/>. Each group was led by a head ({{ill|Banto|en|番頭}}) and overseen by a {{ill|Koban Bugyo|en|小番奉行}}, often concurrently held by a {{ill|Buke Denso|en|武家伝奏}}<ref name="日本史大事典"/>.
The term "Kinri Koban" was established during the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|室町時代}} (although the system itself predates this). Economic hardships during this time led to many neglecting their duties. To address this, in {{ill|Eikyo 2|en|永享}}, 1430, {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshinori|en|足利義教}} of the {{ill|Muromachi Shogunate|en|室町幕府}} implemented reforms mandating that someone from the rotation always be on duty in the palace. Violators faced confiscation of their estates<ref name="国史大辞典">Tanuma, "Kokushi Daijiten."</ref>. The {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|en|江戸幕府}} reinforced this in the {{ill|Kinchu Narabini Kuge Shohatto|en|禁中並公家諸法度}}, recognizing Koban as an essential duty alongside {{ill|Kagyo|en|家業}} and learning<ref name="日本史大事典"/>.
In September 1443 ({{ill|Kakitsu 3|en|嘉吉}}), during the {{ill|Rebellion of the Emperor's Mausoleum|en|禁闕の変}}, the palace was invaded by {{ill|Southern Court loyalists|en|後南朝}}, and young court nobles such as {{ill|Kanroji Chikanaga|en|甘露寺親長}} and {{ill|Yotsutsuji Tokiharu|en|四辻季春}} defended the {{ill|Emperor Go-Hanazono|en|後花園天皇}} by drawing swords and escorting him to safety{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=13}}.
In {{ill|Bunmei 8|en|文明}} (1476), Koban members were divided into "Uchi-uchi" (close attendants) and "Tazama" (outsiders){{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=156–157}}. Over time, these designations became hereditary and reflected a form of {{ill|Kuge House Rank|en|家格}}<ref name="日本歴史大事典"/><ref name=hayashi/>.
In {{ill|Kanbun 3|en|寛文}} (1661), {{ill|Retired Emperor Go-Mizunoo|en|後水尾法皇}} created a new rotation, "Oku no Ban," composed of 21 close attendants selected from the Uchi-uchi and Tazama groups. Initially, these members served double roles, but by {{ill|Kanbun 11|en|寛文}}, 1671, they were exempt from their original duties and formed the specialized "Kinjushu Koban" (Close Attendants)<ref>Hayashi Daiki, "On the Formation of Kinjushu Koban in the Early Modern Era," Ronshu Kinsei, Vol. 40, 2018.</ref>.
In {{ill|Keio 4|en|慶応}}, 1868, the distinctions between Uchi-uchi, Tazama, and Kinjushu were abolished, and the roles were consolidated under "Naiban-shu" (Inner Attendants)<ref name="日本史大事典"/><ref name="日本歴史大事典"/>.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|天皇}} * {{ill|Kinri|en|禁裏}} * {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|en|三種の神器}} * {{ill|Naishi-dokoro|en|内侍所}} * {{ill|Kuge|en|公家}}
== References == <references/>
== Bibliography == * Tanuma Mutsuo, "Kinri Koban," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten (Showa)|en|国史大辞典}}, Vol. 4, Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1984, ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3 * Mori Miwa, "Kinri Koban," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten|en|日本史大事典}}, Vol. 2, Heibonsha, 1993, ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4 * Honda Keiko, "Kinri Koban," in {{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|en|日本歴史大事典}}, Vol. 1, Shogakukan, 2001, ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6 * Nakamoto Makoto, {{ill|Court Rituals and Imperial Gagaku|en|宮廷の御神楽}}, Shinten-sha, 2016 * Nakamoto Makoto, {{ill|Why Kagura Survived the Onin War|en|なぜ神楽は応仁の乱を乗り越えられたのか}}, Shinten-sha, 2021

In September 1443 ({{ill|Kakitsu 3|en|嘉吉}}), during the {{ill|Rebellion of the Emperor's Mausoleum|en|禁闕の変}}, the palace was invaded by {{ill|Southern Court loyalists|en|後南朝}}, and young court nobles such as {{ill|Kanroji Chikanaga|en|甘露寺親長}} and {{ill|Yotsutsuji Tokiharu|en|四辻季春}} defended the {{ill|Emperor Go-Hanazono|en|後花園天皇}} by drawing swords and escorting him to safety{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=13}}.
In {{ill|Bunmei 8|en|文明}} (1476), Koban members were divided into "Uchi-uchi" (close attendants) and "Tazama" (outsiders){{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=156–157}}. Over time, these designations became hereditary and reflected a form of {{ill|Kuge House Rank|en|家格}}<ref name="日本歴史大事典"/><ref name=hayashi/>.
In {{ill|Kanbun 3|en|寛文}} (1661), {{ill|Retired Emperor Go-Mizunoo|en|後水尾法皇}} created a new rotation, "Oku no Ban," composed of 21 close attendants selected from the Uchi-uchi and Tazama groups. Initially, these members served double roles, but by {{ill|Kanbun 11|en|寛文}}, 1671, they were exempt from their original duties and formed the specialized "Kinjushu Koban" (Close Attendants)<ref>Hayashi Daiki, "On the Formation of Kinjushu Koban in the Early Modern Era," Ronshu Kinsei, Vol. 40, 2018.</ref>.
In {{ill|Keio 4|en|慶応}}, 1868, the distinctions between Uchi-uchi, Tazama, and Kinjushu were abolished, and the roles were consolidated under "Naiban-shu" (Inner Attendants)<ref name="日本史大事典"/><ref name="日本歴史大事典"/>.

[[Category:Historical titles]] [[Category:Japanese nobility]] [[Category:Imperial Court of Japan]] [[Category:Pages_with_redlinks_to_fill]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Kinri Koban]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:original pages]][[category:Kinri Koban]][[category:original pages]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Kinri Koban]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Kinri Koban]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:original pages]][[category:Kinri Koban]][[category:original pages]] [[Category:original pages]][[category:Kinri Koban]][[category:original pages]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Kinri Koban]][[Category:pp]] [[ja:禁裏小番]]

{{ページ番号|date=2021年8月}} '''Kinri Koban''' (禁裏小番) refers to the system, from the [[Middle Ages|middle ages]] through the [[Early modern period (Japan)|early modern period]], in which {{ill|Kuge|en|公家}} served in daily rotation ({{ill|Ban (Japanese system)|en|番}}) at the {{ill|Kinri|en|禁裏}} to attend and {{ill|Shukutoku|en|宿直}} for the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|天皇}}.
They were tasked with guarding the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|天皇}} and the {{ill|Sacred Mirror|en|神鏡}}, located at the {{ill|Naishi-dokoro|en|内侍所}} {{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=160}}.
Court nobles, excluding those from the {{ill|Sekkan family|en|摂関家}}, served in this role from the age of 15 after {{ill|Genpuku|en|元服}} until 60 (although those with busy official duties or longstanding contributions were sometimes exempted by imperial decree)<ref name="日本史大事典">Mori, "Nihonshi Daijiten."</ref><ref name="日本歴史大事典">Honda, "Nihon Rekishi Daijiten."</ref>. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|戦国時代 (日本)}}, due to manpower shortages caused by the relocation of nobles to rural areas and the extinction of some families, there were cases where those younger than 15 performed these duties. In the {{ill|Edo period|en|江戸時代}}, {{ill|Emperor Go-Mizunoo|en|後水尾天皇}} established the age of 15 as the minimum for Koban duties, and the establishment of {{ill|Shinke|en|新家}} resolved personnel shortages. Non-official roles, such as {{ill|Oko|en|御児}} for miscellaneous tasks within the palace, were also fulfilled by appointees<ref>Hayashi Daiki, "The Role of 'Gojiko' in Early Modern Court Nobility," Jinbun, Issue 16, 2018; revised version in "Tenno Kinshin to Kinsei no Chotei" (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021), ISBN 978-4-642-04333-5, pp. 93–95.</ref>. In cases where fathers and sons (the family head and successor) served concurrently, sons were granted {{ill|Horyo|en|方領}} (land as compensation)<ref name="日本歴史大事典"/>. Additionally, those serving the {{ill|Retired Emperor|en|太上天皇}} or the {{ill|Crown Prince|en|皇太子}} were known as "In-san-shu" or "Togu Koban" respectively<ref name=hayashi>Hayashi Daiki, "Introduction to Modern Court Studies and Objectives," in "Tenno Kinshin to Kinsei no Chotei" (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021), pp. 8–9.</ref>.
The system varied over time, with rotations organized into groups of five to seven (e.g., five, six, or ten groups). In response to circumstances, the overall number of groups could be reduced or temporary additions ({{ill|Kaban|en|加番}}) ordered to increase group sizes<ref name="日本史大事典"/>. Each group was led by a head ({{ill|Banto|en|番頭}}) and overseen by a {{ill|Koban Bugyo|en|小番奉行}}, often concurrently held by a {{ill|Buke Denso|en|武家伝奏}}<ref name="日本史大事典"/>.
The term "Kinri Koban" was established during the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|室町時代}} (although the system itself predates this). Economic hardships during this time led to many neglecting their duties. To address this, in {{ill|Eikyo 2|en|永享}}, 1430, {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshinori|en|足利義教}} of the {{ill|Muromachi Shogunate|en|室町幕府}} implemented reforms mandating that someone from the rotation always be on duty in the palace. Violators faced confiscation of their estates<ref name="国史大辞典">Tanuma, "Kokushi Daijiten."</ref>. The {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|en|江戸幕府}} reinforced this in the {{ill|Kinchu Narabini Kuge Shohatto|en|禁中並公家諸法度}}, recognizing Koban as an essential duty alongside {{ill|Kagyo|en|家業}} and learning<ref name="日本史大事典"/>.
In September 1443 ({{ill|Kakitsu 3|en|嘉吉}}), during the {{ill|Rebellion of the Emperor's Mausoleum|en|禁闕の変}}, the palace was invaded by {{ill|Southern Court loyalists|en|後南朝}}, and young court nobles such as {{ill|Kanroji Chikanaga|en|甘露寺親長}} and {{ill|Yotsutsuji Tokiharu|en|四辻季春}} defended the {{ill|Emperor Go-Hanazono|en|後花園天皇}} by drawing swords and escorting him to safety{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=13}}.
In {{ill|Bunmei 8|en|文明}} (1476), Koban members were divided into "Uchi-uchi" (close attendants) and "Tazama" (outsiders){{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=156–157}}. Over time, these designations became hereditary and reflected a form of {{ill|Kuge House Rank|en|家格}}<ref name="日本歴史大事典"/><ref name=hayashi/>.
In {{ill|Kanbun 3|en|寛文}} (1661), {{ill|Retired Emperor Go-Mizunoo|en|後水尾法皇}} created a new rotation, "Oku no Ban," composed of 21 close attendants selected from the Uchi-uchi and Tazama groups. Initially, these members served double roles, but by {{ill|Kanbun 11|en|寛文}}, 1671, they were exempt from their original duties and formed the specialized "Kinjushu Koban" (Close Attendants)<ref>Hayashi Daiki, "On the Formation of Kinjushu Koban in the Early Modern Era," Ronshu Kinsei, Vol. 40, 2018.</ref>.
In {{ill|Keio 4|en|慶応}}, 1868, the distinctions between Uchi-uchi, Tazama, and Kinjushu were abolished, and the roles were consolidated under "Naiban-shu" (Inner Attendants)<ref name="日本史大事典"/><ref name="日本歴史大事典"/>.

[[Category:Historical titles]] [[Category:Japanese nobility]] [[Category:Imperial Court of Japan]] [[Category:Pages_with_redlinks_to_fill]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Kinri Koban]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:original pages]][[category:Kinri Koban]][[category:original pages]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Kinri Koban]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Kinri Koban]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:original pages]][[category:Kinri Koban]][[category:original pages]] [[Category:original pages]][[category:Kinri Koban]][[category:original pages]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Kinri Koban]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:bump]] [[Category:original pages]][[category:Kinri Koban]][[category:original pages]] [[Category:dd]] [[ja:禁裏小番]]

{{ページ番号|date=2021年8月}} '''Kinri Koban''' (禁裏小番) refers to the system, from the [[Middle Ages|middle ages]] through the [[Early modern period (Japan)|early modern period]], in which {{ill|Kuge|en|公家}} served in daily rotation ({{ill|Ban (Japanese system)|en|番}}) at the {{ill|Kinri|en|禁裏}} to attend and {{ill|Shukutoku|en|宿直}} for the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|天皇}}.
They were tasked with guarding the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|天皇}} and the {{ill|Sacred Mirror|en|神鏡}}, located at the {{ill|Naishi-dokoro|en|内侍所}} {{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=160}}.
Court nobles, excluding those from the {{ill|Sekkan family|en|摂関家}}, served in this role from the age of 15 after {{ill|Genpuku|en|元服}} until 60 (although those with busy official duties or longstanding contributions were sometimes exempted by imperial decree)<ref name="日本史大事典">Mori, "Nihonshi Daijiten."</ref><ref name="日本歴史大事典">Honda, "Nihon Rekishi Daijiten."</ref>. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|戦国時代 (日本)}}, due to manpower shortages caused by the relocation of nobles to rural areas and the extinction of some families, there were cases where those younger than 15 performed these duties. In the {{ill|Edo period|en|江戸時代}}, {{ill|Emperor Go-Mizunoo|en|後水尾天皇}} established the age of 15 as the minimum for Koban duties, and the establishment of {{ill|Shinke|en|新家}} resolved personnel shortages. Non-official roles, such as {{ill|Oko|en|御児}} for miscellaneous tasks within the palace, were also fulfilled by appointees<ref>Hayashi Daiki, "The Role of 'Gojiko' in Early Modern Court Nobility," Jinbun, Issue 16, 2018; revised version in "Tenno Kinshin to Kinsei no Chotei" (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021), ISBN 978-4-642-04333-5, pp. 93–95.</ref>. In cases where fathers and sons (the family head and successor) served concurrently, sons were granted {{ill|Horyo|en|方領}} (land as compensation)<ref name="日本歴史大事典"/>. Additionally, those serving the {{ill|Retired Emperor|en|太上天皇}} or the {{ill|Crown Prince|en|皇太子}} were known as "In-san-shu" or "Togu Koban" respectively<ref name=hayashi>Hayashi Daiki, "Introduction to Modern Court Studies and Objectives," in "Tenno Kinshin to Kinsei no Chotei" (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021), pp. 8–9.</ref>.
The system varied over time, with rotations organized into groups of five to seven (e.g., five, six, or ten groups). In response to circumstances, the overall number of groups could be reduced or temporary additions ({{ill|Kaban|en|加番}}) ordered to increase group sizes<ref name="日本史大事典"/>. Each group was led by a head ({{ill|Banto|en|番頭}}) and overseen by a {{ill|Koban Bugyo|en|小番奉行}}, often concurrently held by a {{ill|Buke Denso|en|武家伝奏}}<ref name="日本史大事典"/>.
The term "Kinri Koban" was established during the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|室町時代}} (although the system itself predates this). Economic hardships during this time led to many neglecting their duties. To address this, in {{ill|Eikyo 2|en|永享}}, 1430, {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshinori|en|足利義教}} of the {{ill|Muromachi Shogunate|en|室町幕府}} implemented reforms mandating that someone from the rotation always be on duty in the palace. Violators faced confiscation of their estates<ref name="国史大辞典">Tanuma, "Kokushi Daijiten."</ref>. The {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|en|江戸幕府}} reinforced this in the {{ill|Kinchu Narabini Kuge Shohatto|en|禁中並公家諸法度}}, recognizing Koban as an essential duty alongside {{ill|Kagyo|en|家業}} and learning<ref name="日本史大事典"/>.
In September 1443 ({{ill|Kakitsu 3|en|嘉吉}}), during the {{ill|Rebellion of the Emperor's Mausoleum|en|禁闕の変}}, the palace was invaded by {{ill|Southern Court loyalists|en|後南朝}}, and young court nobles such as {{ill|Kanroji Chikanaga|en|甘露寺親長}} and {{ill|Yotsutsuji Tokiharu|en|四辻季春}} defended the {{ill|Emperor Go-Hanazono|en|後花園天皇}} by drawing swords and escorting him to safety{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=13}}.
In {{ill|Bunmei 8|en|文明}} (1476), Koban members were divided into "Uchi-uchi" (close attendants) and "Tazama" (outsiders){{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=156–157}}. Over time, these designations became hereditary and reflected a form of {{ill|Kuge House Rank|en|家格}}<ref name="日本歴史大事典"/><ref name=hayashi/>.
In {{ill|Kanbun 3|en|寛文}} (1661), {{ill|Retired Emperor Go-Mizunoo|en|後水尾法皇}} created a new rotation, "Oku no Ban," composed of 21 close attendants selected from the Uchi-uchi and Tazama groups. Initially, these members served double roles, but by {{ill|Kanbun 11|en|寛文}}, 1671, they were exempt from their original duties and formed the specialized "Kinjushu Koban" (Close Attendants)<ref>Hayashi Daiki, "On the Formation of Kinjushu Koban in the Early Modern Era," Ronshu Kinsei, Vol. 40, 2018.</ref>.
In {{ill|Keio 4|en|慶応}}, 1868, the distinctions between Uchi-uchi, Tazama, and Kinjushu were abolished, and the roles were consolidated under "Naiban-shu" (Inner Attendants)<ref name="日本史大事典"/><ref name="日本歴史大事典"/>.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|天皇}} * {{ill|Kinri|en|禁裏}} * {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|en|三種の神器}} * {{ill|Naishi-dokoro|en|内侍所}} * {{ill|Kuge|en|公家}}
== References == <references/>
== Bibliography == * Tanuma Mutsuo, "Kinri Koban," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten (Showa)|en|国史大辞典}}, Vol. 4, Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1984, ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3 * Mori Miwa, "Kinri Koban," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten|en|日本史大事典}}, Vol. 2, Heibonsha, 1993, ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4 * Honda Keiko, "Kinri Koban," in {{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|en|日本歴史大事典}}, Vol. 1, Shogakukan, 2001, ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6 * Nakamoto Makoto, {{ill|Court Rituals and Imperial Gagaku|en|宮廷の御神楽}}, Shinten-sha, 2016 * Nakamoto Makoto, {{ill|Why Kagura Survived the Onin War|en|なぜ神楽は応仁の乱を乗り越えられたのか}}, Shinten-sha, 2021

During the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|戦国時代 (日本)}}, due to manpower shortages caused by the relocation of nobles to rural areas and the extinction of some families, there were cases where those younger than 15 performed these duties. In the {{ill|Edo period|en|江戸時代}}, {{ill|Emperor Go-Mizunoo|en|後水尾天皇}} established the age of 15 as the minimum for Koban duties, and the establishment of {{ill|Shinke|en|新家}} resolved personnel shortages. Non-official roles, such as {{ill|Oko|en|御児}} for miscellaneous tasks within the palace, were also fulfilled by appointees<ref>Hayashi Daiki, "The Role of 'Gojiko' in Early Modern Court Nobility," Jinbun, Issue 16, 2018; revised version in "Tenno Kinshin to Kinsei no Chotei" (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021), ISBN 978-4-642-04333-5, pp. 93–95.</ref>. In cases where fathers and sons (the family head and successor) served concurrently, sons were granted {{ill|Horyo|en|方領}} (land as compensation)<ref name="日本歴史大事典"/>. Additionally, those serving the {{ill|Retired Emperor|en|太上天皇}} or the {{ill|Crown Prince|en|皇太子}} were known as "In-san-shu" or "Togu Koban" respectively<ref name=hayashi>Hayashi Daiki, "Introduction to Modern Court Studies and Objectives," in "Tenno Kinshin to Kinsei no Chotei" (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021), pp. 8–9.</ref>.
The system varied over time, with rotations organized into groups of five to seven (e.g., five, six, or ten groups). In response to circumstances, the overall number of groups could be reduced or temporary additions ({{ill|Kaban|en|加番}}) ordered to increase group sizes<ref name="日本史大事典"/>. Each group was led by a head ({{ill|Banto|en|番頭}}) and overseen by a {{ill|Koban Bugyo|en|小番奉行}}, often concurrently held by a {{ill|Buke Denso|en|武家伝奏}}<ref name="日本史大事典"/>.
The term "Kinri Koban" was established during the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|室町時代}} (although the system itself predates this). Economic hardships during this time led to many neglecting their duties. To address this, in {{ill|Eikyo 2|en|永享}}, 1430, {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshinori|en|足利義教}} of the {{ill|Muromachi Shogunate|en|室町幕府}} implemented reforms mandating that someone from the rotation always be on duty in the palace. Violators faced confiscation of their estates<ref name="国史大辞典">Tanuma, "Kokushi Daijiten."</ref>. The {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|en|江戸幕府}} reinforced this in the {{ill|Kinchu Narabini Kuge Shohatto|en|禁中並公家諸法度}}, recognizing Koban as an essential duty alongside {{ill|Kagyo|en|家業}} and learning<ref name="日本史大事典"/>.
In September 1443 ({{ill|Kakitsu 3|en|嘉吉}}), during the {{ill|Rebellion of the Emperor's Mausoleum|en|禁闕の変}}, the palace was invaded by {{ill|Southern Court loyalists|en|後南朝}}, and young court nobles such as {{ill|Kanroji Chikanaga|en|甘露寺親長}} and {{ill|Yotsutsuji Tokiharu|en|四辻季春}} defended the {{ill|Emperor Go-Hanazono|en|後花園天皇}} by drawing swords and escorting him to safety{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=13}}.
In {{ill|Bunmei 8|en|文明}} (1476), Koban members were divided into "Uchi-uchi" (close attendants) and "Tazama" (outsiders){{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=156–157}}. Over time, these designations became hereditary and reflected a form of {{ill|Kuge House Rank|en|家格}}<ref name="日本歴史大事典"/><ref name=hayashi/>.
In {{ill|Kanbun 3|en|寛文}} (1661), {{ill|Retired Emperor Go-Mizunoo|en|後水尾法皇}} created a new rotation, "Oku no Ban," composed of 21 close attendants selected from the Uchi-uchi and Tazama groups. Initially, these members served double roles, but by {{ill|Kanbun 11|en|寛文}}, 1671, they were exempt from their original duties and formed the specialized "Kinjushu Koban" (Close Attendants)<ref>Hayashi Daiki, "On the Formation of Kinjushu Koban in the Early Modern Era," Ronshu Kinsei, Vol. 40, 2018.</ref>.
In {{ill|Keio 4|en|慶応}}, 1868, the distinctions between Uchi-uchi, Tazama, and Kinjushu were abolished, and the roles were consolidated under "Naiban-shu" (Inner Attendants)<ref name="日本史大事典"/><ref name="日本歴史大事典"/>.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|天皇}} * {{ill|Kinri|en|禁裏}} * {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|en|三種の神器}} * {{ill|Naishi-dokoro|en|内侍所}} * {{ill|Kuge|en|公家}}
== References == <references/>
== Bibliography == * Tanuma Mutsuo, "Kinri Koban," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten (Showa)|en|国史大辞典}}, Vol. 4, Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1984, ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3 * Mori Miwa, "Kinri Koban," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten|en|日本史大事典}}, Vol. 2, Heibonsha, 1993, ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4 * Honda Keiko, "Kinri Koban," in {{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|en|日本歴史大事典}}, Vol. 1, Shogakukan, 2001, ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6 * Nakamoto Makoto, {{ill|Court Rituals and Imperial Gagaku|en|宮廷の御神楽}}, Shinten-sha, 2016 * Nakamoto Makoto, {{ill|Why Kagura Survived the Onin War|en|なぜ神楽は応仁の乱を乗り越えられたのか}}, Shinten-sha, 2021
[[Category:Historical titles]] [[Category:Japanese nobility]] [[Category:Imperial Court of Japan]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Kinri Koban]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:original pages]][[category:Kinri Koban]][[category:original pages]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Kinri Koban]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Kinri Koban]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:original pages]][[category:Kinri Koban]][[category:original pages]] [[Category:original pages]][[category:Kinri Koban]][[category:original pages]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Kinri Koban]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:bump]] [[Category:original pages]][[category:Kinri Koban]][[category:original pages]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Kinri Koban]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:ファイル]] [[category:most recent 500]][[ja:禁裏小番]]

{{ページ番号|date=2021年8月}} '''Kinri Koban''' (禁裏小番) refers to the system, from the [[Middle Ages|middle ages]] through the [[Early modern period (Japan)|early modern period]], in which {{ill|Kuge|en|公家}} served in daily rotation ({{ill|Ban (Japanese system)|en|番}}) at the {{ill|Kinri|en|禁裏}} to attend and {{ill|Shukutoku|en|宿直}} for the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|天皇}}.
They were tasked with guarding the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|天皇}} and the {{ill|Sacred Mirror|en|神鏡}}, located at the {{ill|Naishi-dokoro|en|内侍所}} {{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=160}}.
Court nobles, excluding those from the {{ill|Sekkan family|en|摂関家}}, served in this role from the age of 15 after {{ill|Genpuku|en|元服}} until 60 (although those with busy official duties or longstanding contributions were sometimes exempted by imperial decree)<ref name="日本史大事典">Mori, "Nihonshi Daijiten."</ref><ref name="日本歴史大事典">Honda, "Nihon Rekishi Daijiten."</ref>. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|戦国時代 (日本)}}, due to manpower shortages caused by the relocation of nobles to rural areas and the extinction of some families, there were cases where those younger than 15 performed these duties. In the {{ill|Edo period|en|江戸時代}}, {{ill|Emperor Go-Mizunoo|en|後水尾天皇}} established the age of 15 as the minimum for Koban duties, and the establishment of {{ill|Shinke|en|新家}} resolved personnel shortages. Non-official roles, such as {{ill|Oko|en|御児}} for miscellaneous tasks within the palace, were also fulfilled by appointees<ref>Hayashi Daiki, "The Role of 'Gojiko' in Early Modern Court Nobility," Jinbun, Issue 16, 2018; revised version in "Tenno Kinshin to Kinsei no Chotei" (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021), ISBN 978-4-642-04333-5, pp. 93–95.</ref>. In cases where fathers and sons (the family head and successor) served concurrently, sons were granted {{ill|Horyo|en|方領}} (land as compensation)<ref name="日本歴史大事典"/>. Additionally, those serving the {{ill|Retired Emperor|en|太上天皇}} or the {{ill|Crown Prince|en|皇太子}} were known as "In-san-shu" or "Togu Koban" respectively<ref name=hayashi>Hayashi Daiki, "Introduction to Modern Court Studies and Objectives," in "Tenno Kinshin to Kinsei no Chotei" (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021), pp. 8–9.</ref>.
The system varied over time, with rotations organized into groups of five to seven (e.g., five, six, or ten groups). In response to circumstances, the overall number of groups could be reduced or temporary additions ({{ill|Kaban|en|加番}}) ordered to increase group sizes<ref name="日本史大事典"/>. Each group was led by a head ({{ill|Banto|en|番頭}}) and overseen by a {{ill|Koban Bugyo|en|小番奉行}}, often concurrently held by a {{ill|Buke Denso|en|武家伝奏}}<ref name="日本史大事典"/>.
The term "Kinri Koban" was established during the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|室町時代}} (although the system itself predates this). Economic hardships during this time led to many neglecting their duties. To address this, in {{ill|Eikyo 2|en|永享}}, 1430, {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshinori|en|足利義教}} of the {{ill|Muromachi Shogunate|en|室町幕府}} implemented reforms mandating that someone from the rotation always be on duty in the palace. Violators faced confiscation of their estates<ref name="国史大辞典">Tanuma, "Kokushi Daijiten."</ref>. The {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|en|江戸幕府}} reinforced this in the {{ill|Kinchu Narabini Kuge Shohatto|en|禁中並公家諸法度}}, recognizing Koban as an essential duty alongside {{ill|Kagyo|en|家業}} and learning<ref name="日本史大事典"/>.
In September 1443 ({{ill|Kakitsu 3|en|嘉吉}}), during the {{ill|Rebellion of the Emperor's Mausoleum|en|禁闕の変}}, the palace was invaded by {{ill|Southern Court loyalists|en|後南朝}}, and young court nobles such as {{ill|Kanroji Chikanaga|en|甘露寺親長}} and {{ill|Yotsutsuji Tokiharu|en|四辻季春}} defended the {{ill|Emperor Go-Hanazono|en|後花園天皇}} by drawing swords and escorting him to safety{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=13}}.
In {{ill|Bunmei 8|en|文明}} (1476), Koban members were divided into "Uchi-uchi" (close attendants) and "Tazama" (outsiders){{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=156–157}}. Over time, these designations became hereditary and reflected a form of {{ill|Kuge House Rank|en|家格}}<ref name="日本歴史大事典"/><ref name=hayashi/>.
In {{ill|Kanbun 3|en|寛文}} (1661), {{ill|Retired Emperor Go-Mizunoo|en|後水尾法皇}} created a new rotation, "Oku no Ban," composed of 21 close attendants selected from the Uchi-uchi and Tazama groups. Initially, these members served double roles, but by {{ill|Kanbun 11|en|寛文}}, 1671, they were exempt from their original duties and formed the specialized "Kinjushu Koban" (Close Attendants)<ref>Hayashi Daiki, "On the Formation of Kinjushu Koban in the Early Modern Era," Ronshu Kinsei, Vol. 40, 2018.</ref>.
In {{ill|Keio 4|en|慶応}}, 1868, the distinctions between Uchi-uchi, Tazama, and Kinjushu were abolished, and the roles were consolidated under "Naiban-shu" (Inner Attendants)<ref name="日本史大事典"/><ref name="日本歴史大事典"/>.

In the {{ill|Edo period|en|江戸時代}}, {{ill|Emperor Go-Mizunoo|en|後水尾天皇}} established the age of 15 as the minimum for Koban duties, and the establishment of {{ill|Shinke|en|新家}} resolved personnel shortages. Non-official roles, such as {{ill|Oko|en|御児}} for miscellaneous tasks within the palace, were also fulfilled by appointees<ref>Hayashi Daiki, "The Role of 'Gojiko' in Early Modern Court Nobility," Jinbun, Issue 16, 2018; revised version in "Tenno Kinshin to Kinsei no Chotei" (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021), ISBN 978-4-642-04333-5, pp. 93–95.</ref>. Additionally, those serving the {{ill|Retired Emperor|en|太上天皇}} or the {{ill|Crown Prince|en|皇太子}} were known as "In-san-shu" or "Togu Koban" respectively<ref name=hayashi>Hayashi Daiki, "Introduction to Modern Court Studies and Objectives," in "Tenno Kinshin to Kinsei no Chotei" (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021), pp. 8–9.</ref>.

In response to circumstances, the overall number of groups could be reduced or temporary additions ({{ill|Kaban|en|加番}}) ordered to increase group sizes<ref name="日本史大事典"/>. Each group was led by a head ({{ill|Banto|en|番頭}}) and overseen by a {{ill|Koban Bugyo|en|小番奉行}}, often concurrently held by a {{ill|Buke Denso|en|武家伝奏}}<ref name="日本史大事典"/>.

The {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|en|江戸幕府}} reinforced this in the {{ill|Kinchu Narabini Kuge Shohatto|en|禁中並公家諸法度}}, recognizing Koban as an essential duty alongside {{ill|Kagyo|en|家業}} and learning<ref name="日本史大事典"/>.

In {{ill|Bunmei 8|en|文明}} (1476), Koban members were divided into "Uchi-uchi" (close attendants) and "Tazama" (outsiders){{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=156–157}}. Over time, these designations became hereditary and reflected a form of {{ill|Kuge House Rank|en|家格}}<ref name="日本歴史大事典"/><ref name=hayashi/>.
In {{ill|Kanbun 3|en|寛文}} (1661), {{ill|Retired Emperor Go-Mizunoo|en|後水尾法皇}} created a new rotation, "Oku no Ban," composed of 21 close attendants selected from the Uchi-uchi and Tazama groups. Initially, these members served double roles, but by {{ill|Kanbun 11|en|寛文}}, 1671, they were exempt from their original duties and formed the specialized "Kinjushu Koban" (Close Attendants)<ref>Hayashi Daiki, "On the Formation of Kinjushu Koban in the Early Modern Era," Ronshu Kinsei, Vol. 40, 2018.</ref>.
In {{ill|Keio 4|en|慶応}}, 1868, the distinctions between Uchi-uchi, Tazama, and Kinjushu were abolished, and the roles were consolidated under "Naiban-shu" (Inner Attendants)<ref name="日本史大事典"/><ref name="日本歴史大事典"/>.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|天皇}} * {{ill|Kinri|en|禁裏}} * {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|en|三種の神器}} * {{ill|Naishi-dokoro|en|内侍所}} * {{ill|Kuge|en|公家}}
== References == <references/>
== Bibliography == * Tanuma Mutsuo, "Kinri Koban," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten (Showa)|en|国史大辞典}}, Vol. 4, Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1984, ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3 * Mori Miwa, "Kinri Koban," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten|en|日本史大事典}}, Vol. 2, Heibonsha, 1993, ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4 * Honda Keiko, "Kinri Koban," in {{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|en|日本歴史大事典}}, Vol. 1, Shogakukan, 2001, ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6 * Nakamoto Makoto, {{ill|Court Rituals and Imperial Gagaku|en|宮廷の御神楽}}, Shinten-sha, 2016 * Nakamoto Makoto, {{ill|Why Kagura Survived the Onin War|en|なぜ神楽は応仁の乱を乗り越えられたのか}}, Shinten-sha, 2021

revision.text:
{{ページ番号|date=2021年8月}} '''Kinri Koban''' (禁裏小番) refers to the system, from the [[Middle Ages|middle ages]] through the [[Early modern period (Japan)|early modern period]], in which {{ill|Kuge|en|公家}} served in daily rotation ({{ill|Ban (Japanese system)|en|番}}) at the {{ill|Kinri|en|禁裏}} to attend and {{ill|Shukutoku|en|宿直}} for the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|天皇}}.
They were tasked with guarding the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|天皇}} and the {{ill|Sacred Mirror|en|神鏡}}, located at the {{ill|Naishi-dokoro|en|内侍所}} {{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=160}}.
Court nobles, excluding those from the {{ill|Sekkan family|en|摂関家}}, served in this role from the age of 15 after {{ill|Genpuku|en|元服}} until 60 (although those with busy official duties or longstanding contributions were sometimes exempted by imperial decree)<ref name="日本史大事典">Mori, "Nihonshi Daijiten."</ref><ref name="日本歴史大事典">Honda, "Nihon Rekishi Daijiten."</ref>. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|戦国時代 (日本)}}, due to manpower shortages caused by the relocation of nobles to rural areas and the extinction of some families, there were cases where those younger than 15 performed these duties. In the {{ill|Edo period|en|江戸時代}}, {{ill|Emperor Go-Mizunoo|en|後水尾天皇}} established the age of 15 as the minimum for Koban duties, and the establishment of {{ill|Shinke|en|新家}} resolved personnel shortages. Non-official roles, such as {{ill|Oko|en|御児}} for miscellaneous tasks within the palace, were also fulfilled by appointees<ref>Hayashi Daiki, "The Role of 'Gojiko' in Early Modern Court Nobility," Jinbun, Issue 16, 2018; revised version in "Tenno Kinshin to Kinsei no Chotei" (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021), ISBN 978-4-642-04333-5, pp. 93–95.</ref>. In cases where fathers and sons (the family head and successor) served concurrently, sons were granted {{ill|Horyo|en|方領}} (land as compensation)<ref name="日本歴史大事典"/>. Additionally, those serving the {{ill|Retired Emperor|en|太上天皇}} or the {{ill|Crown Prince|en|皇太子}} were known as "In-san-shu" or "Togu Koban" respectively<ref name=hayashi>Hayashi Daiki, "Introduction to Modern Court Studies and Objectives," in "Tenno Kinshin to Kinsei no Chotei" (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021), pp. 8–9.</ref>.
The system varied over time, with rotations organized into groups of five to seven (e.g., five, six, or ten groups). In response to circumstances, the overall number of groups could be reduced or temporary additions ({{ill|Kaban|en|加番}}) ordered to increase group sizes<ref name="日本史大事典"/>. Each group was led by a head ({{ill|Banto|en|番頭}}) and overseen by a {{ill|Koban Bugyo|en|小番奉行}}, often concurrently held by a {{ill|Buke Denso|en|武家伝奏}}<ref name="日本史大事典"/>.
The term "Kinri Koban" was established during the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|室町時代}} (although the system itself predates this). Economic hardships during this time led to many neglecting their duties. To address this, in {{ill|Eikyo 2|en|永享}}, 1430, {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshinori|en|足利義教}} of the {{ill|Muromachi Shogunate|en|室町幕府}} implemented reforms mandating that someone from the rotation always be on duty in the palace. Violators faced confiscation of their estates<ref name="国史大辞典">Tanuma, "Kokushi Daijiten."</ref>. The {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|en|江戸幕府}} reinforced this in the {{ill|Kinchu Narabini Kuge Shohatto|en|禁中並公家諸法度}}, recognizing Koban as an essential duty alongside {{ill|Kagyo|en|家業}} and learning<ref name="日本史大事典"/>.
In September 1443 ({{ill|Kakitsu 3|en|嘉吉}}), during the {{ill|Rebellion of the Emperor's Mausoleum|en|禁闕の変}}, the palace was invaded by {{ill|Southern Court loyalists|en|後南朝}}, and young court nobles such as {{ill|Kanroji Chikanaga|en|甘露寺親長}} and {{ill|Yotsutsuji Tokiharu|en|四辻季春}} defended the {{ill|Emperor Go-Hanazono|en|後花園天皇}} by drawing swords and escorting him to safety{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=13}}.
In {{ill|Bunmei 8|en|文明}} (1476), Koban members were divided into "Uchi-uchi" (close attendants) and "Tazama" (outsiders){{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=156–157}}. Over time, these designations became hereditary and reflected a form of {{ill|Kuge House Rank|en|家格}}<ref name="日本歴史大事典"/><ref name=hayashi/>.
In {{ill|Kanbun 3|en|寛文}} (1661), {{ill|Retired Emperor Go-Mizunoo|en|後水尾法皇}} created a new rotation, "Oku no Ban," composed of 21 close attendants selected from the Uchi-uchi and Tazama groups. Initially, these members served double roles, but by {{ill|Kanbun 11|en|寛文}}, 1671, they were exempt from their original duties and formed the specialized "Kinjushu Koban" (Close Attendants)<ref>Hayashi Daiki, "On the Formation of Kinjushu Koban in the Early Modern Era," Ronshu Kinsei, Vol. 40, 2018.</ref>.
In {{ill|Keio 4|en|慶応}}, 1868, the distinctions between Uchi-uchi, Tazama, and Kinjushu were abolished, and the roles were consolidated under "Naiban-shu" (Inner Attendants)<ref name="日本史大事典"/><ref name="日本歴史大事典"/>.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|天皇}} * {{ill|Kinri|en|禁裏}} * {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|en|三種の神器}} * {{ill|Naishi-dokoro|en|内侍所}} * {{ill|Kuge|en|公家}}
== References == <references/>
== Bibliography == * Tanuma Mutsuo, "Kinri Koban," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten (Showa)|en|国史大辞典}}, Vol. 4, Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1984, ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3 * Mori Miwa, "Kinri Koban," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten|en|日本史大事典}}, Vol. 2, Heibonsha, 1993, ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4 * Honda Keiko, "Kinri Koban," in {{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|en|日本歴史大事典}}, Vol. 1, Shogakukan, 2001, ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6 * Nakamoto Makoto, {{ill|Court Rituals and Imperial Gagaku|en|宮廷の御神楽}}, Shinten-sha, 2016 * Nakamoto Makoto, {{ill|Why Kagura Survived the Onin War|en|なぜ神楽は応仁の乱を乗り越えられたのか}}, Shinten-sha, 2021
