[[Category:Kinri Koban]][[Category:Kinri Koban]] [[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[ja:禁裏小番]]

{{ページ番号|date=2021年8月}} '''Kinri Koban''' (禁裏小番) refers to the system, from the [[Middle Ages|middle ages]] through the [[Early modern period (Japan)|early modern period]], in which {{ill|Kuge|en|公家}} served in daily rotation ({{ill|Ban (Japanese system)|en|番}}) at the {{ill|Kinri|en|禁裏}} to attend and {{ill|Shukutoku|en|宿直}} for the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|天皇}}.
They were tasked with guarding the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|天皇}} and the {{ill|Sacred Mirror|en|神鏡}}, located at the {{ill|Naishi-dokoro|en|内侍所}} {{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=160}}.
Court nobles, excluding those from the {{ill|Sekkan family|en|摂関家}}, served in this role from the age of 15 after {{ill|Genpuku|en|元服}} until 60 (although those with busy official duties or longstanding contributions were sometimes exempted by imperial decree)<ref name="日本史大事典">Mori, "Nihonshi Daijiten."</ref><ref name="日本歴史大事典">Honda, "Nihon Rekishi Daijiten."</ref>. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|戦国時代 (日本)}}, due to manpower shortages caused by the relocation of nobles to rural areas and the extinction of some families, there were cases where those younger than 15 performed these duties. In the {{ill|Edo period|en|江戸時代}}, {{ill|Emperor Go-Mizunoo|en|後水尾天皇}} established the age of 15 as the minimum for Koban duties, and the establishment of {{ill|Shinke|en|新家}} resolved personnel shortages. Non-official roles, such as {{ill|Oko|en|御児}} for miscellaneous tasks within the palace, were also fulfilled by appointees<ref>Hayashi Daiki, "The Role of 'Gojiko' in Early Modern Court Nobility," Jinbun, Issue 16, 2018; revised version in "Tenno Kinshin to Kinsei no Chotei" (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021), ISBN 978-4-642-04333-5, pp. 93–95.</ref>. In cases where fathers and sons (the family head and successor) served concurrently, sons were granted {{ill|Horyo|en|方領}} (land as compensation)<ref name="日本歴史大事典"/>. Additionally, those serving the {{ill|Retired Emperor|en|太上天皇}} or the {{ill|Crown Prince|en|皇太子}} were known as "In-san-shu" or "Togu Koban" respectively<ref name=hayashi>Hayashi Daiki, "Introduction to Modern Court Studies and Objectives," in "Tenno Kinshin to Kinsei no Chotei" (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021), pp. 8–9.</ref>.
The system varied over time, with rotations organized into groups of five to seven (e.g., five, six, or ten groups). In response to circumstances, the overall number of groups could be reduced or temporary additions ({{ill|Kaban|en|加番}}) ordered to increase group sizes<ref name="日本史大事典"/>. Each group was led by a head ({{ill|Banto|en|番頭}}) and overseen by a {{ill|Koban Bugyo|en|小番奉行}}, often concurrently held by a {{ill|Buke Denso|en|武家伝奏}}<ref name="日本史大事典"/>.
The term "Kinri Koban" was established during the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|室町時代}} (although the system itself predates this). Economic hardships during this time led to many neglecting their duties. To address this, in {{ill|Eikyo 2|en|永享}}, 1430, {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshinori|en|足利義教}} of the {{ill|Muromachi Shogunate|en|室町幕府}} implemented reforms mandating that someone from the rotation always be on duty in the palace. Violators faced confiscation of their estates<ref name="国史大辞典">Tanuma, "Kokushi Daijiten."</ref>. The {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|en|江戸幕府}} reinforced this in the {{ill|Kinchu Narabini Kuge Shohatto|en|禁中並公家諸法度}}, recognizing Koban as an essential duty alongside {{ill|Kagyo|en|家業}} and learning<ref name="日本史大事典"/>.
In September 1443 ({{ill|Kakitsu 3|en|嘉吉}}), during the {{ill|Rebellion of the Emperor's Mausoleum|en|禁闕の変}}, the palace was invaded by {{ill|Southern Court loyalists|en|後南朝}}, and young court nobles such as {{ill|Kanroji Chikanaga|en|甘露寺親長}} and {{ill|Yotsutsuji Tokiharu|en|四辻季春}} defended the {{ill|Emperor Go-Hanazono|en|後花園天皇}} by drawing swords and escorting him to safety{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=13}}.
In {{ill|Bunmei 8|en|文明}} (1476), Koban members were divided into "Uchi-uchi" (close attendants) and "Tazama" (outsiders){{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=156–157}}. Over time, these designations became hereditary and reflected a form of {{ill|Kuge House Rank|en|家格}}<ref name="日本歴史大事典"/><ref name=hayashi/>.
In {{ill|Kanbun 3|en|寛文}} (1661), {{ill|Retired Emperor Go-Mizunoo|en|後水尾法皇}} created a new rotation, "Oku no Ban," composed of 21 close attendants selected from the Uchi-uchi and Tazama groups. Initially, these members served double roles, but by {{ill|Kanbun 11|en|寛文}}, 1671, they were exempt from their original duties and formed the specialized "Kinjushu Koban" (Close Attendants)<ref>Hayashi Daiki, "On the Formation of Kinjushu Koban in the Early Modern Era," Ronshu Kinsei, Vol. 40, 2018.</ref>.
In {{ill|Keio 4|en|慶応}}, 1868, the distinctions between Uchi-uchi, Tazama, and Kinjushu were abolished, and the roles were consolidated under "Naiban-shu" (Inner Attendants)<ref name="日本史大事典"/><ref name="日本歴史大事典"/>.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|天皇}} * {{ill|Kinri|en|禁裏}} * {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|en|三種の神器}} * {{ill|Naishi-dokoro|en|内侍所}} * {{ill|Kuge|en|公家}}
== References == <references/>
== Bibliography == * Tanuma Mutsuo, "Kinri Koban," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten (Showa)|en|国史大辞典}}, Vol. 4, Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1984, ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3 * Mori Miwa, "Kinri Koban," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten|en|日本史大事典}}, Vol. 2, Heibonsha, 1993, ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4 * Honda Keiko, "Kinri Koban," in {{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|en|日本歴史大事典}}, Vol. 1, Shogakukan, 2001, ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6 * Nakamoto Makoto, {{ill|Court Rituals and Imperial Gagaku|en|宮廷の御神楽}}, Shinten-sha, 2016 * Nakamoto Makoto, {{ill|Why Kagura Survived the Onin War|en|なぜ神楽は応仁の乱を乗り越えられたのか}}, Shinten-sha, 2021

Non-official roles, such as {{ill|Oko|en|御児}} for miscellaneous tasks within the palace, were also fulfilled by appointees<ref>Hayashi Daiki, "The Role of 'Gojiko' in Early Modern Court Nobility," Jinbun, Issue 16, 2018; revised version in "Tenno Kinshin to Kinsei no Chotei" (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021), ISBN 978-4-642-04333-5, pp. 93–95.</ref>. In cases where fathers and sons (the family head and successor) served concurrently, sons were granted {{ill|Horyo|en|方領}} (land as compensation)<ref name="日本歴史大事典"/>. Additionally, those serving the {{ill|Retired Emperor|en|太上天皇}} or the {{ill|Crown Prince|en|皇太子}} were known as "In-san-shu" or "Togu Koban" respectively<ref name=hayashi>Hayashi Daiki, "Introduction to Modern Court Studies and Objectives," in "Tenno Kinshin to Kinsei no Chotei" (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021), pp. 8–9.</ref>.

{{ページ番号|date=2021年8月}} '''Kinri Koban''' (禁裏小番) refers to the system, from the [[Middle Ages|middle ages]] through the [[Early modern period (Japan)|early modern period]], in which {{ill|Kuge|en|公家}} served in daily rotation ({{ill|Ban (Japanese system)|en|番}}) at the {{ill|Kinri|en|禁裏}} to attend and {{ill|Shukutoku|en|宿直}} for the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|天皇}}.
They were tasked with guarding the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|天皇}} and the {{ill|Sacred Mirror|en|神鏡}}, located at the {{ill|Naishi-dokoro|en|内侍所}} {{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=160}}.
Court nobles, excluding those from the {{ill|Sekkan family|en|摂関家}}, served in this role from the age of 15 after {{ill|Genpuku|en|元服}} until 60 (although those with busy official duties or longstanding contributions were sometimes exempted by imperial decree)<ref name="日本史大事典">Mori, "Nihonshi Daijiten."</ref><ref name="日本歴史大事典">Honda, "Nihon Rekishi Daijiten."</ref>. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|戦国時代 (日本)}}, due to manpower shortages caused by the relocation of nobles to rural areas and the extinction of some families, there were cases where those younger than 15 performed these duties. In the {{ill|Edo period|en|江戸時代}}, {{ill|Emperor Go-Mizunoo|en|後水尾天皇}} established the age of 15 as the minimum for Koban duties, and the establishment of {{ill|Shinke|en|新家}} resolved personnel shortages. Non-official roles, such as {{ill|Oko|en|御児}} for miscellaneous tasks within the palace, were also fulfilled by appointees<ref>Hayashi Daiki, "The Role of 'Gojiko' in Early Modern Court Nobility," Jinbun, Issue 16, 2018; revised version in "Tenno Kinshin to Kinsei no Chotei" (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021), ISBN 978-4-642-04333-5, pp. 93–95.</ref>. In cases where fathers and sons (the family head and successor) served concurrently, sons were granted {{ill|Horyo|en|方領}} (land as compensation)<ref name="日本歴史大事典"/>. Additionally, those serving the {{ill|Retired Emperor|en|太上天皇}} or the {{ill|Crown Prince|en|皇太子}} were known as "In-san-shu" or "Togu Koban" respectively<ref name=hayashi>Hayashi Daiki, "Introduction to Modern Court Studies and Objectives," in "Tenno Kinshin to Kinsei no Chotei" (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021), pp. 8–9.</ref>.
The system varied over time, with rotations organized into groups of five to seven (e.g., five, six, or ten groups). In response to circumstances, the overall number of groups could be reduced or temporary additions ({{ill|Kaban|en|加番}}) ordered to increase group sizes<ref name="日本史大事典"/>. Each group was led by a head ({{ill|Banto|en|番頭}}) and overseen by a {{ill|Koban Bugyo|en|小番奉行}}, often concurrently held by a {{ill|Buke Denso|en|武家伝奏}}<ref name="日本史大事典"/>.
The term "Kinri Koban" was established during the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|室町時代}} (although the system itself predates this). Economic hardships during this time led to many neglecting their duties. To address this, in {{ill|Eikyo 2|en|永享}}, 1430, {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshinori|en|足利義教}} of the {{ill|Muromachi Shogunate|en|室町幕府}} implemented reforms mandating that someone from the rotation always be on duty in the palace. Violators faced confiscation of their estates<ref name="国史大辞典">Tanuma, "Kokushi Daijiten."</ref>. The {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|en|江戸幕府}} reinforced this in the {{ill|Kinchu Narabini Kuge Shohatto|en|禁中並公家諸法度}}, recognizing Koban as an essential duty alongside {{ill|Kagyo|en|家業}} and learning<ref name="日本史大事典"/>.
In September 1443 ({{ill|Kakitsu 3|en|嘉吉}}), during the {{ill|Rebellion of the Emperor's Mausoleum|en|禁闕の変}}, the palace was invaded by {{ill|Southern Court loyalists|en|後南朝}}, and young court nobles such as {{ill|Kanroji Chikanaga|en|甘露寺親長}} and {{ill|Yotsutsuji Tokiharu|en|四辻季春}} defended the {{ill|Emperor Go-Hanazono|en|後花園天皇}} by drawing swords and escorting him to safety{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=13}}.
In {{ill|Bunmei 8|en|文明}} (1476), Koban members were divided into "Uchi-uchi" (close attendants) and "Tazama" (outsiders){{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=156–157}}. Over time, these designations became hereditary and reflected a form of {{ill|Kuge House Rank|en|家格}}<ref name="日本歴史大事典"/><ref name=hayashi/>.
In {{ill|Kanbun 3|en|寛文}} (1661), {{ill|Retired Emperor Go-Mizunoo|en|後水尾法皇}} created a new rotation, "Oku no Ban," composed of 21 close attendants selected from the Uchi-uchi and Tazama groups. Initially, these members served double roles, but by {{ill|Kanbun 11|en|寛文}}, 1671, they were exempt from their original duties and formed the specialized "Kinjushu Koban" (Close Attendants)<ref>Hayashi Daiki, "On the Formation of Kinjushu Koban in the Early Modern Era," Ronshu Kinsei, Vol. 40, 2018.</ref>.
In {{ill|Keio 4|en|慶応}}, 1868, the distinctions between Uchi-uchi, Tazama, and Kinjushu were abolished, and the roles were consolidated under "Naiban-shu" (Inner Attendants)<ref name="日本史大事典"/><ref name="日本歴史大事典"/>.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|天皇}} * {{ill|Kinri|en|禁裏}} * {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|en|三種の神器}} * {{ill|Naishi-dokoro|en|内侍所}} * {{ill|Kuge|en|公家}}
== References == <references/>
== Bibliography == * Tanuma Mutsuo, "Kinri Koban," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten (Showa)|en|国史大辞典}}, Vol. 4, Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1984, ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3 * Mori Miwa, "Kinri Koban," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten|en|日本史大事典}}, Vol. 2, Heibonsha, 1993, ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4 * Honda Keiko, "Kinri Koban," in {{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|en|日本歴史大事典}}, Vol. 1, Shogakukan, 2001, ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6 * Nakamoto Makoto, {{ill|Court Rituals and Imperial Gagaku|en|宮廷の御神楽}}, Shinten-sha, 2016 * Nakamoto Makoto, {{ill|Why Kagura Survived the Onin War|en|なぜ神楽は応仁の乱を乗り越えられたのか}}, Shinten-sha, 2021

{{ページ番号|date=2021年8月}} '''Kinri Koban''' (禁裏小番) refers to the system, from the [[Middle Ages|middle ages]] through the [[Early modern period (Japan)|early modern period]], in which {{ill|Kuge|en|公家}} served in daily rotation ({{ill|Ban (Japanese system)|en|番}}) at the {{ill|Kinri|en|禁裏}} to attend and {{ill|Shukutoku|en|宿直}} for the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|天皇}}.
They were tasked with guarding the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|天皇}} and the {{ill|Sacred Mirror|en|神鏡}}, located at the {{ill|Naishi-dokoro|en|内侍所}} {{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=160}}.
Court nobles, excluding those from the {{ill|Sekkan family|en|摂関家}}, served in this role from the age of 15 after {{ill|Genpuku|en|元服}} until 60 (although those with busy official duties or longstanding contributions were sometimes exempted by imperial decree)<ref name="日本史大事典">Mori, "Nihonshi Daijiten."</ref><ref name="日本歴史大事典">Honda, "Nihon Rekishi Daijiten."</ref>. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|戦国時代 (日本)}}, due to manpower shortages caused by the relocation of nobles to rural areas and the extinction of some families, there were cases where those younger than 15 performed these duties. In the {{ill|Edo period|en|江戸時代}}, {{ill|Emperor Go-Mizunoo|en|後水尾天皇}} established the age of 15 as the minimum for Koban duties, and the establishment of {{ill|Shinke|en|新家}} resolved personnel shortages. Non-official roles, such as {{ill|Oko|en|御児}} for miscellaneous tasks within the palace, were also fulfilled by appointees<ref>Hayashi Daiki, "The Role of 'Gojiko' in Early Modern Court Nobility," Jinbun, Issue 16, 2018; revised version in "Tenno Kinshin to Kinsei no Chotei" (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021), ISBN 978-4-642-04333-5, pp. 93–95.</ref>. In cases where fathers and sons (the family head and successor) served concurrently, sons were granted {{ill|Horyo|en|方領}} (land as compensation)<ref name="日本歴史大事典"/>. Additionally, those serving the {{ill|Retired Emperor|en|太上天皇}} or the {{ill|Crown Prince|en|皇太子}} were known as "In-san-shu" or "Togu Koban" respectively<ref name=hayashi>Hayashi Daiki, "Introduction to Modern Court Studies and Objectives," in "Tenno Kinshin to Kinsei no Chotei" (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021), pp. 8–9.</ref>.
The system varied over time, with rotations organized into groups of five to seven (e.g., five, six, or ten groups). In response to circumstances, the overall number of groups could be reduced or temporary additions ({{ill|Kaban|en|加番}}) ordered to increase group sizes<ref name="日本史大事典"/>. Each group was led by a head ({{ill|Banto|en|番頭}}) and overseen by a {{ill|Koban Bugyo|en|小番奉行}}, often concurrently held by a {{ill|Buke Denso|en|武家伝奏}}<ref name="日本史大事典"/>.
The term "Kinri Koban" was established during the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|室町時代}} (although the system itself predates this). Economic hardships during this time led to many neglecting their duties. To address this, in {{ill|Eikyo 2|en|永享}}, 1430, {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshinori|en|足利義教}} of the {{ill|Muromachi Shogunate|en|室町幕府}} implemented reforms mandating that someone from the rotation always be on duty in the palace. Violators faced confiscation of their estates<ref name="国史大辞典">Tanuma, "Kokushi Daijiten."</ref>. The {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|en|江戸幕府}} reinforced this in the {{ill|Kinchu Narabini Kuge Shohatto|en|禁中並公家諸法度}}, recognizing Koban as an essential duty alongside {{ill|Kagyo|en|家業}} and learning<ref name="日本史大事典"/>.
In September 1443 ({{ill|Kakitsu 3|en|嘉吉}}), during the {{ill|Rebellion of the Emperor's Mausoleum|en|禁闕の変}}, the palace was invaded by {{ill|Southern Court loyalists|en|後南朝}}, and young court nobles such as {{ill|Kanroji Chikanaga|en|甘露寺親長}} and {{ill|Yotsutsuji Tokiharu|en|四辻季春}} defended the {{ill|Emperor Go-Hanazono|en|後花園天皇}} by drawing swords and escorting him to safety{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=13}}.

Initially, these members served double roles, but by {{ill|Kanbun 11|en|寛文}}, 1671, they were exempt from their original duties and formed the specialized "Kinjushu Koban" (Close Attendants)<ref>Hayashi Daiki, "On the Formation of Kinjushu Koban in the Early Modern Era," Ronshu Kinsei, Vol. 40, 2018.</ref>.

Non-official roles, such as {{ill|Oko|en|御児}} for miscellaneous tasks within the palace, were also fulfilled by appointees<ref>Hayashi Daiki, "The Role of 'Gojiko' in Early Modern Court Nobility," Jinbun, Issue 16, 2018; revised version in "Tenno Kinshin to Kinsei no Chotei" (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021), ISBN 978-4-642-04333-5, pp. 93–95.</ref>. In cases where fathers and sons (the family head and successor) served concurrently, sons were granted {{ill|Horyo|en|方領}} (land as compensation)<ref name="日本歴史大事典"/>. Additionally, those serving the {{ill|Retired Emperor|en|太上天皇}} or the {{ill|Crown Prince|en|皇太子}} were known as "In-san-shu" or "Togu Koban" respectively<ref name=hayashi>Hayashi Daiki, "Introduction to Modern Court Studies and Objectives," in "Tenno Kinshin to Kinsei no Chotei" (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021), pp. 8–9.</ref>.
The system varied over time, with rotations organized into groups of five to seven (e.g., five, six, or ten groups). In response to circumstances, the overall number of groups could be reduced or temporary additions ({{ill|Kaban|en|加番}}) ordered to increase group sizes<ref name="日本史大事典"/>. Each group was led by a head ({{ill|Banto|en|番頭}}) and overseen by a {{ill|Koban Bugyo|en|小番奉行}}, often concurrently held by a {{ill|Buke Denso|en|武家伝奏}}<ref name="日本史大事典"/>.
The term "Kinri Koban" was established during the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|室町時代}} (although the system itself predates this). Economic hardships during this time led to many neglecting their duties. To address this, in {{ill|Eikyo 2|en|永享}}, 1430, {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshinori|en|足利義教}} of the {{ill|Muromachi Shogunate|en|室町幕府}} implemented reforms mandating that someone from the rotation always be on duty in the palace. Violators faced confiscation of their estates<ref name="国史大辞典">Tanuma, "Kokushi Daijiten."</ref>. The {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|en|江戸幕府}} reinforced this in the {{ill|Kinchu Narabini Kuge Shohatto|en|禁中並公家諸法度}}, recognizing Koban as an essential duty alongside {{ill|Kagyo|en|家業}} and learning<ref name="日本史大事典"/>.
In September 1443 ({{ill|Kakitsu 3|en|嘉吉}}), during the {{ill|Rebellion of the Emperor's Mausoleum|en|禁闕の変}}, the palace was invaded by {{ill|Southern Court loyalists|en|後南朝}}, and young court nobles such as {{ill|Kanroji Chikanaga|en|甘露寺親長}} and {{ill|Yotsutsuji Tokiharu|en|四辻季春}} defended the {{ill|Emperor Go-Hanazono|en|後花園天皇}} by drawing swords and escorting him to safety{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=13}}.
In {{ill|Bunmei 8|en|文明}} (1476), Koban members were divided into "Uchi-uchi" (close attendants) and "Tazama" (outsiders){{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=156–157}}. Over time, these designations became hereditary and reflected a form of {{ill|Kuge House Rank|en|家格}}<ref name="日本歴史大事典"/><ref name=hayashi/>.
In {{ill|Kanbun 3|en|寛文}} (1661), {{ill|Retired Emperor Go-Mizunoo|en|後水尾法皇}} created a new rotation, "Oku no Ban," composed of 21 close attendants selected from the Uchi-uchi and Tazama groups. Initially, these members served double roles, but by {{ill|Kanbun 11|en|寛文}}, 1671, they were exempt from their original duties and formed the specialized "Kinjushu Koban" (Close Attendants)<ref>Hayashi Daiki, "On the Formation of Kinjushu Koban in the Early Modern Era," Ronshu Kinsei, Vol. 40, 2018.</ref>.
In {{ill|Keio 4|en|慶応}}, 1868, the distinctions between Uchi-uchi, Tazama, and Kinjushu were abolished, and the roles were consolidated under "Naiban-shu" (Inner Attendants)<ref name="日本史大事典"/><ref name="日本歴史大事典"/>.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|天皇}} * {{ill|Kinri|en|禁裏}} * {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|en|三種の神器}} * {{ill|Naishi-dokoro|en|内侍所}} * {{ill|Kuge|en|公家}}
== References == <references/>
== Bibliography == * Tanuma Mutsuo, "Kinri Koban," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten (Showa)|en|国史大辞典}}, Vol. 4, Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1984, ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3 * Mori Miwa, "Kinri Koban," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten|en|日本史大事典}}, Vol. 2, Heibonsha, 1993, ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4 * Honda Keiko, "Kinri Koban," in {{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|en|日本歴史大事典}}, Vol. 1, Shogakukan, 2001, ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6 * Nakamoto Makoto, {{ill|Court Rituals and Imperial Gagaku|en|宮廷の御神楽}}, Shinten-sha, 2016 * Nakamoto Makoto, {{ill|Why Kagura Survived the Onin War|en|なぜ神楽は応仁の乱を乗り越えられたのか}}, Shinten-sha, 2021

revision.text:
{{ページ番号|date=2021年8月}} '''Kinri Koban''' (禁裏小番) refers to the system, from the [[Middle Ages|middle ages]] through the [[Early modern period (Japan)|early modern period]], in which {{ill|Kuge|en|公家}} served in daily rotation ({{ill|Ban (Japanese system)|en|番}}) at the {{ill|Kinri|en|禁裏}} to attend and {{ill|Shukutoku|en|宿直}} for the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|天皇}}.
They were tasked with guarding the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|天皇}} and the {{ill|Sacred Mirror|en|神鏡}}, located at the {{ill|Naishi-dokoro|en|内侍所}} {{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=160}}.
Court nobles, excluding those from the {{ill|Sekkan family|en|摂関家}}, served in this role from the age of 15 after {{ill|Genpuku|en|元服}} until 60 (although those with busy official duties or longstanding contributions were sometimes exempted by imperial decree)<ref name="日本史大事典">Mori, "Nihonshi Daijiten."</ref><ref name="日本歴史大事典">Honda, "Nihon Rekishi Daijiten."</ref>. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|戦国時代 (日本)}}, due to manpower shortages caused by the relocation of nobles to rural areas and the extinction of some families, there were cases where those younger than 15 performed these duties. In the {{ill|Edo period|en|江戸時代}}, {{ill|Emperor Go-Mizunoo|en|後水尾天皇}} established the age of 15 as the minimum for Koban duties, and the establishment of {{ill|Shinke|en|新家}} resolved personnel shortages. Non-official roles, such as {{ill|Oko|en|御児}} for miscellaneous tasks within the palace, were also fulfilled by appointees<ref>Hayashi Daiki, "The Role of 'Gojiko' in Early Modern Court Nobility," Jinbun, Issue 16, 2018; revised version in "Tenno Kinshin to Kinsei no Chotei" (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021), ISBN 978-4-642-04333-5, pp. 93–95.</ref>. In cases where fathers and sons (the family head and successor) served concurrently, sons were granted {{ill|Horyo|en|方領}} (land as compensation)<ref name="日本歴史大事典"/>. Additionally, those serving the {{ill|Retired Emperor|en|太上天皇}} or the {{ill|Crown Prince|en|皇太子}} were known as "In-san-shu" or "Togu Koban" respectively<ref name=hayashi>Hayashi Daiki, "Introduction to Modern Court Studies and Objectives," in "Tenno Kinshin to Kinsei no Chotei" (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021), pp. 8–9.</ref>.
The system varied over time, with rotations organized into groups of five to seven (e.g., five, six, or ten groups). In response to circumstances, the overall number of groups could be reduced or temporary additions ({{ill|Kaban|en|加番}}) ordered to increase group sizes<ref name="日本史大事典"/>. Each group was led by a head ({{ill|Banto|en|番頭}}) and overseen by a {{ill|Koban Bugyo|en|小番奉行}}, often concurrently held by a {{ill|Buke Denso|en|武家伝奏}}<ref name="日本史大事典"/>.
The term "Kinri Koban" was established during the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|室町時代}} (although the system itself predates this). Economic hardships during this time led to many neglecting their duties. To address this, in {{ill|Eikyo 2|en|永享}}, 1430, {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshinori|en|足利義教}} of the {{ill|Muromachi Shogunate|en|室町幕府}} implemented reforms mandating that someone from the rotation always be on duty in the palace. Violators faced confiscation of their estates<ref name="国史大辞典">Tanuma, "Kokushi Daijiten."</ref>. The {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|en|江戸幕府}} reinforced this in the {{ill|Kinchu Narabini Kuge Shohatto|en|禁中並公家諸法度}}, recognizing Koban as an essential duty alongside {{ill|Kagyo|en|家業}} and learning<ref name="日本史大事典"/>.
In September 1443 ({{ill|Kakitsu 3|en|嘉吉}}), during the {{ill|Rebellion of the Emperor's Mausoleum|en|禁闕の変}}, the palace was invaded by {{ill|Southern Court loyalists|en|後南朝}}, and young court nobles such as {{ill|Kanroji Chikanaga|en|甘露寺親長}} and {{ill|Yotsutsuji Tokiharu|en|四辻季春}} defended the {{ill|Emperor Go-Hanazono|en|後花園天皇}} by drawing swords and escorting him to safety{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=13}}.
In {{ill|Bunmei 8|en|文明}} (1476), Koban members were divided into "Uchi-uchi" (close attendants) and "Tazama" (outsiders){{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=156–157}}. Over time, these designations became hereditary and reflected a form of {{ill|Kuge House Rank|en|家格}}<ref name="日本歴史大事典"/><ref name=hayashi/>.
In {{ill|Kanbun 3|en|寛文}} (1661), {{ill|Retired Emperor Go-Mizunoo|en|後水尾法皇}} created a new rotation, "Oku no Ban," composed of 21 close attendants selected from the Uchi-uchi and Tazama groups. Initially, these members served double roles, but by {{ill|Kanbun 11|en|寛文}}, 1671, they were exempt from their original duties and formed the specialized "Kinjushu Koban" (Close Attendants)<ref>Hayashi Daiki, "On the Formation of Kinjushu Koban in the Early Modern Era," Ronshu Kinsei, Vol. 40, 2018.</ref>.
In {{ill|Keio 4|en|慶応}}, 1868, the distinctions between Uchi-uchi, Tazama, and Kinjushu were abolished, and the roles were consolidated under "Naiban-shu" (Inner Attendants)<ref name="日本史大事典"/><ref name="日本歴史大事典"/>.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|天皇}} * {{ill|Kinri|en|禁裏}} * {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|en|三種の神器}} * {{ill|Naishi-dokoro|en|内侍所}} * {{ill|Kuge|en|公家}}
== References == <references/>
== Bibliography == * Tanuma Mutsuo, "Kinri Koban," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten (Showa)|en|国史大辞典}}, Vol. 4, Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1984, ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3 * Mori Miwa, "Kinri Koban," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten|en|日本史大事典}}, Vol. 2, Heibonsha, 1993, ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4 * Honda Keiko, "Kinri Koban," in {{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|en|日本歴史大事典}}, Vol. 1, Shogakukan, 2001, ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6 * Nakamoto Makoto, {{ill|Court Rituals and Imperial Gagaku|en|宮廷の御神楽}}, Shinten-sha, 2016 * Nakamoto Makoto, {{ill|Why Kagura Survived the Onin War|en|なぜ神楽は応仁の乱を乗り越えられたのか}}, Shinten-sha, 2021

{{ページ番号|date=2021年8月}} '''Kinri Koban''' (禁裏小番) refers to the system, from the [[Middle Ages|middle ages]] through the [[Early modern period (Japan)|early modern period]], in which {{ill|Kuge|en|公家}} served in daily rotation ({{ill|Ban (Japanese system)|en|番}}) at the {{ill|Kinri|en|禁裏}} to attend and {{ill|Shukutoku|en|宿直}} for the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|天皇}}.
They were tasked with guarding the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|天皇}} and the {{ill|Sacred Mirror|en|神鏡}}, located at the {{ill|Naishi-dokoro|en|内侍所}} {{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=160}}.
Court nobles, excluding those from the {{ill|Sekkan family|en|摂関家}}, served in this role from the age of 15 after {{ill|Genpuku|en|元服}} until 60 (although those with busy official duties or longstanding contributions were sometimes exempted by imperial decree)<ref name="日本史大事典">Mori, "Nihonshi Daijiten."</ref><ref name="日本歴史大事典">Honda, "Nihon Rekishi Daijiten."</ref>. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|戦国時代 (日本)}}, due to manpower shortages caused by the relocation of nobles to rural areas and the extinction of some families, there were cases where those younger than 15 performed these duties. In the {{ill|Edo period|en|江戸時代}}, {{ill|Emperor Go-Mizunoo|en|後水尾天皇}} established the age of 15 as the minimum for Koban duties, and the establishment of {{ill|Shinke|en|新家}} resolved personnel shortages. Non-official roles, such as {{ill|Oko|en|御児}} for miscellaneous tasks within the palace, were also fulfilled by appointees<ref>Hayashi Daiki, "The Role of 'Gojiko' in Early Modern Court Nobility," Jinbun, Issue 16, 2018; revised version in "Tenno Kinshin to Kinsei no Chotei" (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021), ISBN 978-4-642-04333-5, pp. 93–95.</ref>. In cases where fathers and sons (the family head and successor) served concurrently, sons were granted {{ill|Horyo|en|方領}} (land as compensation)<ref name="日本歴史大事典"/>. Additionally, those serving the {{ill|Retired Emperor|en|太上天皇}} or the {{ill|Crown Prince|en|皇太子}} were known as "In-san-shu" or "Togu Koban" respectively<ref name=hayashi>Hayashi Daiki, "Introduction to Modern Court Studies and Objectives," in "Tenno Kinshin to Kinsei no Chotei" (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021), pp. 8–9.</ref>.
The system varied over time, with rotations organized into groups of five to seven (e.g., five, six, or ten groups). In response to circumstances, the overall number of groups could be reduced or temporary additions ({{ill|Kaban|en|加番}}) ordered to increase group sizes<ref name="日本史大事典"/>. Each group was led by a head ({{ill|Banto|en|番頭}}) and overseen by a {{ill|Koban Bugyo|en|小番奉行}}, often concurrently held by a {{ill|Buke Denso|en|武家伝奏}}<ref name="日本史大事典"/>.
The term "Kinri Koban" was established during the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|室町時代}} (although the system itself predates this). Economic hardships during this time led to many neglecting their duties. To address this, in {{ill|Eikyo 2|en|永享}}, 1430, {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshinori|en|足利義教}} of the {{ill|Muromachi Shogunate|en|室町幕府}} implemented reforms mandating that someone from the rotation always be on duty in the palace. Violators faced confiscation of their estates<ref name="国史大辞典">Tanuma, "Kokushi Daijiten."</ref>. The {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|en|江戸幕府}} reinforced this in the {{ill|Kinchu Narabini Kuge Shohatto|en|禁中並公家諸法度}}, recognizing Koban as an essential duty alongside {{ill|Kagyo|en|家業}} and learning<ref name="日本史大事典"/>.
In September 1443 ({{ill|Kakitsu 3|en|嘉吉}}), during the {{ill|Rebellion of the Emperor's Mausoleum|en|禁闕の変}}, the palace was invaded by {{ill|Southern Court loyalists|en|後南朝}}, and young court nobles such as {{ill|Kanroji Chikanaga|en|甘露寺親長}} and {{ill|Yotsutsuji Tokiharu|en|四辻季春}} defended the {{ill|Emperor Go-Hanazono|en|後花園天皇}} by drawing swords and escorting him to safety{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=13}}.
In {{ill|Bunmei 8|en|文明}} (1476), Koban members were divided into "Uchi-uchi" (close attendants) and "Tazama" (outsiders){{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=156–157}}. Over time, these designations became hereditary and reflected a form of {{ill|Kuge House Rank|en|家格}}<ref name="日本歴史大事典"/><ref name=hayashi/>.
In {{ill|Kanbun 3|en|寛文}} (1661), {{ill|Retired Emperor Go-Mizunoo|en|後水尾法皇}} created a new rotation, "Oku no Ban," composed of 21 close attendants selected from the Uchi-uchi and Tazama groups. Initially, these members served double roles, but by {{ill|Kanbun 11|en|寛文}}, 1671, they were exempt from their original duties and formed the specialized "Kinjushu Koban" (Close Attendants)<ref>Hayashi Daiki, "On the Formation of Kinjushu Koban in the Early Modern Era," Ronshu Kinsei, Vol. 40, 2018.</ref>.
In {{ill|Keio 4|en|慶応}}, 1868, the distinctions between Uchi-uchi, Tazama, and Kinjushu were abolished, and the roles were consolidated under "Naiban-shu" (Inner Attendants)<ref name="日本史大事典"/><ref name="日本歴史大事典"/>.

{{ページ番号|date=2021年8月}} '''Kinri Koban''' (禁裏小番) refers to the system, from the [[Middle Ages|middle ages]] through the [[Early modern period (Japan)|early modern period]], in which {{ill|Kuge|en|公家}} served in daily rotation ({{ill|Ban (Japanese system)|en|番}}) at the {{ill|Kinri|en|禁裏}} to attend and {{ill|Shukutoku|en|宿直}} for the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|天皇}}.
They were tasked with guarding the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|天皇}} and the {{ill|Sacred Mirror|en|神鏡}}, located at the {{ill|Naishi-dokoro|en|内侍所}} {{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=160}}.
Court nobles, excluding those from the {{ill|Sekkan family|en|摂関家}}, served in this role from the age of 15 after {{ill|Genpuku|en|元服}} until 60 (although those with busy official duties or longstanding contributions were sometimes exempted by imperial decree)<ref name="日本史大事典">Mori, "Nihonshi Daijiten."</ref><ref name="日本歴史大事典">Honda, "Nihon Rekishi Daijiten."</ref>. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|戦国時代 (日本)}}, due to manpower shortages caused by the relocation of nobles to rural areas and the extinction of some families, there were cases where those younger than 15 performed these duties. In the {{ill|Edo period|en|江戸時代}}, {{ill|Emperor Go-Mizunoo|en|後水尾天皇}} established the age of 15 as the minimum for Koban duties, and the establishment of {{ill|Shinke|en|新家}} resolved personnel shortages. Non-official roles, such as {{ill|Oko|en|御児}} for miscellaneous tasks within the palace, were also fulfilled by appointees<ref>Hayashi Daiki, "The Role of 'Gojiko' in Early Modern Court Nobility," Jinbun, Issue 16, 2018; revised version in "Tenno Kinshin to Kinsei no Chotei" (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021), ISBN 978-4-642-04333-5, pp. 93–95.</ref>. In cases where fathers and sons (the family head and successor) served concurrently, sons were granted {{ill|Horyo|en|方領}} (land as compensation)<ref name="日本歴史大事典"/>. Additionally, those serving the {{ill|Retired Emperor|en|太上天皇}} or the {{ill|Crown Prince|en|皇太子}} were known as "In-san-shu" or "Togu Koban" respectively<ref name=hayashi>Hayashi Daiki, "Introduction to Modern Court Studies and Objectives," in "Tenno Kinshin to Kinsei no Chotei" (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021), pp. 8–9.</ref>.
The system varied over time, with rotations organized into groups of five to seven (e.g., five, six, or ten groups). In response to circumstances, the overall number of groups could be reduced or temporary additions ({{ill|Kaban|en|加番}}) ordered to increase group sizes<ref name="日本史大事典"/>. Each group was led by a head ({{ill|Banto|en|番頭}}) and overseen by a {{ill|Koban Bugyo|en|小番奉行}}, often concurrently held by a {{ill|Buke Denso|en|武家伝奏}}<ref name="日本史大事典"/>.
The term "Kinri Koban" was established during the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|室町時代}} (although the system itself predates this). Economic hardships during this time led to many neglecting their duties. To address this, in {{ill|Eikyo 2|en|永享}}, 1430, {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshinori|en|足利義教}} of the {{ill|Muromachi Shogunate|en|室町幕府}} implemented reforms mandating that someone from the rotation always be on duty in the palace. Violators faced confiscation of their estates<ref name="国史大辞典">Tanuma, "Kokushi Daijiten."</ref>. The {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|en|江戸幕府}} reinforced this in the {{ill|Kinchu Narabini Kuge Shohatto|en|禁中並公家諸法度}}, recognizing Koban as an essential duty alongside {{ill|Kagyo|en|家業}} and learning<ref name="日本史大事典"/>.
In September 1443 ({{ill|Kakitsu 3|en|嘉吉}}), during the {{ill|Rebellion of the Emperor's Mausoleum|en|禁闕の変}}, the palace was invaded by {{ill|Southern Court loyalists|en|後南朝}}, and young court nobles such as {{ill|Kanroji Chikanaga|en|甘露寺親長}} and {{ill|Yotsutsuji Tokiharu|en|四辻季春}} defended the {{ill|Emperor Go-Hanazono|en|後花園天皇}} by drawing swords and escorting him to safety{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=13}}.
In {{ill|Bunmei 8|en|文明}} (1476), Koban members were divided into "Uchi-uchi" (close attendants) and "Tazama" (outsiders){{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=156–157}}. Over time, these designations became hereditary and reflected a form of {{ill|Kuge House Rank|en|家格}}<ref name="日本歴史大事典"/><ref name=hayashi/>.
In {{ill|Kanbun 3|en|寛文}} (1661), {{ill|Retired Emperor Go-Mizunoo|en|後水尾法皇}} created a new rotation, "Oku no Ban," composed of 21 close attendants selected from the Uchi-uchi and Tazama groups. Initially, these members served double roles, but by {{ill|Kanbun 11|en|寛文}}, 1671, they were exempt from their original duties and formed the specialized "Kinjushu Koban" (Close Attendants)<ref>Hayashi Daiki, "On the Formation of Kinjushu Koban in the Early Modern Era," Ronshu Kinsei, Vol. 40, 2018.</ref>.
In {{ill|Keio 4|en|慶応}}, 1868, the distinctions between Uchi-uchi, Tazama, and Kinjushu were abolished, and the roles were consolidated under "Naiban-shu" (Inner Attendants)<ref name="日本史大事典"/><ref name="日本歴史大事典"/>.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|天皇}} * {{ill|Kinri|en|禁裏}} * {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|en|三種の神器}} * {{ill|Naishi-dokoro|en|内侍所}} * {{ill|Kuge|en|公家}}
== References == <references/>
== Bibliography == * Tanuma Mutsuo, "Kinri Koban," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten (Showa)|en|国史大辞典}}, Vol. 4, Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1984, ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3 * Mori Miwa, "Kinri Koban," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten|en|日本史大事典}}, Vol. 2, Heibonsha, 1993, ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4 * Honda Keiko, "Kinri Koban," in {{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|en|日本歴史大事典}}, Vol. 1, Shogakukan, 2001, ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6 * Nakamoto Makoto, {{ill|Court Rituals and Imperial Gagaku|en|宮廷の御神楽}}, Shinten-sha, 2016 * Nakamoto Makoto, {{ill|Why Kagura Survived the Onin War|en|なぜ神楽は応仁の乱を乗り越えられたのか}}, Shinten-sha, 2021

Initially, these members served double roles, but by {{ill|Kanbun 11|en|寛文}}, 1671, they were exempt from their original duties and formed the specialized "Kinjushu Koban" (Close Attendants)<ref>Hayashi Daiki, "On the Formation of Kinjushu Koban in the Early Modern Era," Ronshu Kinsei, Vol. 40, 2018.</ref>.
In {{ill|Keio 4|en|慶応}}, 1868, the distinctions between Uchi-uchi, Tazama, and Kinjushu were abolished, and the roles were consolidated under "Naiban-shu" (Inner Attendants)<ref name="日本史大事典"/><ref name="日本歴史大事典"/>.

[[Category:Historical titles]] [[Category:Japanese nobility]] [[Category:Imperial Court of Japan]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Kinri Koban]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:Kinri Koban]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Kinri Koban]][[category:Kinri Koban]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:Kinri Koban]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Kinri Koban]][[Category:pp]][[category:Kinri Koban]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Kinri Koban]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:Kinri Koban]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Kinri Koban]][[category:Kinri Koban]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:Kinri Koban]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Kinri Koban]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:Kinri Koban]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Kinri Koban]][[category:Kinri Koban]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:Kinri Koban]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Kinri Koban]][[Category:pp]][[category:Kinri Koban]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:bump]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:Kinri Koban]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Kinri Koban]][[category:Kinri Koban]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:Kinri Koban]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Kinri Koban]][[Category:Kinri Koban]][[Category:Kinri Koban]] [[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:refreshing so the massreplace can be done again]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Kinri Koban]][[Category:kk]] [[ja:禁裏小番]]

{{ページ番号|date=2021年8月}} '''Kinri Koban''' (禁裏小番) refers to the system, from the [[Middle Ages|middle ages]] through the [[Early modern period (Japan)|early modern period]], in which {{ill|Kuge|en|公家}} served in daily rotation ({{ill|Ban (Japanese system)|en|番}}) at the {{ill|Kinri|en|禁裏}} to attend and {{ill|Shukutoku|en|宿直}} for the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|天皇}}.
They were tasked with guarding the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|天皇}} and the {{ill|Sacred Mirror|en|神鏡}}, located at the {{ill|Naishi-dokoro|en|内侍所}} {{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=160}}.
Court nobles, excluding those from the {{ill|Sekkan family|en|摂関家}}, served in this role from the age of 15 after {{ill|Genpuku|en|元服}} until 60 (although those with busy official duties or longstanding contributions were sometimes exempted by imperial decree)<ref name="日本史大事典">Mori, "Nihonshi Daijiten."</ref><ref name="日本歴史大事典">Honda, "Nihon Rekishi Daijiten."</ref>. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|戦国時代 (日本)}}, due to manpower shortages caused by the relocation of nobles to rural areas and the extinction of some families, there were cases where those younger than 15 performed these duties. In the {{ill|Edo period|en|江戸時代}}, {{ill|Emperor Go-Mizunoo|en|後水尾天皇}} established the age of 15 as the minimum for Koban duties, and the establishment of {{ill|Shinke|en|新家}} resolved personnel shortages. Non-official roles, such as {{ill|Oko|en|御児}} for miscellaneous tasks within the palace, were also fulfilled by appointees<ref>Hayashi Daiki, "The Role of 'Gojiko' in Early Modern Court Nobility," Jinbun, Issue 16, 2018; revised version in "Tenno Kinshin to Kinsei no Chotei" (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021), ISBN 978-4-642-04333-5, pp. 93–95.</ref>. In cases where fathers and sons (the family head and successor) served concurrently, sons were granted {{ill|Horyo|en|方領}} (land as compensation)<ref name="日本歴史大事典"/>. Additionally, those serving the {{ill|Retired Emperor|en|太上天皇}} or the {{ill|Crown Prince|en|皇太子}} were known as "In-san-shu" or "Togu Koban" respectively<ref name=hayashi>Hayashi Daiki, "Introduction to Modern Court Studies and Objectives," in "Tenno Kinshin to Kinsei no Chotei" (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021), pp. 8–9.</ref>.
The system varied over time, with rotations organized into groups of five to seven (e.g., five, six, or ten groups). In response to circumstances, the overall number of groups could be reduced or temporary additions ({{ill|Kaban|en|加番}}) ordered to increase group sizes<ref name="日本史大事典"/>. Each group was led by a head ({{ill|Banto|en|番頭}}) and overseen by a {{ill|Koban Bugyo|en|小番奉行}}, often concurrently held by a {{ill|Buke Denso|en|武家伝奏}}<ref name="日本史大事典"/>.
The term "Kinri Koban" was established during the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|室町時代}} (although the system itself predates this). Economic hardships during this time led to many neglecting their duties. To address this, in {{ill|Eikyo 2|en|永享}}, 1430, {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshinori|en|足利義教}} of the {{ill|Muromachi Shogunate|en|室町幕府}} implemented reforms mandating that someone from the rotation always be on duty in the palace. Violators faced confiscation of their estates<ref name="国史大辞典">Tanuma, "Kokushi Daijiten."</ref>. The {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|en|江戸幕府}} reinforced this in the {{ill|Kinchu Narabini Kuge Shohatto|en|禁中並公家諸法度}}, recognizing Koban as an essential duty alongside {{ill|Kagyo|en|家業}} and learning<ref name="日本史大事典"/>.
In September 1443 ({{ill|Kakitsu 3|en|嘉吉}}), during the {{ill|Rebellion of the Emperor's Mausoleum|en|禁闕の変}}, the palace was invaded by {{ill|Southern Court loyalists|en|後南朝}}, and young court nobles such as {{ill|Kanroji Chikanaga|en|甘露寺親長}} and {{ill|Yotsutsuji Tokiharu|en|四辻季春}} defended the {{ill|Emperor Go-Hanazono|en|後花園天皇}} by drawing swords and escorting him to safety{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=13}}.
In {{ill|Bunmei 8|en|文明}} (1476), Koban members were divided into "Uchi-uchi" (close attendants) and "Tazama" (outsiders){{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=156–157}}. Over time, these designations became hereditary and reflected a form of {{ill|Kuge House Rank|en|家格}}<ref name="日本歴史大事典"/><ref name=hayashi/>.
In {{ill|Kanbun 3|en|寛文}} (1661), {{ill|Retired Emperor Go-Mizunoo|en|後水尾法皇}} created a new rotation, "Oku no Ban," composed of 21 close attendants selected from the Uchi-uchi and Tazama groups. Initially, these members served double roles, but by {{ill|Kanbun 11|en|寛文}}, 1671, they were exempt from their original duties and formed the specialized "Kinjushu Koban" (Close Attendants)<ref>Hayashi Daiki, "On the Formation of Kinjushu Koban in the Early Modern Era," Ronshu Kinsei, Vol. 40, 2018.</ref>.
In {{ill|Keio 4|en|慶応}}, 1868, the distinctions between Uchi-uchi, Tazama, and Kinjushu were abolished, and the roles were consolidated under "Naiban-shu" (Inner Attendants)<ref name="日本史大事典"/><ref name="日本歴史大事典"/>.

revision.text:
{{ページ番号|date=2021年8月}} '''Kinri Koban''' (禁裏小番) refers to the system, from the [[Middle Ages|middle ages]] through the [[Early modern period (Japan)|early modern period]], in which {{ill|Kuge|ja|公家}} served in daily rotation ({{ill|Ban (Japanese system)|ja|番}}) at the {{ill|Kinri|ja|禁裏}} to attend and {{ill|Shukutoku|ja|宿直}} for the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}.
They were tasked with guarding the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}} and the {{ill|Sacred Mirror|ja|神鏡}}, located at the {{ill|Naishi-dokoro|ja|内侍所}} {{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=160}}.
Court nobles, excluding those from the {{ill|Sekkan family|ja|摂関家}}, served in this role from the age of 15 after {{ill|Genpuku|ja|元服}} until 60 (although those with busy official duties or longstanding contributions were sometimes exempted by imperial decree)<ref name="日本史大事典">Mori, "Nihonshi Daijiten."</ref><ref name="日本歴史大事典">Honda, "Nihon Rekishi Daijiten."</ref>. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代 (日本)}}, due to manpower shortages caused by the relocation of nobles to rural areas and the extinction of some families, there were cases where those younger than 15 performed these duties. In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, {{ill|Emperor Go-Mizunoo|ja|後水尾天皇}} established the age of 15 as the minimum for Koban duties, and the establishment of {{ill|Shinke|ja|新家}} resolved personnel shortages. Non-official roles, such as {{ill|Oko|ja|御児}} for miscellaneous tasks within the palace, were also fulfilled by appointees<ref>Hayashi Daiki, "The Role of 'Gojiko' in Early Modern Court Nobility," Jinbun, Issue 16, 2018; revised version in "Tenno Kinshin to Kinsei no Chotei" (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021), ISBN 978-4-642-04333-5, pp. 93–95.</ref>. In cases where fathers and sons (the family head and successor) served concurrently, sons were granted {{ill|Horyo|ja|方領}} (land as compensation)<ref name="日本歴史大事典"/>. Additionally, those serving the {{ill|Retired Emperor|ja|太上天皇}} or the {{ill|Crown Prince|ja|皇太子}} were known as "In-san-shu" or "Togu Koban" respectively<ref name=hayashi>Hayashi Daiki, "Introduction to Modern Court Studies and Objectives," in "Tenno Kinshin to Kinsei no Chotei" (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021), pp. 8–9.</ref>.
The system varied over time, with rotations organized into groups of five to seven (e.g., five, six, or ten groups). In response to circumstances, the overall number of groups could be reduced or temporary additions ({{ill|Kaban|ja|加番}}) ordered to increase group sizes<ref name="日本史大事典"/>. Each group was led by a head ({{ill|Banto|ja|番頭}}) and overseen by a {{ill|Koban Bugyo|ja|小番奉行}}, often concurrently held by a {{ill|Buke Denso|ja|武家伝奏}}<ref name="日本史大事典"/>.
The term "Kinri Koban" was established during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}} (although the system itself predates this). Economic hardships during this time led to many neglecting their duties. To address this, in {{ill|Eikyo 2|ja|永享}}, 1430, {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshinori|ja|足利義教}} of the {{ill|Muromachi Shogunate|ja|室町幕府}} implemented reforms mandating that someone from the rotation always be on duty in the palace. Violators faced confiscation of their estates<ref name="国史大辞典">Tanuma, "Kokushi Daijiten."</ref>. The {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}} reinforced this in the {{ill|Kinchu Narabini Kuge Shohatto|ja|禁中並公家諸法度}}, recognizing Koban as an essential duty alongside {{ill|Kagyo|ja|家業}} and learning<ref name="日本史大事典"/>.
In September 1443 ({{ill|Kakitsu 3|ja|嘉吉}}), during the {{ill|Rebellion of the Emperor's Mausoleum|ja|禁闕の変}}, the palace was invaded by {{ill|Southern Court loyalists|ja|後南朝}}, and young court nobles such as {{ill|Kanroji Chikanaga|ja|甘露寺親長}} and {{ill|Yotsutsuji Tokiharu|ja|四辻季春}} defended the {{ill|Emperor Go-Hanazono|ja|後花園天皇}} by drawing swords and escorting him to safety{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=13}}.
In {{ill|Bunmei 8|ja|文明}} (1476), Koban members were divided into "Uchi-uchi" (close attendants) and "Tazama" (outsiders){{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=156–157}}. Over time, these designations became hereditary and reflected a form of {{ill|Kuge House Rank|ja|家格}}<ref name="日本歴史大事典"/><ref name=hayashi/>.
In {{ill|Kanbun 3|ja|寛文}} (1661), {{ill|Retired Emperor Go-Mizunoo|ja|後水尾法皇}} created a new rotation, "Oku no Ban," composed of 21 close attendants selected from the Uchi-uchi and Tazama groups. Initially, these members served double roles, but by {{ill|Kanbun 11|ja|寛文}}, 1671, they were exempt from their original duties and formed the specialized "Kinjushu Koban" (Close Attendants)<ref>Hayashi Daiki, "On the Formation of Kinjushu Koban in the Early Modern Era," Ronshu Kinsei, Vol. 40, 2018.</ref>.
In {{ill|Keio 4|ja|慶応}}, 1868, the distinctions between Uchi-uchi, Tazama, and Kinjushu were abolished, and the roles were consolidated under "Naiban-shu" (Inner Attendants)<ref name="日本史大事典"/><ref name="日本歴史大事典"/>.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}} * {{ill|Kinri|ja|禁裏}} * {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|ja|三種の神器}} * {{ill|Naishi-dokoro|ja|内侍所}} * {{ill|Kuge|ja|公家}}
== References == <references/>
== Bibliography == * Tanuma Mutsuo, "Kinri Koban," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten (Showa)|ja|国史大辞典}}, Vol. 4, Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1984, ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3 * Mori Miwa, "Kinri Koban," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten|ja|日本史大事典}}, Vol. 2, Heibonsha, 1993, ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4 * Honda Keiko, "Kinri Koban," in {{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja|日本歴史大事典}}, Vol. 1, Shogakukan, 2001, ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6 * Nakamoto Makoto, {{ill|Court Rituals and Imperial Gagaku|ja|宮廷の御神楽}}, Shinten-sha, 2016 * Nakamoto Makoto, {{ill|Why Kagura Survived the Onin War|ja|なぜ神楽は応仁の乱を乗り越えられたのか}}, Shinten-sha, 2021

{{ページ番号|date=2021年8月}} '''Kinri Koban''' (禁裏小番) refers to the system, from the [[Middle Ages|middle ages]] through the [[Early modern period (Japan)|early modern period]], in which {{ill|Kuge|ja|公家}} served in daily rotation ({{ill|Ban (Japanese system)|ja|番}}) at the {{ill|Kinri|ja|禁裏}} to attend and {{ill|Shukutoku|ja|宿直}} for the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}.
They were tasked with guarding the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}} and the {{ill|Sacred Mirror|ja|神鏡}}, located at the {{ill|Naishi-dokoro|ja|内侍所}} {{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=160}}.
Court nobles, excluding those from the {{ill|Sekkan family|ja|摂関家}}, served in this role from the age of 15 after {{ill|Genpuku|ja|元服}} until 60 (although those with busy official duties or longstanding contributions were sometimes exempted by imperial decree)<ref name="日本史大事典">Mori, "Nihonshi Daijiten."</ref><ref name="日本歴史大事典">Honda, "Nihon Rekishi Daijiten."</ref>. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代 (日本)}}, due to manpower shortages caused by the relocation of nobles to rural areas and the extinction of some families, there were cases where those younger than 15 performed these duties. In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, {{ill|Emperor Go-Mizunoo|ja|後水尾天皇}} established the age of 15 as the minimum for Koban duties, and the establishment of {{ill|Shinke|ja|新家}} resolved personnel shortages. Non-official roles, such as {{ill|Oko|ja|御児}} for miscellaneous tasks within the palace, were also fulfilled by appointees<ref>Hayashi Daiki, "The Role of 'Gojiko' in Early Modern Court Nobility," Jinbun, Issue 16, 2018; revised version in "Tenno Kinshin to Kinsei no Chotei" (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021), ISBN 978-4-642-04333-5, pp. 93–95.</ref>. In cases where fathers and sons (the family head and successor) served concurrently, sons were granted {{ill|Horyo|ja|方領}} (land as compensation)<ref name="日本歴史大事典"/>. Additionally, those serving the {{ill|Retired Emperor|ja|太上天皇}} or the {{ill|Crown Prince|ja|皇太子}} were known as "In-san-shu" or "Togu Koban" respectively<ref name=hayashi>Hayashi Daiki, "Introduction to Modern Court Studies and Objectives," in "Tenno Kinshin to Kinsei no Chotei" (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021), pp. 8–9.</ref>.
The system varied over time, with rotations organized into groups of five to seven (e.g., five, six, or ten groups). In response to circumstances, the overall number of groups could be reduced or temporary additions ({{ill|Kaban|ja|加番}}) ordered to increase group sizes<ref name="日本史大事典"/>. Each group was led by a head ({{ill|Banto|ja|番頭}}) and overseen by a {{ill|Koban Bugyo|ja|小番奉行}}, often concurrently held by a {{ill|Buke Denso|ja|武家伝奏}}<ref name="日本史大事典"/>.
The term "Kinri Koban" was established during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}} (although the system itself predates this). Economic hardships during this time led to many neglecting their duties. To address this, in {{ill|Eikyo 2|ja|永享}}, 1430, {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshinori|ja|足利義教}} of the {{ill|Muromachi Shogunate|ja|室町幕府}} implemented reforms mandating that someone from the rotation always be on duty in the palace. Violators faced confiscation of their estates<ref name="国史大辞典">Tanuma, "Kokushi Daijiten."</ref>. The {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}} reinforced this in the {{ill|Kinchu Narabini Kuge Shohatto|ja|禁中並公家諸法度}}, recognizing Koban as an essential duty alongside {{ill|Kagyo|ja|家業}} and learning<ref name="日本史大事典"/>.
In September 1443 ({{ill|Kakitsu 3|ja|嘉吉}}), during the {{ill|Rebellion of the Emperor's Mausoleum|ja|禁闕の変}}, the palace was invaded by {{ill|Southern Court loyalists|ja|後南朝}}, and young court nobles such as {{ill|Kanroji Chikanaga|ja|甘露寺親長}} and {{ill|Yotsutsuji Tokiharu|ja|四辻季春}} defended the {{ill|Emperor Go-Hanazono|ja|後花園天皇}} by drawing swords and escorting him to safety{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=13}}.
In {{ill|Bunmei 8|ja|文明}} (1476), Koban members were divided into "Uchi-uchi" (close attendants) and "Tazama" (outsiders){{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=156–157}}. Over time, these designations became hereditary and reflected a form of {{ill|Kuge House Rank|ja|家格}}<ref name="日本歴史大事典"/><ref name=hayashi/>.
In {{ill|Kanbun 3|ja|寛文}} (1661), {{ill|Retired Emperor Go-Mizunoo|ja|後水尾法皇}} created a new rotation, "Oku no Ban," composed of 21 close attendants selected from the Uchi-uchi and Tazama groups. Initially, these members served double roles, but by {{ill|Kanbun 11|ja|寛文}}, 1671, they were exempt from their original duties and formed the specialized "Kinjushu Koban" (Close Attendants)<ref>Hayashi Daiki, "On the Formation of Kinjushu Koban in the Early Modern Era," Ronshu Kinsei, Vol. 40, 2018.</ref>.
In {{ill|Keio 4|ja|慶応}}, 1868, the distinctions between Uchi-uchi, Tazama, and Kinjushu were abolished, and the roles were consolidated under "Naiban-shu" (Inner Attendants)<ref name="日本史大事典"/><ref name="日本歴史大事典"/>.

{{ページ番号|date=2021年8月}} '''Kinri Koban''' (禁裏小番) refers to the system, from the [[Middle Ages|middle ages]] through the [[Early modern period (Japan)|early modern period]], in which {{ill|Kuge|ja|公家}} served in daily rotation ({{ill|Ban (Japanese system)|ja|番}}) at the {{ill|Kinri|ja|禁裏}} to attend and {{ill|Shukutoku|ja|宿直}} for the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}.
They were tasked with guarding the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}} and the {{ill|Sacred Mirror|ja|神鏡}}, located at the {{ill|Naishi-dokoro|ja|内侍所}} {{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=160}}.
Court nobles, excluding those from the {{ill|Sekkan family|ja|摂関家}}, served in this role from the age of 15 after {{ill|Genpuku|ja|元服}} until 60 (although those with busy official duties or longstanding contributions were sometimes exempted by imperial decree)<ref name="日本史大事典">Mori, "Nihonshi Daijiten."</ref><ref name="日本歴史大事典">Honda, "Nihon Rekishi Daijiten."</ref>. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代 (日本)}}, due to manpower shortages caused by the relocation of nobles to rural areas and the extinction of some families, there were cases where those younger than 15 performed these duties. In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, {{ill|Emperor Go-Mizunoo|ja|後水尾天皇}} established the age of 15 as the minimum for Koban duties, and the establishment of {{ill|Shinke|ja|新家}} resolved personnel shortages. Non-official roles, such as {{ill|Oko|ja|御児}} for miscellaneous tasks within the palace, were also fulfilled by appointees<ref>Hayashi Daiki, "The Role of 'Gojiko' in Early Modern Court Nobility," Jinbun, Issue 16, 2018; revised version in "Tenno Kinshin to Kinsei no Chotei" (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021), ISBN 978-4-642-04333-5, pp. 93–95.</ref>. In cases where fathers and sons (the family head and successor) served concurrently, sons were granted {{ill|Horyo|ja|方領}} (land as compensation)<ref name="日本歴史大事典"/>. Additionally, those serving the {{ill|Retired Emperor|ja|太上天皇}} or the {{ill|Crown Prince|ja|皇太子}} were known as "In-san-shu" or "Togu Koban" respectively<ref name=hayashi>Hayashi Daiki, "Introduction to Modern Court Studies and Objectives," in "Tenno Kinshin to Kinsei no Chotei" (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021), pp. 8–9.</ref>.
The system varied over time, with rotations organized into groups of five to seven (e.g., five, six, or ten groups). In response to circumstances, the overall number of groups could be reduced or temporary additions ({{ill|Kaban|ja|加番}}) ordered to increase group sizes<ref name="日本史大事典"/>. Each group was led by a head ({{ill|Banto|ja|番頭}}) and overseen by a {{ill|Koban Bugyo|ja|小番奉行}}, often concurrently held by a {{ill|Buke Denso|ja|武家伝奏}}<ref name="日本史大事典"/>.
The term "Kinri Koban" was established during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}} (although the system itself predates this). Economic hardships during this time led to many neglecting their duties. To address this, in {{ill|Eikyo 2|ja|永享}}, 1430, {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshinori|ja|足利義教}} of the {{ill|Muromachi Shogunate|ja|室町幕府}} implemented reforms mandating that someone from the rotation always be on duty in the palace. Violators faced confiscation of their estates<ref name="国史大辞典">Tanuma, "Kokushi Daijiten."</ref>. The {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}} reinforced this in the {{ill|Kinchu Narabini Kuge Shohatto|ja|禁中並公家諸法度}}, recognizing Koban as an essential duty alongside {{ill|Kagyo|ja|家業}} and learning<ref name="日本史大事典"/>.
In September 1443 ({{ill|Kakitsu 3|ja|嘉吉}}), during the {{ill|Rebellion of the Emperor's Mausoleum|ja|禁闕の変}}, the palace was invaded by {{ill|Southern Court loyalists|ja|後南朝}}, and young court nobles such as {{ill|Kanroji Chikanaga|ja|甘露寺親長}} and {{ill|Yotsutsuji Tokiharu|ja|四辻季春}} defended the {{ill|Emperor Go-Hanazono|ja|後花園天皇}} by drawing swords and escorting him to safety{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=13}}.
In {{ill|Bunmei 8|ja|文明}} (1476), Koban members were divided into "Uchi-uchi" (close attendants) and "Tazama" (outsiders){{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=156–157}}. Over time, these designations became hereditary and reflected a form of {{ill|Kuge House Rank|ja|家格}}<ref name="日本歴史大事典"/><ref name=hayashi/>.
In {{ill|Kanbun 3|ja|寛文}} (1661), {{ill|Retired Emperor Go-Mizunoo|ja|後水尾法皇}} created a new rotation, "Oku no Ban," composed of 21 close attendants selected from the Uchi-uchi and Tazama groups. Initially, these members served double roles, but by {{ill|Kanbun 11|ja|寛文}}, 1671, they were exempt from their original duties and formed the specialized "Kinjushu Koban" (Close Attendants)<ref>Hayashi Daiki, "On the Formation of Kinjushu Koban in the Early Modern Era," Ronshu Kinsei, Vol. 40, 2018.</ref>.
In {{ill|Keio 4|ja|慶応}}, 1868, the distinctions between Uchi-uchi, Tazama, and Kinjushu were abolished, and the roles were consolidated under "Naiban-shu" (Inner Attendants)<ref name="日本史大事典"/><ref name="日本歴史大事典"/>.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}} * {{ill|Kinri|ja|禁裏}} * {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|ja|三種の神器}} * {{ill|Naishi-dokoro|ja|内侍所}} * {{ill|Kuge|ja|公家}}

== Bibliography == * Tanuma Mutsuo, "Kinri Koban," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten (Showa)|ja|国史大辞典}}, Vol. 4, Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1984, ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3 * Mori Miwa, "Kinri Koban," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten|ja|日本史大事典}}, Vol. 2, Heibonsha, 1993, ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4 * Honda Keiko, "Kinri Koban," in {{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja|日本歴史大事典}}, Vol. 1, Shogakukan, 2001, ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6 * Nakamoto Makoto, {{ill|Court Rituals and Imperial Gagaku|ja|宮廷の御神楽}}, Shinten-sha, 2016 * Nakamoto Makoto, {{ill|Why Kagura Survived the Onin War|ja|なぜ神楽は応仁の乱を乗り越えられたのか}}, Shinten-sha, 2021

In {{ill|Keio 4|ja|慶応}}, 1868, the distinctions between Uchi-uchi, Tazama, and Kinjushu were abolished, and the roles were consolidated under "Naiban-shu" (Inner Attendants)<ref name="日本史大事典"/><ref name="日本歴史大事典"/>.

[[ja:禁裏小番]]

{{ページ番号|date=2021年8月}} '''Kinri Koban''' (禁裏小番) refers to the system, from the [[Middle Ages|middle ages]] through the [[Early modern period (Japan)|early modern period]], in which {{ill|Kuge|ja|公家}} served in daily rotation ({{ill|Ban (Japanese system)|ja|番}}) at the {{ill|Kinri|ja|禁裏}} to attend and {{ill|Shukutoku|ja|宿直}} for the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}.
They were tasked with guarding the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}} and the {{ill|Sacred Mirror|ja|神鏡}}, located at the {{ill|Naishi-dokoro|ja|内侍所}} {{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=160}}.
Court nobles, excluding those from the {{ill|Sekkan family|ja|摂関家}}, served in this role from the age of 15 after {{ill|Genpuku|ja|元服}} until 60 (although those with busy official duties or longstanding contributions were sometimes exempted by imperial decree)<ref name="日本史大事典">Mori, "Nihonshi Daijiten."</ref><ref name="日本歴史大事典">Honda, "Nihon Rekishi Daijiten."</ref>. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代 (日本)}}, due to manpower shortages caused by the relocation of nobles to rural areas and the extinction of some families, there were cases where those younger than 15 performed these duties. In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, {{ill|Emperor Go-Mizunoo|ja|後水尾天皇}} established the age of 15 as the minimum for Koban duties, and the establishment of {{ill|Shinke|ja|新家}} resolved personnel shortages. Non-official roles, such as {{ill|Oko|ja|御児}} for miscellaneous tasks within the palace, were also fulfilled by appointees<ref>Hayashi Daiki, "The Role of 'Gojiko' in Early Modern Court Nobility," Jinbun, Issue 16, 2018; revised version in "Tenno Kinshin to Kinsei no Chotei" (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021), ISBN 978-4-642-04333-5, pp. 93–95.</ref>. In cases where fathers and sons (the family head and successor) served concurrently, sons were granted {{ill|Horyo|ja|方領}} (land as compensation)<ref name="日本歴史大事典"/>. Additionally, those serving the {{ill|Retired Emperor|ja|太上天皇}} or the {{ill|Crown Prince|ja|皇太子}} were known as "In-san-shu" or "Togu Koban" respectively<ref name=hayashi>Hayashi Daiki, "Introduction to Modern Court Studies and Objectives," in "Tenno Kinshin to Kinsei no Chotei" (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021), pp. 8–9.</ref>.
The system varied over time, with rotations organized into groups of five to seven (e.g., five, six, or ten groups). In response to circumstances, the overall number of groups could be reduced or temporary additions ({{ill|Kaban|ja|加番}}) ordered to increase group sizes<ref name="日本史大事典"/>. Each group was led by a head ({{ill|Banto|ja|番頭}}) and overseen by a {{ill|Koban Bugyo|ja|小番奉行}}, often concurrently held by a {{ill|Buke Denso|ja|武家伝奏}}<ref name="日本史大事典"/>.
The term "Kinri Koban" was established during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}} (although the system itself predates this). Economic hardships during this time led to many neglecting their duties. To address this, in {{ill|Eikyo 2|ja|永享}}, 1430, {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshinori|ja|足利義教}} of the {{ill|Muromachi Shogunate|ja|室町幕府}} implemented reforms mandating that someone from the rotation always be on duty in the palace. Violators faced confiscation of their estates<ref name="国史大辞典">Tanuma, "Kokushi Daijiten."</ref>. The {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}} reinforced this in the {{ill|Kinchu Narabini Kuge Shohatto|ja|禁中並公家諸法度}}, recognizing Koban as an essential duty alongside {{ill|Kagyo|ja|家業}} and learning<ref name="日本史大事典"/>.
In September 1443 ({{ill|Kakitsu 3|ja|嘉吉}}), during the {{ill|Rebellion of the Emperor's Mausoleum|ja|禁闕の変}}, the palace was invaded by {{ill|Southern Court loyalists|ja|後南朝}}, and young court nobles such as {{ill|Kanroji Chikanaga|ja|甘露寺親長}} and {{ill|Yotsutsuji Tokiharu|ja|四辻季春}} defended the {{ill|Emperor Go-Hanazono|ja|後花園天皇}} by drawing swords and escorting him to safety{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=13}}.
In {{ill|Bunmei 8|ja|文明}} (1476), Koban members were divided into "Uchi-uchi" (close attendants) and "Tazama" (outsiders){{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=156–157}}. Over time, these designations became hereditary and reflected a form of {{ill|Kuge House Rank|ja|家格}}<ref name="日本歴史大事典"/><ref name=hayashi/>.
In {{ill|Kanbun 3|ja|寛文}} (1661), {{ill|Retired Emperor Go-Mizunoo|ja|後水尾法皇}} created a new rotation, "Oku no Ban," composed of 21 close attendants selected from the Uchi-uchi and Tazama groups. Initially, these members served double roles, but by {{ill|Kanbun 11|ja|寛文}}, 1671, they were exempt from their original duties and formed the specialized "Kinjushu Koban" (Close Attendants)<ref>Hayashi Daiki, "On the Formation of Kinjushu Koban in the Early Modern Era," Ronshu Kinsei, Vol. 40, 2018.</ref>.
In {{ill|Keio 4|ja|慶応}}, 1868, the distinctions between Uchi-uchi, Tazama, and Kinjushu were abolished, and the roles were consolidated under "Naiban-shu" (Inner Attendants)<ref name="日本史大事典"/><ref name="日本歴史大事典"/>.

The {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}} reinforced this in the {{ill|Kinchu Narabini Kuge Shohatto|ja|禁中並公家諸法度}}, recognizing Koban as an essential duty alongside {{ill|Kagyo|ja|家業}} and learning<ref name="日本史大事典"/>.
In September 1443 ({{ill|Kakitsu 3|ja|嘉吉}}), during the {{ill|Rebellion of the Emperor's Mausoleum|ja|禁闕の変}}, the palace was invaded by {{ill|Southern Court loyalists|ja|後南朝}}, and young court nobles such as {{ill|Kanroji Chikanaga|ja|甘露寺親長}} and {{ill|Yotsutsuji Tokiharu|ja|四辻季春}} defended the {{ill|Emperor Go-Hanazono|ja|後花園天皇}} by drawing swords and escorting him to safety{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=13}}.
In {{ill|Bunmei 8|ja|文明}} (1476), Koban members were divided into "Uchi-uchi" (close attendants) and "Tazama" (outsiders){{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=156–157}}. Over time, these designations became hereditary and reflected a form of {{ill|Kuge House Rank|ja|家格}}<ref name="日本歴史大事典"/><ref name=hayashi/>.
In {{ill|Kanbun 3|ja|寛文}} (1661), {{ill|Retired Emperor Go-Mizunoo|ja|後水尾法皇}} created a new rotation, "Oku no Ban," composed of 21 close attendants selected from the Uchi-uchi and Tazama groups. Initially, these members served double roles, but by {{ill|Kanbun 11|ja|寛文}}, 1671, they were exempt from their original duties and formed the specialized "Kinjushu Koban" (Close Attendants)<ref>Hayashi Daiki, "On the Formation of Kinjushu Koban in the Early Modern Era," Ronshu Kinsei, Vol. 40, 2018.</ref>.
In {{ill|Keio 4|ja|慶応}}, 1868, the distinctions between Uchi-uchi, Tazama, and Kinjushu were abolished, and the roles were consolidated under "Naiban-shu" (Inner Attendants)<ref name="日本史大事典"/><ref name="日本歴史大事典"/>.

{{ページ番号|date=2021年8月}} '''Kinri Koban''' (禁裏小番) refers to the system, from the [[Middle Ages|middle ages]] through the [[Early modern period (Japan)|early modern period]], in which {{ill|Kuge|ja|公家}} served in daily rotation ({{ill|Ban (Japanese system)|ja|番}}) at the {{ill|Kinri|ja|禁裏}} to attend and {{ill|Shukutoku|ja|宿直}} for the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}.
They were tasked with guarding the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}} and the {{ill|Sacred Mirror|ja|神鏡}}, located at the {{ill|Naishi-dokoro|ja|内侍所}} {{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=160}}.
Court nobles, excluding those from the {{ill|Sekkan family|ja|摂関家}}, served in this role from the age of 15 after {{ill|Genpuku|ja|元服}} until 60 (although those with busy official duties or longstanding contributions were sometimes exempted by imperial decree)<ref name="日本史大事典">Mori, "Nihonshi Daijiten."</ref><ref name="日本歴史大事典">Honda, "Nihon Rekishi Daijiten."</ref>. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代 (日本)}}, due to manpower shortages caused by the relocation of nobles to rural areas and the extinction of some families, there were cases where those younger than 15 performed these duties. In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, {{ill|Emperor Go-Mizunoo|ja|後水尾天皇}} established the age of 15 as the minimum for Koban duties, and the establishment of {{ill|Shinke|ja|新家}} resolved personnel shortages. Non-official roles, such as {{ill|Oko|ja|御児}} for miscellaneous tasks within the palace, were also fulfilled by appointees<ref>Hayashi Daiki, "The Role of 'Gojiko' in Early Modern Court Nobility," Jinbun, Issue 16, 2018; revised version in "Tenno Kinshin to Kinsei no Chotei" (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021), ISBN 978-4-642-04333-5, pp. 93–95.</ref>. In cases where fathers and sons (the family head and successor) served concurrently, sons were granted {{ill|Horyo|ja|方領}} (land as compensation)<ref name="日本歴史大事典"/>. Additionally, those serving the {{ill|Retired Emperor|ja|太上天皇}} or the {{ill|Crown Prince|ja|皇太子}} were known as "In-san-shu" or "Togu Koban" respectively<ref name=hayashi>Hayashi Daiki, "Introduction to Modern Court Studies and Objectives," in "Tenno Kinshin to Kinsei no Chotei" (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021), pp. 8–9.</ref>.
The system varied over time, with rotations organized into groups of five to seven (e.g., five, six, or ten groups). In response to circumstances, the overall number of groups could be reduced or temporary additions ({{ill|Kaban|ja|加番}}) ordered to increase group sizes<ref name="日本史大事典"/>. Each group was led by a head ({{ill|Banto|ja|番頭}}) and overseen by a {{ill|Koban Bugyo|ja|小番奉行}}, often concurrently held by a {{ill|Buke Denso|ja|武家伝奏}}<ref name="日本史大事典"/>.
The term "Kinri Koban" was established during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}} (although the system itself predates this). Economic hardships during this time led to many neglecting their duties. To address this, in {{ill|Eikyo 2|ja|永享}}, 1430, {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshinori|ja|足利義教}} of the {{ill|Muromachi Shogunate|ja|室町幕府}} implemented reforms mandating that someone from the rotation always be on duty in the palace. Violators faced confiscation of their estates<ref name="国史大辞典">Tanuma, "Kokushi Daijiten."</ref>. The {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}} reinforced this in the {{ill|Kinchu Narabini Kuge Shohatto|ja|禁中並公家諸法度}}, recognizing Koban as an essential duty alongside {{ill|Kagyo|ja|家業}} and learning<ref name="日本史大事典"/>.
In September 1443 ({{ill|Kakitsu 3|ja|嘉吉}}), during the {{ill|Rebellion of the Emperor's Mausoleum|ja|禁闕の変}}, the palace was invaded by {{ill|Southern Court loyalists|ja|後南朝}}, and young court nobles such as {{ill|Kanroji Chikanaga|ja|甘露寺親長}} and {{ill|Yotsutsuji Tokiharu|ja|四辻季春}} defended the {{ill|Emperor Go-Hanazono|ja|後花園天皇}} by drawing swords and escorting him to safety{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=13}}.
In {{ill|Bunmei 8|ja|文明}} (1476), Koban members were divided into "Uchi-uchi" (close attendants) and "Tazama" (outsiders){{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=156–157}}. Over time, these designations became hereditary and reflected a form of {{ill|Kuge House Rank|ja|家格}}<ref name="日本歴史大事典"/><ref name=hayashi/>.
In {{ill|Kanbun 3|ja|寛文}} (1661), {{ill|Retired Emperor Go-Mizunoo|ja|後水尾法皇}} created a new rotation, "Oku no Ban," composed of 21 close attendants selected from the Uchi-uchi and Tazama groups. Initially, these members served double roles, but by {{ill|Kanbun 11|ja|寛文}}, 1671, they were exempt from their original duties and formed the specialized "Kinjushu Koban" (Close Attendants)<ref>Hayashi Daiki, "On the Formation of Kinjushu Koban in the Early Modern Era," Ronshu Kinsei, Vol. 40, 2018.</ref>.
In {{ill|Keio 4|ja|慶応}}, 1868, the distinctions between Uchi-uchi, Tazama, and Kinjushu were abolished, and the roles were consolidated under "Naiban-shu" (Inner Attendants)<ref name="日本史大事典"/><ref name="日本歴史大事典"/>.

During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代 (日本)}}, due to manpower shortages caused by the relocation of nobles to rural areas and the extinction of some families, there were cases where those younger than 15 performed these duties. In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, {{ill|Emperor Go-Mizunoo|ja|後水尾天皇}} established the age of 15 as the minimum for Koban duties, and the establishment of {{ill|Shinke|ja|新家}} resolved personnel shortages. Non-official roles, such as {{ill|Oko|ja|御児}} for miscellaneous tasks within the palace, were also fulfilled by appointees<ref>Hayashi Daiki, "The Role of 'Gojiko' in Early Modern Court Nobility," Jinbun, Issue 16, 2018; revised version in "Tenno Kinshin to Kinsei no Chotei" (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021), ISBN 978-4-642-04333-5, pp. 93–95.</ref>. In cases where fathers and sons (the family head and successor) served concurrently, sons were granted {{ill|Horyo|ja|方領}} (land as compensation)<ref name="日本歴史大事典"/>. Additionally, those serving the {{ill|Retired Emperor|ja|太上天皇}} or the {{ill|Crown Prince|ja|皇太子}} were known as "In-san-shu" or "Togu Koban" respectively<ref name=hayashi>Hayashi Daiki, "Introduction to Modern Court Studies and Objectives," in "Tenno Kinshin to Kinsei no Chotei" (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021), pp. 8–9.</ref>.
The system varied over time, with rotations organized into groups of five to seven (e.g., five, six, or ten groups). In response to circumstances, the overall number of groups could be reduced or temporary additions ({{ill|Kaban|ja|加番}}) ordered to increase group sizes<ref name="日本史大事典"/>. Each group was led by a head ({{ill|Banto|ja|番頭}}) and overseen by a {{ill|Koban Bugyo|ja|小番奉行}}, often concurrently held by a {{ill|Buke Denso|ja|武家伝奏}}<ref name="日本史大事典"/>.
The term "Kinri Koban" was established during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}} (although the system itself predates this). Economic hardships during this time led to many neglecting their duties. To address this, in {{ill|Eikyo 2|ja|永享}}, 1430, {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshinori|ja|足利義教}} of the {{ill|Muromachi Shogunate|ja|室町幕府}} implemented reforms mandating that someone from the rotation always be on duty in the palace. Violators faced confiscation of their estates<ref name="国史大辞典">Tanuma, "Kokushi Daijiten."</ref>. The {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}} reinforced this in the {{ill|Kinchu Narabini Kuge Shohatto|ja|禁中並公家諸法度}}, recognizing Koban as an essential duty alongside {{ill|Kagyo|ja|家業}} and learning<ref name="日本史大事典"/>.
In September 1443 ({{ill|Kakitsu 3|ja|嘉吉}}), during the {{ill|Rebellion of the Emperor's Mausoleum|ja|禁闕の変}}, the palace was invaded by {{ill|Southern Court loyalists|ja|後南朝}}, and young court nobles such as {{ill|Kanroji Chikanaga|ja|甘露寺親長}} and {{ill|Yotsutsuji Tokiharu|ja|四辻季春}} defended the {{ill|Emperor Go-Hanazono|ja|後花園天皇}} by drawing swords and escorting him to safety{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=13}}.
In {{ill|Bunmei 8|ja|文明}} (1476), Koban members were divided into "Uchi-uchi" (close attendants) and "Tazama" (outsiders){{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=156–157}}. Over time, these designations became hereditary and reflected a form of {{ill|Kuge House Rank|ja|家格}}<ref name="日本歴史大事典"/><ref name=hayashi/>.
In {{ill|Kanbun 3|ja|寛文}} (1661), {{ill|Retired Emperor Go-Mizunoo|ja|後水尾法皇}} created a new rotation, "Oku no Ban," composed of 21 close attendants selected from the Uchi-uchi and Tazama groups. Initially, these members served double roles, but by {{ill|Kanbun 11|ja|寛文}}, 1671, they were exempt from their original duties and formed the specialized "Kinjushu Koban" (Close Attendants)<ref>Hayashi Daiki, "On the Formation of Kinjushu Koban in the Early Modern Era," Ronshu Kinsei, Vol. 40, 2018.</ref>.
In {{ill|Keio 4|ja|慶応}}, 1868, the distinctions between Uchi-uchi, Tazama, and Kinjushu were abolished, and the roles were consolidated under "Naiban-shu" (Inner Attendants)<ref name="日本史大事典"/><ref name="日本歴史大事典"/>.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}} * {{ill|Kinri|ja|禁裏}} * {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|ja|三種の神器}} * {{ill|Naishi-dokoro|ja|内侍所}} * {{ill|Kuge|ja|公家}}
== References == <references/>
== Bibliography == * Tanuma Mutsuo, "Kinri Koban," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten (Showa)|ja|国史大辞典}}, Vol. 4, Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1984, ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3 * Mori Miwa, "Kinri Koban," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten|ja|日本史大事典}}, Vol. 2, Heibonsha, 1993, ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4 * Honda Keiko, "Kinri Koban," in {{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja|日本歴史大事典}}, Vol. 1, Shogakukan, 2001, ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6 * Nakamoto Makoto, {{ill|Court Rituals and Imperial Gagaku|ja|宮廷の御神楽}}, Shinten-sha, 2016 * Nakamoto Makoto, {{ill|Why Kagura Survived the Onin War|ja|なぜ神楽は応仁の乱を乗り越えられたのか}}, Shinten-sha, 2021
