<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:禁裏小番]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:禁裏小番]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:禁裏小番]]
{{ページ番号|date=2021st year 8月}} '''Kinri Koban''' (禁裏小番) refers to the system, from the {{ill|Middle Ages|en|Middle Ages|WD=Q12554|lt=middle ages|1=Middle Ages}} through the [[Early modern period (Japan)|early modern period]], in which {{ill|Kuge|en|Kuge|WD=Q1196276|lt=Kuge|1=Kuge}} served in daily rotation ({{ill|Ban (Japanese system)|ja|番|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ban (Japanese system)|1=rotation|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11578730}}) at the {{ill|Kinri|ja|禁裏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinri|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 禁中|1=Imperial Palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11591117}} to attend and {{ill|Shukutoku|ja|宿直|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shukutoku|1=tonoi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11455649}} for the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor of Japan|1=Emperor of Japan}}.
They were tasked with guarding the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor of Japan|1=Emperor of Japan}} and the {{ill|Mirrors in Shinto|en|Mirrors in Shinto|WD=Q11591009|lt=Sacred Mirror|1=Mirrors in Shinto}}, located at the {{ill|Naishi-dokoro|ja|内侍所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naishi-dokoro|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 賢所|1=Kashiko-dokoro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11635085}} {{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=160}}.
Court nobles, excluding those from the {{ill|Five regent houses|en|Five regent houses|WD=Q1067142|lt=Five regent houses|1=Five regent houses}}, served in this role from the age of 15 after {{ill|Genpuku|ja|元服|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Genpuku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Genpuku|WD=Q138321}} until 60 (although those with busy official duties or longstanding contributions were sometimes exempted by imperial decree)<ref name="日本史大事典">Mori, "Nihonshi Daijiten."</ref><ref name="日本歴史大事典">Honda, "Nihon Rekishi Daijiten."</ref>. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}}, due to manpower shortages caused by the relocation of nobles to rural areas and the extinction of some families, there were cases where those younger than 15 performed these duties. In the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, {{ill|Emperor Go-Mizunoo|en|Emperor Go-Mizunoo|WD=Q320229|lt=Emperor Go-Mizunoo|1=Emperor Go-Mizunoo}} established the age of 15 as the minimum for Koban duties, and the establishment of {{ill|Shinke|ja|新家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinke|1=shinke|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11501762}} resolved personnel shortages. Non-official roles, such as {{ill|Oko|ja|御児|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oko|qq=}} for miscellaneous tasks within the palace, were also fulfilled by a11ointees<ref>Hayashi Daiki, "The Role of 'Gojiko' in Early Modern Court Nobility," Jinbun, Issue 16, 2018; revised version in "Tenno Kinshin to Kinsei no Chotei" (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021), ISBN 978-4-642-04333-5, 11. 93–95.</ref>. In cases where fathers and sons (the family head and successor) served concurrently, sons were granted {{ill|Horyo|ja|方領|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Horyo|1=kataryou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11504385}} (land as compensation)<ref name="日本歴史大事典"/>. Additionally, those serving the {{ill|Daijō Tennō|en|Daijō Tennō|WD=Q14303973|lt=Retired Emperor|1=Daijō Tennō}} or the {{ill|Crown prince|en|Crown prince|WD=Q207293|lt=Crown Prince|1=Crown prince}} were known as "In-san-shu" or "Togu Koban" respectively<ref name=hayashi>Hayashi Daiki, "Introduction to Modern Court Studies and Objectives," in "Tenno Kinshin to Kinsei no Chotei" (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021), 11. 8–9.</ref>.
The system varied over time, with rotations organized into groups of five to seven (e.g., five, six, or ten groups). In response to circumstances, the overall number of groups could be reduced or temporary additions ({{ill|Kaban|ja|加番|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaban|1=kaban|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11398727}}) ordered to increase group sizes<ref name="日本史大事典"/>. Each group was led by a head ({{ill|Banto|ja|番頭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Banto|1=bantou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11578792}}) and overseen by a {{ill|Koban Bugyo|ja|小番奉行|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koban Bugyo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, often concurrently held by a {{ill|Buke Denso|ja|武家伝奏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buke Denso|1=Samurai Envoy|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11124096}}<ref name="日本史大事典"/>.
The term "Kinri Koban" was established during the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}} (although the system itself predates this). Economic hardships during this time led to many neglecting their duties. To address this, in {{ill|Eikyō|en|Eikyō|WD=Q1193059|lt=Eikyo 2|1=Eikyō}}, 1430, {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshinori|en|Ashikaga Yoshinori|WD=Q239170|lt=Ashikaga Yoshinori|1=Ashikaga Yoshinori}} of the {{ill|Muromachi Shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|1=Ashikaga shogunate|2=ja|3=室町幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi Shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1154406}} implemented reforms mandating that someone from the rotation always be on duty in the palace. Violators faced confiscation of their estates<ref name="国史大辞典">Tanuma, "Kokushi Daijiten."</ref>. The {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|lt=Tokugawa shogunate|lt=Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|lt=Tokugawa shogunate|lt=Tokugawa Shogunate|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} reinforced this in the {{ill|Kinchu narabini kuge shohatto|en|Kinchu narabini kuge shohatto|WD=Q5371034|lt=Kinchu Narabini Kuge Shohatto|1=Kinchu narabini kuge shohatto}}, recognizing Koban as an essential duty alongside {{ill|Kagyo|ja|家業|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagyo|1=Family business|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17239757}} and learning<ref name="日本史大事典"/>.
In September 1443 ({{ill|Kakitsu|en|Kakitsu|WD=Q1193238|lt=Kakitsu 3|1=Kakitsu}}), during the {{ill|Rebellion of the Emperor's Mausoleum|ja|禁闕の変|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rebellion of the Emperor's Mausoleum|1=Kinketsu incident|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11591164}}, the palace was invaded by {{ill|Southern Court loyalists|ja|後南朝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Southern Court loyalists|1=Later Southern Court|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488318}}, and young court nobles such as {{ill|Kanroji Chikanaga|ja|甘露寺親長|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanroji Chikanaga|1=Chikanaga Kanroji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11574316}} and {{ill|Yotsutsuji Tokiharu|ja|四辻季春|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yotsutsuji Tokiharu|1=Yotsutsuji Sueharu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q131226080}} defended the {{ill|Emperor Go-Hanazono|en|Emperor Go-Hanazono|WD=Q451926|lt=Emperor Go-Hanazono|1=Emperor Go-Hanazono}} by drawing swords and escorting him to safety{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=13}}.
In {{ill|Civilization|en|Civilization|WD=Q8432|lt=Bunmei 8|1=Civilization}} (1476), Koban members were divided into "Uchi-uchi" (close attendants) and "Tazama" (outsiders){{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=156–157}}. Over time, these designations became hereditary and reflected a form of {{ill|Kuge House Rank|ja|家格|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuge House Rank|1=Kakaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11455533}}<ref name="日本歴史大事典"/><ref name=hayashi/>.
In {{ill|Kanbun (era)|en|Kanbun (era)|WD=Q1146228|lt=Kanbun 3|1=Kanbun (era)}} (1661), {{ill|Retired Emperor Go-Mizunoo|ja|後水尾法皇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Retired Emperor Go-Mizunoo|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 後水尾天皇|1=Emperor Go-Mizunoo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q320229}} created a new rotation, "Oku no Ban," composed of 21 close attendants selected from the Uchi-uchi and Tazama groups. Initially, these members served double roles, but by {{ill|Kanbun (era)|en|Kanbun (era)|WD=Q1146228|lt=Kanbun 11|1=Kanbun (era)}}, 1671, they were exempt from their original duties and formed the specialized "Kinjushu Koban" (Close Attendants)<ref>Hayashi Daiki, "On the Formation of Kinjushu Koban in the Early Modern Era," Ronshu Kinsei, Vol. 40, 2018.</ref>.
In {{ill|Keiō|en|Keiō|WD=Q776775|lt=Keio 4|1=Keiō}}, 1868, the distinctions between Uchi-uchi, Tazama, and Kinjushu were abolished, and the roles were consolidated under "Naiban-shu" (Inner Attendants)<ref name="日本史大事典"/><ref name="日本歴史大事典"/>.
== See Also == * {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor of Japan|1=Emperor of Japan}} * {{ill|Kinri|ja|禁裏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinri|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 禁中|1=Imperial Palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11591117}} * {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|en|Imperial Regalia of Japan|WD=Q327465|lt=Three Sacred Treasures|1=Imperial Regalia of Japan}} * {{ill|Naishi-dokoro|ja|内侍所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naishi-dokoro|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 賢所|1=Kashiko-dokoro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11635085}} * {{ill|Kuge|en|Kuge|WD=Q1196276|lt=Kuge|1=Kuge}}
== References == <references/>
== Bibliography == * Tanuma Mutsuo, "Kinri Koban," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten (Showa)|ja|国史大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Daijiten (Showa)|1=National History Encyclopedia|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6426904}}, Vol. 4, Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1984, ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3 * Mori Miwa, "Kinri Koban," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten|ja|日本史大事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihonshi Daijiten|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Vol. 2, Heibonsha, 1993, ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4 * Honda Keiko, "Kinri Koban," in {{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja|日本歴史大事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135524488|1=Nihon Rekishi Daijiten}}, Vol. 1, Shogakukan, 2001, ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6 * Nakamoto Makoto, {{ill|Court Rituals and Imperial Gagaku|ja|宮廷の御神楽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Court Rituals and Imperial Gagaku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Shinten-sha, 2016 * Nakamoto Makoto, {{ill|Why Kagura Survived the Onin War|ja|なぜ神楽は応仁の乱を乗り越えられたのか|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Why Kagura Survived the Onin War|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Shinten-sha, 2021

In the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, {{ill|Emperor Go-Mizunoo|en|Emperor Go-Mizunoo|WD=Q320229|lt=Emperor Go-Mizunoo|1=Emperor Go-Mizunoo}} established the age of 15 as the minimum for Koban duties, and the establishment of {{ill|Shinke|ja|新家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinke|1=shinke|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11501762}} resolved personnel shortages. In cases where fathers and sons (the family head and successor) served concurrently, sons were granted {{ill|Horyo|ja|方領|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Horyo|1=kataryou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11504385}} (land as compensation)<ref name="日本歴史大事典"/>. Additionally, those serving the {{ill|Daijō Tennō|en|Daijō Tennō|WD=Q14303973|lt=Retired Emperor|1=Daijō Tennō}} or the {{ill|Crown prince|en|Crown prince|WD=Q207293|lt=Crown Prince|1=Crown prince}} were known as "In-san-shu" or "Togu Koban" respectively<ref name=hayashi>Hayashi Daiki, "Introduction to Modern Court Studies and Objectives," in "Tenno Kinshin to Kinsei no Chotei" (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021), 11. 8–9.</ref>.
The system varied over time, with rotations organized into groups of five to seven (e.g., five, six, or ten groups). In response to circumstances, the overall number of groups could be reduced or temporary additions ({{ill|Kaban|ja|加番|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaban|1=kaban|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11398727}}) ordered to increase group sizes<ref name="日本史大事典"/>. Each group was led by a head ({{ill|Banto|ja|番頭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Banto|1=bantou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11578792}}) and overseen by a {{ill|Koban Bugyo|ja|小番奉行|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koban Bugyo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, often concurrently held by a {{ill|Buke Denso|ja|武家伝奏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buke Denso|1=Samurai Envoy|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11124096}}<ref name="日本史大事典"/>.
The term "Kinri Koban" was established during the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}} (although the system itself predates this). Economic hardships during this time led to many neglecting their duties. To address this, in {{ill|Eikyō|en|Eikyō|WD=Q1193059|lt=Eikyo 2|1=Eikyō}}, 1430, {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshinori|en|Ashikaga Yoshinori|WD=Q239170|lt=Ashikaga Yoshinori|1=Ashikaga Yoshinori}} of the {{ill|Muromachi Shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|1=Ashikaga shogunate|2=ja|3=室町幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi Shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1154406}} implemented reforms mandating that someone from the rotation always be on duty in the palace. Violators faced confiscation of their estates<ref name="国史大辞典">Tanuma, "Kokushi Daijiten."</ref>. The {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|lt=Tokugawa shogunate|lt=Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|lt=Tokugawa shogunate|lt=Tokugawa Shogunate|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} reinforced this in the {{ill|Kinchu narabini kuge shohatto|en|Kinchu narabini kuge shohatto|WD=Q5371034|lt=Kinchu Narabini Kuge Shohatto|1=Kinchu narabini kuge shohatto}}, recognizing Koban as an essential duty alongside {{ill|Kagyo|ja|家業|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagyo|1=Family business|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17239757}} and learning<ref name="日本史大事典"/>.
In September 1443 ({{ill|Kakitsu|en|Kakitsu|WD=Q1193238|lt=Kakitsu 3|1=Kakitsu}}), during the {{ill|Rebellion of the Emperor's Mausoleum|ja|禁闕の変|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rebellion of the Emperor's Mausoleum|1=Kinketsu incident|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11591164}}, the palace was invaded by {{ill|Southern Court loyalists|ja|後南朝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Southern Court loyalists|1=Later Southern Court|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488318}}, and young court nobles such as {{ill|Kanroji Chikanaga|ja|甘露寺親長|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanroji Chikanaga|1=Chikanaga Kanroji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11574316}} and {{ill|Yotsutsuji Tokiharu|ja|四辻季春|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yotsutsuji Tokiharu|1=Yotsutsuji Sueharu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q131226080}} defended the {{ill|Emperor Go-Hanazono|en|Emperor Go-Hanazono|WD=Q451926|lt=Emperor Go-Hanazono|1=Emperor Go-Hanazono}} by drawing swords and escorting him to safety{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=13}}.
In {{ill|Civilization|en|Civilization|WD=Q8432|lt=Bunmei 8|1=Civilization}} (1476), Koban members were divided into "Uchi-uchi" (close attendants) and "Tazama" (outsiders){{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=156–157}}. Over time, these designations became hereditary and reflected a form of {{ill|Kuge House Rank|ja|家格|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuge House Rank|1=Kakaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11455533}}<ref name="日本歴史大事典"/><ref name=hayashi/>.
In {{ill|Kanbun (era)|en|Kanbun (era)|WD=Q1146228|lt=Kanbun 3|1=Kanbun (era)}} (1661), {{ill|Retired Emperor Go-Mizunoo|ja|後水尾法皇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Retired Emperor Go-Mizunoo|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 後水尾天皇|1=Emperor Go-Mizunoo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q320229}} created a new rotation, "Oku no Ban," composed of 21 close attendants selected from the Uchi-uchi and Tazama groups. Initially, these members served double roles, but by {{ill|Kanbun (era)|en|Kanbun (era)|WD=Q1146228|lt=Kanbun 11|1=Kanbun (era)}}, 1671, they were exempt from their original duties and formed the specialized "Kinjushu Koban" (Close Attendants)<ref>Hayashi Daiki, "On the Formation of Kinjushu Koban in the Early Modern Era," Ronshu Kinsei, Vol. 40, 2018.</ref>.
In {{ill|Keiō|en|Keiō|WD=Q776775|lt=Keio 4|1=Keiō}}, 1868, the distinctions between Uchi-uchi, Tazama, and Kinjushu were abolished, and the roles were consolidated under "Naiban-shu" (Inner Attendants)<ref name="日本史大事典"/><ref name="日本歴史大事典"/>.

[[en:Kinri Koban]] {{wikidata link|Q136731942}} [[Category:Extraordinary Government Officials of Ancient Japan]] [[Category:Japanese nobility]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:pages]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:禁裏小番]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:禁裏小番]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:禁裏小番]]
{{ページ番号|date=2021st year 8月}} '''Kinri Koban''' (禁裏小番) refers to the system, from the {{ill|Middle Ages|en|Middle Ages|WD=Q12554|lt=middle ages|1=Middle Ages}} through the [[Early modern period (Japan)|early modern period]], in which {{ill|Kuge|en|Kuge|WD=Q1196276|lt=Kuge|1=Kuge}} served in daily rotation ({{ill|Ban (Japanese system)|ja|番|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ban (Japanese system)|1=rotation|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11578730}}) at the {{ill|Kinri|ja|禁裏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinri|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 禁中|1=Imperial Palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11591117}} to attend and {{ill|Shukutoku|ja|宿直|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shukutoku|1=tonoi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11455649}} for the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor of Japan|1=Emperor of Japan}}.
They were tasked with guarding the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor of Japan|1=Emperor of Japan}} and the {{ill|Mirrors in Shinto|en|Mirrors in Shinto|WD=Q11591009|lt=Sacred Mirror|1=Mirrors in Shinto}}, located at the {{ill|Naishi-dokoro|ja|内侍所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naishi-dokoro|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 賢所|1=Kashiko-dokoro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11635085}} {{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=160}}.
Court nobles, excluding those from the {{ill|Five regent houses|en|Five regent houses|WD=Q1067142|lt=Five regent houses|1=Five regent houses}}, served in this role from the age of 15 after {{ill|Genpuku|ja|元服|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Genpuku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Genpuku|WD=Q138321}} until 60 (although those with busy official duties or longstanding contributions were sometimes exempted by imperial decree)<ref name="日本史大事典">Mori, "Nihonshi Daijiten."</ref><ref name="日本歴史大事典">Honda, "Nihon Rekishi Daijiten."</ref>. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}}, due to manpower shortages caused by the relocation of nobles to rural areas and the extinction of some families, there were cases where those younger than 15 performed these duties. In the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, {{ill|Emperor Go-Mizunoo|en|Emperor Go-Mizunoo|WD=Q320229|lt=Emperor Go-Mizunoo|1=Emperor Go-Mizunoo}} established the age of 15 as the minimum for Koban duties, and the establishment of {{ill|Shinke|ja|新家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinke|1=shinke|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11501762}} resolved personnel shortages. Non-official roles, such as {{ill|Oko|ja|御児|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oko|qq=}} for miscellaneous tasks within the palace, were also fulfilled by a11ointees<ref>Hayashi Daiki, "The Role of 'Gojiko' in Early Modern Court Nobility," Jinbun, Issue 16, 2018; revised version in "Tenno Kinshin to Kinsei no Chotei" (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021), ISBN 978-4-642-04333-5, 11. 93–95.</ref>. In cases where fathers and sons (the family head and successor) served concurrently, sons were granted {{ill|Horyo|ja|方領|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Horyo|1=kataryou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11504385}} (land as compensation)<ref name="日本歴史大事典"/>. Additionally, those serving the {{ill|Daijō Tennō|en|Daijō Tennō|WD=Q14303973|lt=Retired Emperor|1=Daijō Tennō}} or the {{ill|Crown prince|en|Crown prince|WD=Q207293|lt=Crown Prince|1=Crown prince}} were known as "In-san-shu" or "Togu Koban" respectively<ref name=hayashi>Hayashi Daiki, "Introduction to Modern Court Studies and Objectives," in "Tenno Kinshin to Kinsei no Chotei" (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021), 11. 8–9.</ref>.
The system varied over time, with rotations organized into groups of five to seven (e.g., five, six, or ten groups). In response to circumstances, the overall number of groups could be reduced or temporary additions ({{ill|Kaban|ja|加番|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaban|1=kaban|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11398727}}) ordered to increase group sizes<ref name="日本史大事典"/>. Each group was led by a head ({{ill|Banto|ja|番頭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Banto|1=bantou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11578792}}) and overseen by a {{ill|Koban Bugyo|ja|小番奉行|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koban Bugyo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, often concurrently held by a {{ill|Buke Denso|ja|武家伝奏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buke Denso|1=Samurai Envoy|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11124096}}<ref name="日本史大事典"/>.
The term "Kinri Koban" was established during the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}} (although the system itself predates this). Economic hardships during this time led to many neglecting their duties. To address this, in {{ill|Eikyō|en|Eikyō|WD=Q1193059|lt=Eikyo 2|1=Eikyō}}, 1430, {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshinori|en|Ashikaga Yoshinori|WD=Q239170|lt=Ashikaga Yoshinori|1=Ashikaga Yoshinori}} of the {{ill|Muromachi Shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|1=Ashikaga shogunate|2=ja|3=室町幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi Shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1154406}} implemented reforms mandating that someone from the rotation always be on duty in the palace. Violators faced confiscation of their estates<ref name="国史大辞典">Tanuma, "Kokushi Daijiten."</ref>. The {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|lt=Tokugawa shogunate|lt=Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|lt=Tokugawa shogunate|lt=Tokugawa Shogunate|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} reinforced this in the {{ill|Kinchu narabini kuge shohatto|en|Kinchu narabini kuge shohatto|WD=Q5371034|lt=Kinchu Narabini Kuge Shohatto|1=Kinchu narabini kuge shohatto}}, recognizing Koban as an essential duty alongside {{ill|Kagyo|ja|家業|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagyo|1=Family business|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17239757}} and learning<ref name="日本史大事典"/>.
In September 1443 ({{ill|Kakitsu|en|Kakitsu|WD=Q1193238|lt=Kakitsu 3|1=Kakitsu}}), during the {{ill|Rebellion of the Emperor's Mausoleum|ja|禁闕の変|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rebellion of the Emperor's Mausoleum|1=Kinketsu incident|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11591164}}, the palace was invaded by {{ill|Southern Court loyalists|ja|後南朝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Southern Court loyalists|1=Later Southern Court|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488318}}, and young court nobles such as {{ill|Kanroji Chikanaga|ja|甘露寺親長|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanroji Chikanaga|1=Chikanaga Kanroji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11574316}} and {{ill|Yotsutsuji Tokiharu|ja|四辻季春|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yotsutsuji Tokiharu|1=Yotsutsuji Sueharu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q131226080}} defended the {{ill|Emperor Go-Hanazono|en|Emperor Go-Hanazono|WD=Q451926|lt=Emperor Go-Hanazono|1=Emperor Go-Hanazono}} by drawing swords and escorting him to safety{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=13}}.
In {{ill|Civilization|en|Civilization|WD=Q8432|lt=Bunmei 8|1=Civilization}} (1476), Koban members were divided into "Uchi-uchi" (close attendants) and "Tazama" (outsiders){{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=156–157}}. Over time, these designations became hereditary and reflected a form of {{ill|Kuge House Rank|ja|家格|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuge House Rank|1=Kakaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11455533}}<ref name="日本歴史大事典"/><ref name=hayashi/>.
In {{ill|Kanbun (era)|en|Kanbun (era)|WD=Q1146228|lt=Kanbun 3|1=Kanbun (era)}} (1661), {{ill|Retired Emperor Go-Mizunoo|ja|後水尾法皇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Retired Emperor Go-Mizunoo|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 後水尾天皇|1=Emperor Go-Mizunoo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q320229}} created a new rotation, "Oku no Ban," composed of 21 close attendants selected from the Uchi-uchi and Tazama groups. Initially, these members served double roles, but by {{ill|Kanbun (era)|en|Kanbun (era)|WD=Q1146228|lt=Kanbun 11|1=Kanbun (era)}}, 1671, they were exempt from their original duties and formed the specialized "Kinjushu Koban" (Close Attendants)<ref>Hayashi Daiki, "On the Formation of Kinjushu Koban in the Early Modern Era," Ronshu Kinsei, Vol. 40, 2018.</ref>.
In {{ill|Keiō|en|Keiō|WD=Q776775|lt=Keio 4|1=Keiō}}, 1868, the distinctions between Uchi-uchi, Tazama, and Kinjushu were abolished, and the roles were consolidated under "Naiban-shu" (Inner Attendants)<ref name="日本史大事典"/><ref name="日本歴史大事典"/>.

In the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, {{ill|Emperor Go-Mizunoo|en|Emperor Go-Mizunoo|qid=Q320229|lt=Emperor Go-Mizunoo|1=Emperor Go-Mizunoo}} established the age of 15 as the minimum for Koban duties, and the establishment of {{ill|Shinke|ja|新家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinke|1=shinke|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11501762}} resolved personnel shortages. Non-official roles, such as {{ill|Oko|ja|御児|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oko|qq=}} for miscellaneous tasks within the palace, were also fulfilled by a11ointees<ref>Hayashi Daiki, "The Role of 'Gojiko' in Early Modern Court Nobility," Jinbun, Issue 16, 2018; revised version in "Tenno Kinshin to Kinsei no Chotei" (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021), ISBN 978-4-642-04333-5, 11. 93–95.</ref>. In cases where fathers and sons (the family head and successor) served concurrently, sons were granted {{ill|Horyo|ja|方領|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Horyo|1=kataryou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11504385}} (land as compensation)<ref name="日本歴史大事典"/>. Additionally, those serving the {{ill|Daijō Tennō|en|Daijō Tennō|qid=Q14303973|lt=Retired Emperor|1=Daijō Tennō}} or the {{ill|Crown prince|en|Crown prince|qid=Q207293|lt=Crown Prince|1=Crown prince}} were known as "In-san-shu" or "Togu Koban" respectively<ref name=hayashi>Hayashi Daiki, "Introduction to Modern Court Studies and Objectives," in "Tenno Kinshin to Kinsei no Chotei" (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021), 11. 8–9.</ref>.
The system varied over time, with rotations organized into groups of five to seven (e.g., five, six, or ten groups). In response to circumstances, the overall number of groups could be reduced or temporary additions ({{ill|Kaban|ja|加番|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaban|1=kaban|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11398727}}) ordered to increase group sizes<ref name="日本史大事典"/>. Each group was led by a head ({{ill|Banto|ja|番頭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Banto|1=bantou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11578792}}) and overseen by a {{ill|Koban Bugyo|ja|小番奉行|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koban Bugyo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, often concurrently held by a {{ill|Buke Denso|ja|武家伝奏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buke Denso|1=Samurai Envoy|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11124096}}<ref name="日本史大事典"/>.
The term "Kinri Koban" was established during the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|qid=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}} (although the system itself predates this). Economic hardships during this time led to many neglecting their duties. To address this, in {{ill|Eikyō|en|Eikyō|qid=Q1193059|lt=Eikyo 2|1=Eikyō}}, 1430, {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshinori|en|Ashikaga Yoshinori|qid=Q239170|lt=Ashikaga Yoshinori|1=Ashikaga Yoshinori}} of the {{ill|Muromachi Shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|1=Ashikaga shogunate|2=ja|3=室町幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi Shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1154406}} implemented reforms mandating that someone from the rotation always be on duty in the palace. Violators faced confiscation of their estates<ref name="国史大辞典">Tanuma, "Kokushi Daijiten."</ref>. The {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|lt=Tokugawa shogunate|lt=Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|lt=Tokugawa shogunate|lt=Tokugawa Shogunate|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} reinforced this in the {{ill|Kinchu narabini kuge shohatto|en|Kinchu narabini kuge shohatto|qid=Q5371034|lt=Kinchu Narabini Kuge Shohatto|1=Kinchu narabini kuge shohatto}}, recognizing Koban as an essential duty alongside {{ill|Kagyo|ja|家業|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagyo|1=Family business|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q17239757}} and learning<ref name="日本史大事典"/>.
In September 1443 ({{ill|Kakitsu|en|Kakitsu|qid=Q1193238|lt=Kakitsu 3|1=Kakitsu}}), during the {{ill|Rebellion of the Emperor's Mausoleum|ja|禁闕の変|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rebellion of the Emperor's Mausoleum|1=Kinketsu incident|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11591164}}, the palace was invaded by {{ill|Southern Court loyalists|ja|後南朝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Southern Court loyalists|1=Later Southern Court|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11488318}}, and young court nobles such as {{ill|Kanroji Chikanaga|ja|甘露寺親長|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanroji Chikanaga|1=Chikanaga Kanroji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11574316}} and {{ill|Yotsutsuji Tokiharu|ja|四辻季春|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yotsutsuji Tokiharu|1=Yotsutsuji Sueharu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q131226080}} defended the {{ill|Emperor Go-Hanazono|en|Emperor Go-Hanazono|qid=Q451926|lt=Emperor Go-Hanazono|1=Emperor Go-Hanazono}} by drawing swords and escorting him to safety{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=13}}.
In {{ill|Civilization|en|Civilization|qid=Q8432|lt=Bunmei 8|1=Civilization}} (1476), Koban members were divided into "Uchi-uchi" (close attendants) and "Tazama" (outsiders){{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=156–157}}. Over time, these designations became hereditary and reflected a form of {{ill|Kuge House Rank|ja|家格|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuge House Rank|1=Kakaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11455533}}<ref name="日本歴史大事典"/><ref name=hayashi/>.
In {{ill|Kanbun (era)|en|Kanbun (era)|qid=Q1146228|lt=Kanbun 3|1=Kanbun (era)}} (1661), {{ill|Retired Emperor Go-Mizunoo|ja|後水尾法皇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Retired Emperor Go-Mizunoo|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 後水尾天皇|1=Emperor Go-Mizunoo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q320229}} created a new rotation, "Oku no Ban," composed of 21 close attendants selected from the Uchi-uchi and Tazama groups. Initially, these members served double roles, but by {{ill|Kanbun (era)|en|Kanbun (era)|qid=Q1146228|lt=Kanbun 11|1=Kanbun (era)}}, 1671, they were exempt from their original duties and formed the specialized "Kinjushu Koban" (Close Attendants)<ref>Hayashi Daiki, "On the Formation of Kinjushu Koban in the Early Modern Era," Ronshu Kinsei, Vol. 40, 2018.</ref>.
In {{ill|Keiō|en|Keiō|qid=Q776775|lt=Keio 4|1=Keiō}}, 1868, the distinctions between Uchi-uchi, Tazama, and Kinjushu were abolished, and the roles were consolidated under "Naiban-shu" (Inner Attendants)<ref name="日本史大事典"/><ref name="日本歴史大事典"/>.
== See Also == * {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|qid=Q208233|lt=Emperor of Japan|1=Emperor of Japan}} * {{ill|Kinri|ja|禁裏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinri|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 禁中|1=Imperial Palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11591117}} * {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|en|Imperial Regalia of Japan|qid=Q327465|lt=Three Sacred Treasures|1=Imperial Regalia of Japan}} * {{ill|Naishi-dokoro|ja|内侍所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naishi-dokoro|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 賢所|1=Kashiko-dokoro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11635085}} * {{ill|Kuge|en|Kuge|qid=Q1196276|lt=Kuge|1=Kuge}}
== References == <references/>
== Bibliography == * Tanuma Mutsuo, "Kinri Koban," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten (Showa)|ja|国史大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Daijiten (Showa)|1=National History Encyclopedia|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q6426904}}, Vol. 4, Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1984, ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3 * Mori Miwa, "Kinri Koban," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten|ja|日本史大事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihonshi Daijiten|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Vol. 2, Heibonsha, 1993, ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4 * Honda Keiko, "Kinri Koban," in {{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja|日本歴史大事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135524488|1=Nihon Rekishi Daijiten}}, Vol. 1, Shogakukan, 2001, ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6 * Nakamoto Makoto, {{ill|Court Rituals and Imperial Gagaku|ja|宮廷の御神楽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Court Rituals and Imperial Gagaku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Shinten-sha, 2016 * Nakamoto Makoto, {{ill|Why Kagura Survived the Onin War|ja|なぜ神楽は応仁の乱を乗り越えられたのか|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Why Kagura Survived the Onin War|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Shinten-sha, 2021

revision.text:
{{ページ番号|date=2021st year 8月}} '''Kinri Koban''' (禁裏小番) refers to the system, from the {{ill|Middle Ages|en|Middle Ages|qid=Q12554|lt=middle ages|1=Middle Ages}} through the [[Early modern period (Japan)|early modern period]], in which {{ill|Kuge|en|Kuge|qid=Q1196276|lt=Kuge|1=Kuge}} served in daily rotation ({{ill|Ban (Japanese system)|ja|番|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ban (Japanese system)|1=rotation|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11578730}}) at the {{ill|Kinri|ja|禁裏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinri|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 禁中|1=Imperial Palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11591117}} to attend and {{ill|Shukutoku|ja|宿直|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shukutoku|1=tonoi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11455649}} for the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|qid=Q208233|lt=Emperor of Japan|1=Emperor of Japan}}.
They were tasked with guarding the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|qid=Q208233|lt=Emperor of Japan|1=Emperor of Japan}} and the {{ill|Mirrors in Shinto|en|Mirrors in Shinto|qid=Q11591009|lt=Sacred Mirror|1=Mirrors in Shinto}}, located at the {{ill|Naishi-dokoro|ja|内侍所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naishi-dokoro|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 賢所|1=Kashiko-dokoro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11635085}} {{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=160}}.
Court nobles, excluding those from the {{ill|Five regent houses|en|Five regent houses|qid=Q1067142|lt=Five regent houses|1=Five regent houses}}, served in this role from the age of 15 after {{ill|Genpuku|ja|元服|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Genpuku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Genpuku|qid=Q138321}} until 60 (although those with busy official duties or longstanding contributions were sometimes exempted by imperial decree)<ref name="日本史大事典">Mori, "Nihonshi Daijiten."</ref><ref name="日本歴史大事典">Honda, "Nihon Rekishi Daijiten."</ref>. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|qid=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}}, due to manpower shortages caused by the relocation of nobles to rural areas and the extinction of some families, there were cases where those younger than 15 performed these duties. In the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, {{ill|Emperor Go-Mizunoo|en|Emperor Go-Mizunoo|qid=Q320229|lt=Emperor Go-Mizunoo|1=Emperor Go-Mizunoo}} established the age of 15 as the minimum for Koban duties, and the establishment of {{ill|Shinke|ja|新家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinke|1=shinke|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11501762}} resolved personnel shortages. Non-official roles, such as {{ill|Oko|ja|御児|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oko|qq=}} for miscellaneous tasks within the palace, were also fulfilled by a11ointees<ref>Hayashi Daiki, "The Role of 'Gojiko' in Early Modern Court Nobility," Jinbun, Issue 16, 2018; revised version in "Tenno Kinshin to Kinsei no Chotei" (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021), ISBN 978-4-642-04333-5, 11. 93–95.</ref>. In cases where fathers and sons (the family head and successor) served concurrently, sons were granted {{ill|Horyo|ja|方領|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Horyo|1=kataryou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11504385}} (land as compensation)<ref name="日本歴史大事典"/>. Additionally, those serving the {{ill|Daijō Tennō|en|Daijō Tennō|qid=Q14303973|lt=Retired Emperor|1=Daijō Tennō}} or the {{ill|Crown prince|en|Crown prince|qid=Q207293|lt=Crown Prince|1=Crown prince}} were known as "In-san-shu" or "Togu Koban" respectively<ref name=hayashi>Hayashi Daiki, "Introduction to Modern Court Studies and Objectives," in "Tenno Kinshin to Kinsei no Chotei" (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2021), 11. 8–9.</ref>.
The system varied over time, with rotations organized into groups of five to seven (e.g., five, six, or ten groups). In response to circumstances, the overall number of groups could be reduced or temporary additions ({{ill|Kaban|ja|加番|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaban|1=kaban|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11398727}}) ordered to increase group sizes<ref name="日本史大事典"/>. Each group was led by a head ({{ill|Banto|ja|番頭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Banto|1=bantou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11578792}}) and overseen by a {{ill|Koban Bugyo|ja|小番奉行|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koban Bugyo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, often concurrently held by a {{ill|Buke Denso|ja|武家伝奏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buke Denso|1=Samurai Envoy|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11124096}}<ref name="日本史大事典"/>.
The term "Kinri Koban" was established during the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|qid=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}} (although the system itself predates this). Economic hardships during this time led to many neglecting their duties. To address this, in {{ill|Eikyō|en|Eikyō|qid=Q1193059|lt=Eikyo 2|1=Eikyō}}, 1430, {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshinori|en|Ashikaga Yoshinori|qid=Q239170|lt=Ashikaga Yoshinori|1=Ashikaga Yoshinori}} of the {{ill|Muromachi Shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|1=Ashikaga shogunate|2=ja|3=室町幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi Shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1154406}} implemented reforms mandating that someone from the rotation always be on duty in the palace. Violators faced confiscation of their estates<ref name="国史大辞典">Tanuma, "Kokushi Daijiten."</ref>. The {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|lt=Tokugawa shogunate|lt=Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|lt=Tokugawa shogunate|lt=Tokugawa Shogunate|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} reinforced this in the {{ill|Kinchu narabini kuge shohatto|en|Kinchu narabini kuge shohatto|qid=Q5371034|lt=Kinchu Narabini Kuge Shohatto|1=Kinchu narabini kuge shohatto}}, recognizing Koban as an essential duty alongside {{ill|Kagyo|ja|家業|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagyo|1=Family business|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q17239757}} and learning<ref name="日本史大事典"/>.
In September 1443 ({{ill|Kakitsu|en|Kakitsu|qid=Q1193238|lt=Kakitsu 3|1=Kakitsu}}), during the {{ill|Rebellion of the Emperor's Mausoleum|ja|禁闕の変|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rebellion of the Emperor's Mausoleum|1=Kinketsu incident|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11591164}}, the palace was invaded by {{ill|Southern Court loyalists|ja|後南朝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Southern Court loyalists|1=Later Southern Court|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11488318}}, and young court nobles such as {{ill|Kanroji Chikanaga|ja|甘露寺親長|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanroji Chikanaga|1=Chikanaga Kanroji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11574316}} and {{ill|Yotsutsuji Tokiharu|ja|四辻季春|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yotsutsuji Tokiharu|1=Yotsutsuji Sueharu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q131226080}} defended the {{ill|Emperor Go-Hanazono|en|Emperor Go-Hanazono|qid=Q451926|lt=Emperor Go-Hanazono|1=Emperor Go-Hanazono}} by drawing swords and escorting him to safety{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=13}}.
In {{ill|Civilization|en|Civilization|qid=Q8432|lt=Bunmei 8|1=Civilization}} (1476), Koban members were divided into "Uchi-uchi" (close attendants) and "Tazama" (outsiders){{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=156–157}}. Over time, these designations became hereditary and reflected a form of {{ill|Kuge House Rank|ja|家格|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuge House Rank|1=Kakaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11455533}}<ref name="日本歴史大事典"/><ref name=hayashi/>.
In {{ill|Kanbun (era)|en|Kanbun (era)|qid=Q1146228|lt=Kanbun 3|1=Kanbun (era)}} (1661), {{ill|Retired Emperor Go-Mizunoo|ja|後水尾法皇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Retired Emperor Go-Mizunoo|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 後水尾天皇|1=Emperor Go-Mizunoo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q320229}} created a new rotation, "Oku no Ban," composed of 21 close attendants selected from the Uchi-uchi and Tazama groups. Initially, these members served double roles, but by {{ill|Kanbun (era)|en|Kanbun (era)|qid=Q1146228|lt=Kanbun 11|1=Kanbun (era)}}, 1671, they were exempt from their original duties and formed the specialized "Kinjushu Koban" (Close Attendants)<ref>Hayashi Daiki, "On the Formation of Kinjushu Koban in the Early Modern Era," Ronshu Kinsei, Vol. 40, 2018.</ref>.
In {{ill|Keiō|en|Keiō|qid=Q776775|lt=Keio 4|1=Keiō}}, 1868, the distinctions between Uchi-uchi, Tazama, and Kinjushu were abolished, and the roles were consolidated under "Naiban-shu" (Inner Attendants)<ref name="日本史大事典"/><ref name="日本歴史大事典"/>.
